This is the first detection of thermal X-ray radiation on kiloparsec scales from an extragalactic radio source. Two other hotspots located 47 north-northeast and 26 southwest from the core were also detected.

The thermal X-ray emission from the first hotspot is offset from the radio emission but is coincident with optical filaments detected with broadband filters of HST /WFPC2. The best explanation for the X-ray, radio, and optical emission is that of a `jet-cloud' interaction.

sources.summary: The paper describes PKS 2152-699 as a well-studied bright southern FR II radio galaxy with a measured redshift and a bright knot near the host galaxy.

quotes.quote: PKS 2152-699: PKS 2152-699 is a well-studied FR II radio galaxy at a redshift of z = 0.0283, corresponding to a luminosity distance of 122 Mpc Ly2005 . This radio galaxy is one of the brightest sources in the southern sky at 2.7 GHz Ly2005 . Worrall2012 found a bright knot D about 10 from the host galaxy and estimate a total time-averaged jet power 4 10^ 44 erg \ s^ 1 . The radio and optical data for this knot are taken from Fosbury1998 and Worrall2012 .

sources.summary: PKS 2153-69 is discussed as a radio-jet gas-cloud interaction case where lobe-blown cavities shape surface brightness but larger IGM morphology remains measurable.

quotes.quote: PKS 2153-69 ( z=0.028 ) : This data has been published before with regard to interaction of the radio jet with a cloud of gas ly05,young05 . The gross morphology agrees well with the C02 geometric relation, although the surface brightness decrement to the north and south of the galaxy is due to the cavities blown out by the radio lobes, as is evident from the visible shocked bubbles of gas in the intragroup medium. Despite these cavities, the morphology of the larger IGM is easily measurable on larger scales, assuming the internal structure is entirely due to the radio galaxy activity.

gmw_00001825.name: G4Jy 1504
gmw_00001825.overview: G4Jy 1504 is an early-type radio galaxy that lacks strong optical emission lines [arXiv:2308.10071, arXiv:1008.2683]. It is interacting with two nearby companion galaxies and features a secondary nucleus, a bright arc-like shell, and soft X-ray emission [arXiv:2308.10071, arXiv:1008.2683].

sources.summary: G4Jy 1504 is treated as a WAT or head-tailed radio galaxy with an early-type host, companion interactions, a secondary nucleus, shell structure, and X-ray/ray emission.

quotes.quote: G4Jy 1504: is classified as a WAT (a.k.a. PKS 1839-48) given the radio structure shown in Morganti et al. (1993) and Mingo et al. (2017) but classified, more recently, as a head-tailed radio galaxy by White et al. (2020b). The host galaxy identification adopted here is the same found in the literature and corresponding to an early-type galaxy at z =0.1108 simpson93,jones09,white20b lacking strong optical emission lines tadhunter93 . G4Jy 1504 is interacting with the two nearby companion galaxies, and it shows an apparent secondary nucleus located at 6 kpc in the south-eastern direction ramos11 . It also shows bright arc-like shell of 7 kpc in size, potentially due to gravitational lensing, in the north-western direction ramos11,ramos13 . The ICM emission around G4Jy 1504 is clearly detected in the soft X-rays mingo17 and the radio source is also associated with a Fermi ray object abdollahi20 .

sources.summary: The paper describes PKS 1839-48 as a powerful radio source whose GMOS-S morphology shows disturbed structure, companions, shells, fans, dust, tails, bridges, or multiple nuclei indicating interaction or merger activity.

quotes.quote: This WLRG/FRI galaxy is the brightest member of a group or cluster, with its spectrum presenting several absorption lines typical of early-type galaxies, but no strong emission lines Tadhunter93 . Our GMOS-S image (Figure pks1839_online ) shows an apparent secondary nucleus within a 5.6 kpc radius to the SE, a bright arc-like shell 7 kpc to the NW, and a tentative narrow and sharp tail NE. It is also possible that the radio galaxy is interacting with the two galaxies aligned along the SW direction, since at least one them appears clearly disturbed. Emission line contamination can be ruled out, since strong emission lines do not fall in the filter used.

gmw_00001826.name: G4Jy 1613
gmw_00001826.overview: G4Jy 1613 is a restarted giant radio galaxy [arXiv:0811.1907, arXiv:2308.10071] classified as an FR-I type [arXiv:0811.1907, arXiv:2003.09183] with a double-boomerang, X-shaped morphology [arXiv:2308.10071, arXiv:2401.05595]. It features a double-double radio structure with an inner lobe pair aligned with its outer lobes [arXiv:0811.1907, arXiv:2003.09183], alongside dust lanes that suggest a merger history [arXiv:2403.00580].

sources.summary: The paper uses AIRI-ASKAP imaging of PKS 2014-55, finding smoother diffuse X-shaped radio emission, lower compact-lobe resolution, measured sub-band flux densities, and frequency-dependent flux differences from uSARA.

quotes.quote: The second selected field covers the merging galaxy cluster SPT-CL J2023-5535 (hereafter SPT2023) and the X-shaped radio galaxy PKS 2014-55. As stated in Part I, two recent studies have been separately published for these sources of interest: 2020ApJ...900..127H confirmed the detection of a radio halo and radio relic in SPT2023 with the same data used in this work and 2020MNRAS.495.1271C used MeerKAT observations to generate total intensity, polarisation, B-field maps, and spectral index maps of the X-shaped radio galaxy.

Compared to uSARA, the X-shape radio galaxy as reconstructed by AIRI appears to have a greater extent of diffuse emission, though with a noticeable loss in the resolution of the compact structure within the lobes. This behaviour is consistent across all the sub-band images of AIRI, where smoother and more diffuse edges are observed. Table  tab:flux-xshape reports the measured flux densities per spectral window for the X-shaped radio galaxy. AIRI flux measurements are consistently lower than the uSARA flux measurements for SPW:1 through SPW:5, then increase for SPW:6 and SPW:7, and drop lower again in spectral window 8.

sources.summary: The paper describes B2014 558 as an unusual FR-I X-shaped giant radio galaxy with classic X-structure, double-double morphology, and a small inner double aligned with the outer lobes.

quotes.quote: B2014 558: A study of the radio properties of this unusual FR-I X-shaped radio galaxy has been presented by sar08 . To our knowledge, it is the first clearly FR-I type X-shaped radio galaxy to be recognized as such and studied in detail. The source is atypical in more than one aspect. It is the only known giant radio source with the classic X-structure. As discovered by sar08 , B2014 558 is also a double-double radio source and has an inner double at the centre, aligned with the 1.5-Mpc outer lobes, and nearly two orders of magnitude smaller in linear extent.

sources.summary: PKS 2014--55 is treated as an atypical X-shaped giant radio galaxy with FR-I type, double-double inner lobes embedded within older Mpc-scale emission, and halo-backflow interpretation.

quotes.quote: PKS 2014--55: this source is atypical in many ways. It is a clear example of an X-shaped radio galaxy, the first to be found in an FR I type source, but also the only one associated to a GRG. We report here the ATCA map at 1.4 GHz from , showing a double-double radio structure with an inner couple of lobes (see Fig. maps2 , middle-left panel and relative zoom), aligned with the 1.5 Mpc outer lobes and nearly two orders of magnitude smaller in linear extent. This second more recent phase of activity is thus embedded in the Mpc-scale (and thus older) emission region. Those authors suggested that the X-shaped morphology of the more extended structure could be due to interaction of a gas backflow with the host galaxy halo.

sources.summary: G4Jy 1613 is presented as an X-shaped restarted giant radio galaxy with emission lines, hard X-ray emission, jet reorientation signatures, and hot-halo backflows.

quotes.quote: G4Jy 1613: is the X-shaped radio source a.k.a. PKS 2013-557 with a z =0.060629 with an optical spectrum showing the following emission lines: [ OII ] 3727, [ Ne III ] 3868, [ OIII ] 4959,5007 tritton72,jones09 . It is a restarted giant radio galaxy malarecki15,kuzmicz18 , belonging to the sample selected as hard X-ray emitters cusumano10,oh18,ursini18,bruni19,bruni20 , and its X-shaped structure could be a signature of jet reorientation saripalli07 . Recent MeerKAT spectro-polarimetric observations revealed a double boomerang' radio structure at 1.28 GHz mainly due to hydrodynamical backflows from the straight radio jets deflected by the large and oblique hot-gas halo of its host galaxy cotton20 . The radio source is also embedded in relatively faint cocoons with uniform brightness temperature and sharp edges due to subsonic expansion into the ambient intragroup medium cotton20 . The source is also listed in the MS4 and in the CRATES catalogs see respectively burgess06a,healey07 .

sources.summary: The review presents PKS 2014-55 as a classic XRG with restarted central AGN activity, prominent dust lanes suggesting merger history, and smooth magnetic transitions implying connected lobe and wing structures.

quotes.quote: A limited number of XRGs exhibit passive evolution in both the wing and the active lobe, suggesting that the phase of jet activity responsible for producing the structure has ceased Saripalli2009 . However, a notable fraction of such sources demonstrate a restart of jetted activity near the center, as observed in 5 out of 8 discovered sources by Saripalli2009 . Classic examples of sources with this morphological feature include PKS 2014-55 Cotton2020 and CGCG 292-057 Misra2023 . The renewed AGN activity for PKS2014-55 can be observed near the centre in Fig. Fig:PKS2014-55 , although not very well resolved. Interestingly, in all these cases, the propagating fresh jet pair aligns along the active jet of the earlier episode, suggesting a scenario where jet reorientation to another direction, at least for XRGs, is not common.
The presence of prominent dust lanes identified as a possible merger signature, see Giri2023A has also been observed in a few XRGs, including 3C 433 Miller2009 and PKS 2014-55 Cotton2020 .

In numerous XRGs, the smooth transition of magnetic field lines into wing-lobe structures suggests that the two pairs of lobes are likely interconnected entities, implying a shared evolutionary process rather than distinct and independent developments e.g., 3C 315, 3C 223.1, 3C 34, 3C 136.1, 3C 403, PKS 2014-55 Hogbom1974,Black1992,Johnson1995,Rottmann2001,Dennett-Thorpe2002,Cotton2020 .

sources.summary: The paper treats PKS 2014-55 as a giant double-boomerang X-shaped radio galaxy with spectral flattening near a lobe backflow rebound, possibly sharing 3C 223.1-like reacceleration.

quotes.quote: Recently, Gopal-Krishna Dabhade (2023) have underscored the ``double boomerang'' type radio morphology of this XRG 3C 223.1, similar to that found by Cotton et al. \ (2020) in their spectacular MeerKAT maps of the giant XRG PKS 2014-55 .

As supporting evidences, they cite the detection of spectral flattening near the bouncing point of (i) the back-flowing synchrotron plasma of the northern primary lobe of the XRG PKS 2014-55 (see Fig. \ 5 in Cotton et al. \ 2020), and (ii) in the northern jet of the Wide-Angle-Tail (WAT) radio galaxy MRC 0600 399 (termed ``double-scythe"), as seen in its MeerKAT map at 1.28 GHz (Fig. \ 1 of Chibueze et al. \ 2021).
A similar process may be operating in both known ``double boomerang'' type XRGs, PKS 2014 55 and 3C 223.1, as proposed in Gopal-Krishna Dabhade (2022).

gmw_00001827.name: G4Jy 48
gmw_00001827.overview: G4Jy 48 is a young radio galaxy hosted by an early-type galaxy [arXiv:2308.10071] that exhibits a type-2-QSO-like luminosity [arXiv:2308.10071] and a high star formation rate [arXiv:2308.10071]. It features symmetric radio lobes [arXiv:2308.10071], warm ionized outflows [arXiv:2009.11175], and a cocoon-driven molecular gas bubble [arXiv:2201.04157].

sources.summary: The paper presents new ACS/WFC imaging of PKS 0023--26 as one of three sources used to determine warm ionized outflow extents from emission-line images.

quotes.quote: HST observations In order to determine the extents of their warm outflows, three of the sources in our sample --- PKS 0023--26, PKS 1306--09, and PKS 1549--79 --- were observed using the High Resolution Camera (HRC) or Wide Field Camera (WFC) of the Advanced Camera for Surveys (ACS) mounted on the HST. The reduction and analysis of the ACS/HRC \ imaging observations for PKS 1549--79 is presented in Batcheldor2007 , and further analysis and discussion on the relationship between the \ and radio structures in this source is presented in Oosterloo2019 . Here we present new ACS/WFC observations for PKS 0023--26 and PKS 1306--09, which were taken under HST programme GO12579 (PI J. Holt). The new HST observations are detailed in Table hst . They were taken using the ramp filters in ACS/WFC, with narrow-band filters ( 140 ) centred on the wavelengths of the redshifted 5007 \ features, and medium-band filters ( 580 ) centred on adjacent continuum regions, in order to facilitate continuum subtraction. Observations in each filter comprised four separate exposures taken in a box dither pattern.

In each of the three objects with HST imaging, we estimate the outflow radius as the distance between the continuum nucleus of the galaxy and the position of the maximum in the flux of the off-nuclear emission in the continuum-subtracted \ images. These HST estimates for the warm outflow radii are within a factor of two of the radio source radii (see Table Table_radius ).

sources.summary: G4Jy 48 is described as a type-2-QSO-like young radio source in an early-type host, with symmetric lobes, X-ray jet contribution, molecular gas, and high star formation.

quotes.quote: G4Jy 48: is associated with PKS 0023-26, having both an [ OIII ] and a bolometric luminosity typical of type 2 QSOs dicken09a , with a first redshift estimate of z =0.32162 holt08 more recently refined at z =0.32188 santoro20 . It is also a young radio source, showing two relatively symmetric lobes tzioumis02,morganti21a , hosted by an early-type galaxy ramos11 . G4Jy 48 was also observed during the X-ray survey of the 2 Jy sample see wall85 , showing an X-ray spectrum with a dominant jet component and a low intrinsic absorption mingo14 . Morganti et al. (2021a) recently observed this radio source using ALMA and discovered a very extended distribution of molecular gas revealing that, already on galaxy scales, the impact of the AGN is not limited to outflows. Given its high star formation rate, G4Jy 48 lies in the region occupied by the star forming galaxies on the on the SFR-M _* plane see e.g., bernhard21 .

sources.summary: The paper describes PKS 0023-26 as a powerful radio source whose GMOS-S morphology shows disturbed structure, companions, shells, fans, dust, tails, bridges, or multiple nuclei indicating interaction or merger activity.

quotes.quote: This NLRG/CSS source appears to lie within a dense cluster environment. Indeed, the two galaxies to either side (at 25 kpc SW and 33 kpc NE) have similar redshifts to the radio galaxy host Tadhunter10 . In our GMOS-S image (Figure pks0023_online ), all three galaxies appear embedded in a common envelope, which has amorphous appearance close to the radio galaxy, and is possibly crossed by dust lanes. The presence of dust here is consistent with the detection of a YSP in the optical spectrum of the radio galaxy Holt07 , and the FIR excess revealed by Spitzer Dicken09 .

sources.summary: The paper describes PKS 0023--26 as the sample's most extended powerful radio source, with only mild sub-kpc outflow and a cocoon-driven molecular bubble on kpc scales.

quotes.quote: Interestingly, in the most extended ( 4 kpc) and powerful radio source of the sample (PKS 0023--26, which also hosts a quasar AGN), only a mild outflow is observed limited to the sub-kpc region. Instead, on kpc scales, the molecular gas is distributed predominantly around the radio lobes forming a bubble-like structure (see Fig. \ fig3 and Morganti21 ). This bubble is likely driven by the expansion of the cocoon created by the jet-ISM interaction, pushing aside the preexisting molecular gas and resulting in dispersing and heating the molecular clouds. This is more similar to the ``maintenance'' phase, preventing the gas from cooling, known to happen on even larger scales.

gmw_00001828.name: G4Jy 1829
gmw_00001828.overview: G4Jy 1829 is a giant elliptical brightest cluster galaxy [arXiv:1606.02304, arXiv:2403.03974] hosting a radio-loud, LINER-like active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1002.3297]. Its central supermassive black hole drives a galaxy-spanning fountain [arXiv:1808.00473], launching symmetric jets and inflating buoyant cavities [arXiv:2308.10071, arXiv:2403.03974] that interact with a massive, star-forming molecular nebula [arXiv:1606.02304, arXiv:2403.03974].

sources.summary: The paper describes A2597 as a BCG with weaker MIR and CO emission than A1068, a powerful radio source, and multiple detected FIR cooling lines.

quotes.quote: On the other hand, A2597 is a relatively weak MIR source (Donahue et al. 2007) with a weak CO detection (Salom e, priv. comm.) but a powerful radio source (Sarazin et al. 1995). The implied FIR luminosity of A2597 of 8.8 10^ 9 L _ is a factor of around 30 below that of A1068 (3.5 10^ 11 L _ ) and, in addition, the fractional contribution from an AGN in the MIR is also lower. We have observed the [ C ii ] and [ O i ] lines at 157.74 m and 63.18 m for A1068 and A2597 with the PACS spectrometer on Herschel . These are the primary cooling lines of the cold gas at a temperature T < 40 K (Kaufman et al. 1999). In addition for A2597 we observed the [ N ii ] , [ O i ] and [ O iii ] lines at 121.90 m, 145.52 m and 88.36 m.

The much weaker [ N ii ] 122 m and [ O i b ] 145 m lines are detected at the 3--5 level for A2597. The [ O iii ] 88 m line was not detected in A2597, an upper limit for this line is given in Table 2. The line spectra are fitted by a model consisting of; (i) a linear function to determine the continuum flux, and (ii) a single gaussian function to determine the line flux. Continuum subtracted line spectra are shown for the central spaxel in Fig. 1 for A1068 and Fig. 2 for A2597. The fitted line centers agree well with the redshift of CO in the BCG and the fitted FWHM line widths indicate gas with velocities of 300--500 km s ^ 1 . The [ C ii ] and [ N ii ] lines in the central spaxel of both objects are well described by a single gaussian. However, the [ O i ] 63 m lines, where the PACS spectral resolution is best, have profiles indicative of weak (2--3 ) deviations from a single gaussian function. The [ O i ] line in A1068 hints at a two-component structure in the form of a narrow core component on top of a broad underlying component comparable to the CO(2-1) profile in Edge (2001). Both [ O i ] lines observed in A2597 appear to have their dominant flux component at the systemic redshift of the BCG and a weaker component offset by about +250 km s ^ 1 which is also seen in the CO data (Salom e, priv. comm).

sources.summary: The paper describes A2597 as a relatively weak-MIR BCG, much fainter in FIR than A1068, marginal in the longest SPIRE band, and having a modest inferred SFR.

quotes.quote: On the other hand, Abell 2597 is a relatively weak MIR source (Donahue et al. 2007) with a weak CO detection (Salome, priv. comm.) and a powerful central radio source (Sarazin et al. 1995). The implied FIR luminosity of A2597 is a factor of around 30 below that of A1068 and, in addition, the fractional contribution from an AGN in the MIR is also lower in A2597.

A2597 and Zw3146, while clearly detected in PSW and PMW bands, have a 1--2 detection in the PLW band. Table 1 gives the photometric results for the three galaxies, with 2 upper-limit for A2597 and Zw3146 in PLW. Figure 1 presents the radio to optical spectral energy distributions (SEDs) for the three targets. These plots show the significant variation in the relative radio-FIR-optical contributions for each of our galaxies.
For A2597 a much more modest SFR of 2 M _ yr ^ 1 is inferred.

sources.summary: The paper identifies PMN J2323-1207/PKS B2322-12 as the central cD radio source in Abell 2597, describing its steep spectrum, compact double-lobed morphology, and prior outburst cavities.

quotes.quote: The central cD galaxy contains the radio source PMN J2323-1207 (PKS B2322-12) wrig90,grif94 . The total flux density of this source is 1.7 Jy at 1.5 GHz mcna01,owen92 , and 0.3 Jy at 5.0 GHz ball93 , implying a fairly steep spectral index = -1.3 ( S ^ ). The source is physically small, 7 5, 5 2 arcsec at 1.5, 5.0 GHz respectively. The source is only moderately resolved at these frequencies, but at the higher frequency has an elongated appearance suggesting a double-lobed structure. At 8.44 GHz sara95 , the source is resolved into a nucleus and two lobes ( 5 arcsec across), orientated roughly NE--SW.

A short ( 20 ks of good time) observation mcna01 highlighted the interaction between the radio source and the X-ray gas, through the presence of so-called `ghost cavities' of low surface brightness, seemingly coincident with spurs of low frequency ( 1.4 GHz ) radio emission. Such brightness depressions are believed to be buoyantly rising bubbles e.g. birz04 associated with a prior ( 10^ 8 yr previously, in this case) outburst of the central radio source.

sources.summary: The paper maps Abell 2597 BCG cold and warm gas, finding a three-billion-solar-mass nebula whose clouds feed, leave, and return from the black-hole-driven fountain.
sources.paper_coordinate_text: Right Ascension (R.A.) 23 ^ h 25 ^ m 19.7 ^ s and Declination 12 \ 07 \ 27 \ (J2000)

quotes.quote: ZuHone Harvard-Smithsonian Center for Astrophysics, 60 Garden St., Cambridge, MA 02138, USA We present ALMA and MUSE observations of the Brightest Cluster Galaxy in Abell 2597, a nearby ( z=0.0821 ) cool core cluster of galaxies. The data map the kinematics of a three billion solar mass filamentary nebula that spans the innermost 30 kpc of the galaxy's core. Its warm ionized and cold molecular components are both cospatial and comoving, consistent with the hypothesis that the optical nebula traces the warm envelopes of many cold molecular clouds that drift in the velocity field of the hot X-ray atmosphere. The clouds are not in dynamical equilibrium, and instead show evidence for inflow toward the central supermassive black hole, outflow along the jets it launches, and uplift by the buoyant hot bubbles those jets inflate.

The entire scenario is therefore consistent with a galaxy-spanning ``fountain'', wherein cold gas clouds drain into the black hole accretion reservoir, powering jets and bubbles that uplift a cooling plume of low-entropy multiphase gas, which may stimulate additional cooling and accretion as part of a self-regulating feedback loop. All velocities are below the escape speed from the galaxy, and so these clouds should rain back toward the galaxy center from which they came, keeping the fountain long-lived.

sources.summary: Abell 2597 is described as having a broad molecular gas concentration and diffuse emission, with absorption probably tracing clouds distinct from the emission.

quotes.quote: Abell 2597 has an approximately 10 kpc wide concentration of molecular gas centred on its radio core, with a velocity range of 200 km/s. More diffuse emission extends 30 kpc out to the north and south, taking the total molecular mass to 4.6 0.5 10^ 9 M _ find a mass of 3.2 0.1 10^ 9 M _ . From the extraction region they show, this mass likely includes less of the extended emission Tremblay2018 .

Molecular emission with velocities matched to the absorption lies outside the radio core, at radii between 300 pc and 3 kpc. The column density of molecular gas towards the continuum is estimated at 130 M _ /pc ^ 2 , so on average only a small number of clouds would be expected to cover the continuum. This is in stark contrast to the absorption's actual column density of 6800 M _ /pc ^ 2 , suggesting it is not caused by the same population of clouds responsible for the emission.

sources.summary: The paper reports Spitzer photometry of the Abell 2597 BCG, finding far-infrared excess, a 4-5 solar-mass-per-year star formation rate, LIRG-like luminosity, and an old stellar population.

quotes.quote: We observed the brightest central galaxy (BCG) in the nearby ( z=0.0821 ) cool core galaxy cluster Abell 2597 with the IRAC and MIPS instruments on board the Spitzer Space Telescope. The BCG was clearly detected in all Spitzer bandpasses, including the 70 and 160 m wavebands. We report aperture photometry of the BCG. The spectral energy distribution exhibits a clear excess in the FIR over a Rayleigh-Jeans stellar tail, indicating a star formation rate of 4-5 solar masses per year, consistent with the estimates from the UV and its H luminosity. This large FIR luminosity is consistent with that of a starburst or a Luminous Infrared Galaxy (LIRG), but together with a very massive and old population of stars that dominate the energy output of the galaxy.

sources.summary: PKS 2322--123 is used as a fuelling example where H I and molecular gas reveal redshifted broad and narrow components, including gas close to the nucleus.

quotes.quote: A similar situation is seen in PKS 2322--123, where identifying the infalling gas could be done in even more detail by the combination of \ and of molecular gas Tremblay16 . In this object, both a narrow (110 \ FWHM) and a very broad (735 \ FWHM) line are seen redshifted ( 220 100 ) with respect to the systemic velocity. Thanks to VLBI observations, the location of the components could be determined with the very broad line seen only against the core while the narrow is found about 20 pc from the core ODea94,Taylor99 . These changes on such a small scale suggest that we are looking at gas very close to the nucleus and that the kinematics of the gas can be explained as the result of an atomic torus centred on the nucleus with considerable turbulence and inward streaming motions suggesting infalling gas. This has been nicely confirmed by the ALMA observations of the same object showing absorption due to molecular clouds with similar properties (width and velocities) to the one of the \ (see Tremblay16 ).

sources.summary: G4Jy 1829 is presented as a young radio source in Abell 2597 whose jets, lobes, ionized gas, molecular gas, and cavities trace feedback and cold accretion.

quotes.quote: G4Jy 1829: is a young radio source with molecular gas ``hugging" its radio lobes morganti21a . It lies at z =0.082991 schmidt65,costero77,owen95,jones09,fernandez16 in the central region of the massive cooling flow galaxy cluster Abell 2597 abell58,abell89,crawford89 . At radio frequencies it shows straight and symmetric jets emerging from both sides, and an inverted spectrum of its radio core taylor99 , all confined by the ambient X-ray gas having the southern lobe deflected from its original south-western direction to the south pollack05 . Both ionized gas and cold molecular gas components wrap around the radio jet and the X-ray cavities tremblay16 , and the cospatial and comoving warm ionized and cold molecular components are consistent with a scenario of chaotic cold accretion, precipitation, and stimulated feedback tremblay18 . A ghost cavity at 330 MHz was also detected kokotanekov17 .

sources.summary: The paper describes Abell 2597 as a cool-core BCG with jet-inflated cavities, star formation fueled by cooling gas, large molecular mass, extended filaments, and redshifted absorption.

quotes.quote: Abell 2597 Galaxy and environment The brightest cluster galaxy of Abell 2597 is a giant elliptical at the centre of a cool core. It is hosted by dense halo of hot and bright X-ray emitting plasma of megaparsec scales, within which are buoyantly rising cavities inflated by the radio jets and lobes of the central galaxy McNamara2001 . The gas cooling towards the central galaxy is fueling active star formation at a rate of 5 M _ / year Tremblay2012 .

Abell 2597 is extremely gas rich, with a molecular mass in its central regions of 4.6 0.5 10^ 9 M _ (by central regions, we refer the the approximate region shown in Fig fig:A2597_maps_and_spectra ). However, there are also filaments of molecular gas to the north and south extending out to around 20 kpc Tremblay2016, Tremblay2018 .

sources.summary: The paper treats PGC 071390 as a LINER-like brightest cluster galaxy with a soft ionising spectrum and radio-loud AGN, embedded in the Abell 2597 cool-core system.

quotes.quote: In the work presented here we focus on two LINER-like BCGs from our previous samples, PGC 071390 in Abell 2597 (hereafter A2597) and ESO 0291-G009 in Sersic 159-03 (hereafter S159). Optical ( [ O III ] /H and [ O I ] /H , VD97; BTh ) and mid-infrared spectra ( [ Ne III ] / [ Ne II ] and [ Ne V ] / [ Ne II ] , Jaffe Bremer in prep.) of these BCGs indicate that their ionising spectrum is very soft i.e. they are extreme LINERs VD97; BTh . However, these BCGs do contain radio-loud AGN. Their 1.4 GHz radio specific luminosity is, 29.3 10 ^ 31 and 1.6 10 ^ 31 erg s ^ 1 Hz ^ 1 for A2597 and S159 Bi08 respectively, which are typical for BCGs in cool core galaxy clusters Q08 .

sources.summary: The paper describes the Abell 2597 BCG as a nearby giant elliptical whose cold molecular clouds sustain star formation and fall inward to fuel its active central black hole.

quotes.quote: Here we report observations that reveal a cold, clumpy accretion flow towards a supermassive black hole fuel reservoir in the nucleus of the Abell 2597 Brightest Cluster Galaxy (BCG), a nearby (redshift z=0.0821 ) giant elliptical galaxy surrounded by a dense halo of hot plasma . Under the right conditions, thermal instabilities can precipitate from this hot gas, producing a rain of cold clouds that fall toward the galaxy's centre , sustaining star formation amid a kiloparsec-scale molecular nebula that inhabits its core . The observations show that these cold clouds also fuel black hole accretion, revealing `shadows' cast by the molecular clouds as they move inward at about 300 kilometres per second towards the active supermassive black hole in the galaxy centre, which serves as a bright backlight.

While no observation of a single galaxy can prove this theoretical prediction to be definitively true, the combined ALMA and VLBA dataset for Abell 2597 enable a uniquely unambiguous observation of molecular clouds that are either directly associated with black hole growth, or are soon about to be.

sources.summary: The paper cites independent low-metallicity evidence for A 2597 and groups it among BCGs requiring periodic starbursts at 20-200 Myr intervals.

quotes.quote: For A 2597 and NGC 1275, independent measurements lead to further evidence for low metallicities.

The derived abundances of N ii and S ii red and blue emission line doublets led them to conclude that the metallicty in A 2597 is about 0.5 solar, consistent with the result from X-ray observations Morris2005 .

While ZwCl 3146, A 1795, A 1835 and RXC J1504 seem to require continuous star formation, NGC 1275, PKS 0745, Hydra-A, A 1068 and A 2597 seem to require periodic starbursts with the interval ranging between 20 Myr and 200 Myr.

gmw_00001829.name: G4Jy 1795
gmw_00001829.overview: G4Jy 1795 is an FR II radio galaxy hosted by an elliptical galaxy [arXiv:2308.10071] that is likely interacting with nearby companion galaxies [arXiv:1008.2683, arXiv:2308.10071]. Faint bridges link G4Jy 1795 to these companions [arXiv:1008.2683], which may trigger star formation and disturb its molecular gas [arXiv:2308.10071].

sources.summary: The paper describes PKS 2250-41 as a powerful radio source whose GMOS-S morphology shows disturbed structure, companions, shells, fans, dust, tails, bridges, or multiple nuclei indicating interaction or merger activity.

quotes.quote: This NLRG is likely lying within a group, and possibly interacting with one or even more objects according the detailed analysis presented by Inskip08 using imaging and spectroscopy in the optical and NIR. The presence of a YSP is confirmed by the presence of PAH features in the Spitzer MIR spectrum of this source Dicken10 . The disk galaxy at 47 kpc to the NE of the radio source is at an almost identical redshift to PKS 2250-41 Inskip08 , and appears clearly disturbed in the direction of the radio galaxy. Our i' band GMOS-S image (Figure pks2250_online ) reveals a faint bridge linking with this NE companion, and a shorter one linking the radio galaxy with the smaller galaxy at 27 kpc towards the SW.

sources.summary: G4Jy 1795 is described as an FR II elliptical-host radio galaxy with jet-cloud interactions, shock-shaped emission, companion-triggered star formation, and disturbed molecular gas.

quotes.quote: G4Jy 1795: (a.k.a. PKS 2250-41) is a FR II radio galaxy hosted by an elliptical galaxy at a redshift of z =0.308 tadhunter93,inskip08 also listed in the MS4 and in the 2 Jy samples respectively burgess06a,morganti93 . It has an emission line region showing clear evidence of jet–cloud interactions tadhunter94,clark97,villar99,tilak05 , shaped as an arc around the western radio lobe with shock signatures inskip08 , probably due to star formation triggered by a faint companion. Recent ALMA observations revealed molecular gas with the broadest velocity profiles in the central, sub-kiloparsec region due to a direct interaction of the jet with dense clouds and outflowing molecular gas. On larger, kiloparsec-scales, the molecular gas appears to avoid the radio lobes, affected by the expanding cocoon around the radio source, likely dispersing and heating preexisting molecular clouds morganti21b .

gmw_00001830.name: G4Jy 386
gmw_00001830.overview: G4Jy 386 is a radio galaxy [arXiv:2308.10071] hosted by a Seyfert II galaxy [arXiv:1809.09327] that exhibits polarized emission from both of its radio lobes [arXiv:1809.09327]. The system features optical bridges connecting it to two companion galaxies, which suggests ongoing tidal interactions or mergers [arXiv:2308.10071].

sources.summary: The paper detects both lobes of PMN J0351 2744 in low-frequency polarisation, noting its Seyfert II host and consistency with prior MWA measurements.

quotes.quote: The North-Eastern lobe of PMN J0351 2744 (hereafter PMNJ0351) was the first polarised source detected by the MWA 32-tile prototype Bernardi2013 . Later, with the improved sensitivity and resolution afforded by the full 128-tile MWA, detected polarised emission from both lobes. We present a postage stamp image of PMNJ0351 in Figure fig:pmnj0351_postage . We detect both lobes in polarisation, at slightly different FD, although both are consistent with previous MWA results. PMNJ0351 is hosted by a Seyfert II galaxy at redshift z=0.0656 Mahony2011 .

sources.summary: G4Jy 386 is described as a radio galaxy with extended optical emission, narrow-line structure, bridges to companion galaxies, tidal interaction or merger signatures, and X-ray emission.

quotes.quote: G4Jy 386: is also known as PKS 0349-27 and PMN J0351-2744 and it was already detected as extended at 408 MHz schilizzi75a,schilizzi75b as well as at higher frequencies reid99 . It was then optically identified with the same counterpart associated in both the G4Jy at mid-IR frequencies and in our analysis being bolton65 classified as a radio galaxy at z= 0.6569 searle68,jones09 . This radio source shows extended emission in the optical band with remarkable features, including an extended narrow line region and bridges connecting G4Jy 386 to two companion galaxies inskip10,ramos13 . These bridges are interpreted as due to tidal interaction with neighbor galaxies and/or mergers danziger84,tadhunter89 . X-ray emission was also detected between the lobes and, with an offset of a few arcsec, on the location of the northern hotspot mingo17 .

gmw_00001831.name: G4Jy 462
gmw_00001831.overview: G4Jy 462 is a Fanaroff-Riley class I radio galaxy located in the nearby galaxy cluster Abell S 463 [arXiv:2308.10071]. It is characterized as a dumbbell galaxy with twin tails and exhibits weak emission lines in its optical spectrum [arXiv:2308.10071].

sources.summary: G4Jy 462 is identified as a dumb-bell FR I radio galaxy with twin tails in Abell S 463, weak emission lines, and a literature optical identification.

quotes.quote: G4Jy 462: is one of the dumb-bell FR I radio galaxies in the southern hemisphere ekers69,mcadam88,morganti93 , also known as IC 2082 and PKS 0427-53, with a twin tail carter81,lilly87 , and lying in the nearby galaxy cluster Abell S 463. It has a redshift measurement of z =0.03931 see e.g., raimann05 showing weak emission lines in its optical spectrum. It also belongs to both the sample of local radio galaxies detected at 20 GHz sadler14 . We adopted here the same optical identification provided in the literature see also jones92,burgess06b,white20b . However, the lack of high resolution radio maps prevented us to classify this radio source as a wide-angle tail (WAT) radio galaxy burns81,owen76,odonoghue90,odonoghue93,sakelliou00,missaglia19 .

gmw_00001832.name: G4Jy 85
gmw_00001832.overview: G4Jy 85 is a low-excitation Fanaroff-Riley II radio galaxy located at the center of a galaxy group or cluster [arXiv:1008.2683, arXiv:2308.10071]. It is surrounded by a diffuse halo and interacts with a nearby companion galaxy via an optical and infrared bridge [arXiv:1008.2683, arXiv:2308.10071].

sources.summary: The paper describes PKS 0043-42 as a powerful radio source whose GMOS-S morphology shows disturbed structure, companions, shells, fans, dust, tails, bridges, or multiple nuclei indicating interaction or merger activity.

quotes.quote: This WLRG/FRII appears to be located in the center of a group or cluster, surrounded by an extended diffuse halo (Figure pks0043_online ). There is faint companion located 8 kpc NE of the galaxy center. However, the lack of any other features and of galaxy distortion in this direction prevents us from classifying the radio galaxy as a double nucleus system, based on its optical morphology. However, from the K-band image presented in Inskip10 , the authors reported a central isophotal twist and an excess of emission along the NS direction.

sources.summary: PKS 0043-42 is presented as a WLRG with clear MIR silicate absorption, X-ray evidence for an obscuring torus, and clumpy torus fits implying cold-gas fueling.

quotes.quote: The deep Spitzer InfraRed Spectrograph (IRS) rest-frame mid-infrared (MIR) spectrum of the WLRG PKS 0043-42 (z=0.116) shows a clear spectral turnover at 20 suggestive of warm dust, as well as a 9.7 silicate absorption feature. In addition, the hard X-ray results, based on Chandra data, strongly support a picture in which PKS 0043 42 has a torus and accretion disc more typical of Strong-Line Radio Galaxies (SLRGs).

The MIR and X-ray spectra are markedly different from those of other WLRGs at similar redshifts, and here we show that the former can be successfully fitted with clumpy torus models with parameters characteristic of Type-2 AGN tori: close to edge-on ( i =74 ) and relatively broad ( =60 ), with an outer radius of 2 pc, N _H =1.6 ^ 0.2 _ 0.1 10 ^ 23 cm^ 2 , and AGN bolometric luminosity L _ bol ^ AGN = 1.6 ^ 0.2 _ 0.1 10 ^ 44 erg s^ 1 . The presence of a compact torus in PKS 0043-42 provides evidence that this WLRG is fuelled by cold, rather than hot, gas accretion.

sources.summary: G4Jy 85 is presented as a low-excitation FR II radio galaxy in a group or cluster environment, with a diffuse halo, companion bridge, hotspots, and faint ICM emission.

quotes.quote: G4Jy 85: , belonging to the 2 Jy sample a.k.a. PKS J0043-42; see e.g., wall85,morganti97 , is LERG hine79 at z =0.0526 whiteoak72,tadhunter93 with a very extended radio structure morganti99 and a classical FR II radio morphology. It appears to be located at the centre of a group/cluster of galaxies being also surrounded by a diffuse halo ramos11 . It also shows the presence of a bridge detected at infrared and optical frequencies related to the interaction with a nearby companion galaxy see also inskip10 . Both hotspots are detected in the soft X-rays together with a relative faint extended emission of the ICM mingo17 .

gmw_00001833.name: G4Jy 347
gmw_00001833.overview: G4Jy 347 is a giant radio galaxy with a physical size of 2.5 Mpc [arXiv:2308.10071]. It is associated with the optical source ESO 248-G-10 and resides within a galaxy filament near several galaxy clusters [arXiv:2308.10071].

sources.summary: G4Jy 347 is described as a 2.5 Mpc giant radio galaxy associated with ESO 248-G-10, located in a filamentary environment with nearby galaxy clusters.

quotes.quote: G4Jy 347: is a 2.5 Mpc giant radio galaxy a.k.a.MRC 0319-454, PMN J0321-4510 and MSH 03-43; see e.g., for a recent analysis burgess06b,malarecki15 located within a galaxy filament of the Horologium-Reticulum supercluster fleenor05 . The host galaxy bryant00 is located close to the north-eastern radio lobe, the only part of its radio structure reported in our finding chart, and it is associated with the optical source ESO 248-G-10 having a weak counterpart at 20 GHz saripalli94 . The redshift of the host galaxy is z= 0.0622 safouris09 and its radio structure in the north-eastern side goes through an environment having higher galaxy density than the southern radio structure, indicating the presence of a surrounding group of galaxies. There are also several galaxy clusters in its vicinity fleenor06 , namely: S0345, Abell 3111, Abell 3112 and APMCC369 at z =0.071, 0.078, 0.075 and 0.075, respectively.

gmw_00001834.name: G4Jy 747
gmw_00001834.overview: G4Jy 747 is a Fanaroff-Riley II narrow-line radio galaxy located at a redshift of 0.305 [arXiv:2308.10071]. It has been identified as a variable quasar with X-ray and near-infrared counterparts [arXiv:1311.2989], and it appears as a double system with a faint companion galaxy to its southwest [arXiv:2308.10071].

sources.summary: The paper identifies LEDA 2825724 as a variable quasar source with X-ray and near-infrared counterparts and gives a fitted radio spectral index from VLSS, MWA, and NVSS data.

quotes.quote: MWA J0901-2256 : Identified as quasar LEDA 2825724 LEDA . It also has an X-ray counterpart (1RXS J090129.1-255357) and near infrared galaxy (DENIS-P J090152.0-255333; Dennis ) within the positional errors. The spectral index derived from a best fit to the VLSS, MWA and NVSS data points is =-0.72 .

sources.summary: G4Jy 747 is described as an FR II narrow-line radio galaxy in a crowded field, appearing as a double system with a nearby faint component.

quotes.quote: G4Jy 747: is a FR II radio galaxy, optically classified as Narrow Line Radio Galaxy NLRG; ramos11 , at z =0.305 tadhunter93 , and listed in the 2 Jy sample wall85,morganti97 with mid-IR colors of ray blazars dabrusco19 . G4Jy 747 lies in a relatively crowded field with several nearby companion galaxies, and appears as a double system, including the radio galaxy nucleus and a faint component a few kpc in the south-western direction ramos11 . Its morphological classification is the same attributed from the near infrared investigation presented by Inskip et al. (2010).

gmw_00001835.name: G4Jy 644
gmw_00001835.overview: G4Jy 644 is a giant radio galaxy hosted by a Seyfert II galaxy at a redshift of z = 0.11 [arXiv:1809.09327, arXiv:2308.10071]. It exhibits a misaligned double-double, X-shaped morphology with polarized hotspots and emits hard X-rays [arXiv:1809.09327, arXiv:2308.10071].

sources.summary: The paper identifies PKS 0707 35 as a Seyfert II-hosted giant radio galaxy whose two polarised hotspots show Faraday-complex behavior and slight low-frequency repolarisation.

quotes.quote: PKS 0707 35 (hereafter PKS0707) is hosted by a Seyfert II galaxy at redshift z=0.11 Burgess2006 . At this redshift, the angular diameter between hotspots (approx. 8.5 arcmin) corresponds to a physical separation of 1.1 Mpc. We present postage stamp images of PKS0707 in the lower panels of Figure fig:doubles_postage . We detect two polarised sources associated with the hotspots of PKS0707.

For the NW (SE) lobe, our measured FD are =+62.5 0.3 ( =+56.6 0.2) rad m ^ 2 . The NW hotspot is consistent with the NVSS RM: _ NVSS = +56.5 12.6 , although the SE hotspot exhibits a discrepancy ( _ NVSS = +40.3 9.1) rad m ^ 2 . Additionally, both hotspots exhibit slight repolarisation, with a depolarisation ratio m_ 216MHz /m_ 1.4GHz = 1.34 for the SE hotspot and m_ 216MHz /m_ 1.4GHz = 1.14 for the NW hotspot.

sources.summary: PKS 0707--35 is described as an X-shaped, misaligned double-double giant radio galaxy whose inner emission is interpreted as remnant jet activity on a different axis.

quotes.quote: PKS 0707--35: presents a misaligned double-double morphology (X-shaped) as shown by . In Fig. maps1 (top-right panel), we report the ATCA image at 2.5 GHz presented by those authors: following their interpretation, the almost symmetric emission near the core is the remnant of a previous jet activity, with a different axis with respect to the external and much brighter lobes (sign of a recent fueling of plasma). The jet axis change, that presumably happened at a rate of a few degree/Myr, could likely be the cause of the X-shaped morphology. The same authors provide a radiative age for the inner lobes < 100 Myr and, based on the high advance speeds of the ends of the outer lobes (0.3 ), suggest a relatively young age of 6 Myr for the Mpc structure. 4C 73.08: thanks to a combination of interferometric and single-dish observations at 1.4 GHz, found an extended radio emission enveloping the whole Mpc-scale structure of the lobes.

sources.summary: The paper discusses PKS 0707-35 as a giant radio galaxy core, using literature or archive radio measurements to evaluate spectral shape, variability, or restarting activity.

quotes.quote: PKS 0707-35 : the source was previously studied by , who presented ATCA images at 1.4 GHz and 2.3 GHz. The flux densities reported for the core are 44.6 mJy and 53 mJy, respectively, with an inverted spectral index =0.44 (adopting the convention S= ^ , where S is the flux density, the frequency, and the spectral index). We use these two points, together with TGSS observations, to draw the spectral shape from 150 MHz to 2.3 GHz, finding no peak in this range.

sources.summary: G4Jy 644 is described as a giant and candidate double-double radio galaxy with misaligned inner and outer structures, hard X-ray emission, and a measured redshift.

quotes.quote: G4Jy 644: can be considered a giant radio galaxy even if its extension is below the 1 Mpc threshold ishwara99,malarecki15,proctor16,kuzmicz21 . It is also a double-double radio galaxy candidate, where the inner radio structure is misaligned with respect to the outer one jones92,bruni20 . G4Jy 644 emits in the hard X-rays a.k.a. PBC J0709.2-3601 cusumano10,koss17,bruni21 , and it lies at z =0.11 parisi14 .

gmw_00001836.name: G4Jy 33
gmw_00001836.overview: G4Jy 33 is a high-redshift radio galaxy with an estimated redshift of z = 1.589 [arXiv:2308.10071]. The galaxy is situated within a galaxy-rich, large-scale environment [arXiv:2308.10071].

sources.summary: G4Jy 33 is described as a high-redshift radio galaxy in a galaxy-rich environment, associated with 3C 8 and other radio designations.

quotes.quote: G4Jy 33: is also known as 3C 8, PKS 0016-12 and MRC 0016-129. This source belongs to the original 3C catalog edge59 but not to its revised version spinrad85 . This radio source, classified as a high redshift radio galaxy, lies in a galaxy-rich large-scale environment wylezalek13 and it has a redshift estimate of z =1.589 best99 .

sources.summary: G4Jy 33 is described as a high-redshift radio galaxy in a galaxy-rich environment, associated with 3C 8 and other radio designations.

quotes.quote: G4Jy 33: is also known as 3C 8, PKS 0016-12 and MRC 0016-129. This source belongs to the original 3C catalog edge59 but not to its revised version spinrad85 . This radio source, classified as a high redshift radio galaxy, lies in a galaxy-rich large-scale environment wylezalek13 and it has a redshift estimate of z =1.589 best99 .

gmw_00001837.name: G4Jy 685
gmw_00001837.overview: G4Jy 685 is a Fanaroff-Riley II narrow-line radio galaxy [arXiv:0810.3764, arXiv:2308.10071] located in a galaxy-rich environment [arXiv:2308.10071]. It exhibits a disturbed optical morphology with shells, a broad fan, and interaction signatures [arXiv:1008.2683, arXiv:2308.10071], alongside complex double radio lobes and bright hotspots [arXiv:0810.3764].

sources.summary: The paper presents 3C 195 as the most distant narrow-line radio galaxy in the sample, with double lobes, bright hotspots, complex lobe structure, and VLT-targeted hotspots.

quotes.quote: With a redshift of z =0.11 the Narrow-Line Radio Galaxy (Morganti et al. 1997) 3C 195 is the most distant source in our sample. Neff et al. (1995) describe it as a large double-lobe source with bright hotspots and a complex lobe structure, suggesting small changes of the radio axis. The optical protrusion, or jet, orthogonal to the radio axis, noted by these authors, turns out to be an underlying spiral galaxy as can be seen at higher resolution (Fig. 2). Both, southern and northern hotspot, were selected as targets for the VLT observations (Fig. 3).

sources.summary: The paper describes PKS 0806-10 as a powerful radio source whose GMOS-S morphology shows disturbed structure, companions, shells, fans, dust, tails, bridges, or multiple nuclei indicating interaction or merger activity.

quotes.quote: The GMOS-S image of this NLRG/FRII (Figure pks0806_online ) shows a clearly disturbed morphology, including features such as a broad fan towards the East ( _V =23.4 mag arcsec ^ 2 ), a bright shell to NW, and a fainter shell towards the West ( _V =23.3 and 24.6 mag arcsec ^ 2 , respectively). The unsharp-masked image also show several bright knots of emission closer to the galaxy center (see Figure pks0806_online a). Emission line contamination of the features is not a serious issue here because strong emission lines do not fall in the ' band filter employed in our observations. Inskip10 also report the detection of the NW shell (or arc-like feature) in their K-band images.

sources.summary: G4Jy 685 is described as an FR II radio galaxy whose X-ray core emission and optical interaction signatures place it in a galaxy-rich environment.

quotes.quote: G4Jy 685: is known as 3C 195 and PKS 0806-10, a FR II radio galaxy at z =0.10898 tadhunter93,jones09 listed in the 2Jy sample wall85,morganti93 . The detection of extended X-ray emission around the radio core ineson15,mingo17 coupled with optical signatures of galaxy interaction inskip10,ramos11 clearly indicates that G4Jy 685 is harbored in a galaxy-rich large-scale environment. The southern radio hotspot is also detected in the X-rays mingo17 .

gmw_00001838.name: G4Jy 492
gmw_00001838.overview: G4Jy 492 is a Fanaroff-Riley II narrow-line radio galaxy [arXiv:2211.13031, arXiv:2308.10071] that exhibits a faint, sharply defined shell [arXiv:1008.2683] and surrounding diffuse X-ray emission [arXiv:2308.10071]. It has several nearby companion galaxies [arXiv:1008.2683, arXiv:2308.10071] and shows potential for non-thermal core contamination at far-infrared wavelengths [arXiv:2211.13031].

sources.summary: The paper identifies PKS0442-28 as a potential and especially clear NLRG/FRII core-contamination case, links the contamination to high radio luminosity, and flags possible ALMA-core variability.

quotes.quote: Using this criterion, six objects show potential for non-thermal core contamination at far-IR wavelengths: PKS0034-01, PKS0038+09, PKS0442-28, PKS0625-53, PKS1602+01 and PKS1932-46.

The two NLRG/FRII objects that show clearest evidence for non-thermal core contamination at far-IR wavelengths are PKS0442-28 Note that although its ALMA and high frequency radio fluxes are high relative to the far-IR fluxes for this source, PKS0042-28 remains an uncertain case because of the lack of LABOCA and SPIRE flux measurements. and PKS2356-61. It is unlikely that the prominence of the non-thermal core components in these cases is due to beaming effects, for example related to intermediate inclinations of their jets to the line of sight, since the core-to-extended radio flux ratios of the two sources are typical of NLRG sources and somewhat below the average values for BLRG/Q objects in the 2Jy sample morganti97 . Rather, it is more plausible that the relatively high level of radio core contamination in these sources is related to their high overall radio luminosities: they represent the two objects with the highest total radio luminosities of the 20 FRII radio galaxies in the 2Jy sample at z<0.15 tadhunter98 .
However, we only see such evidence in two objects: PKS0043-42 and PKS0442-28.

sources.summary: The paper describes PKS 0442-28 as a powerful radio source whose GMOS-S morphology shows disturbed structure, companions, shells, fans, dust, tails, bridges, or multiple nuclei indicating interaction or merger activity.

quotes.quote: This NLRG/FRII galaxy shows a faint, but sharply defined shell 50 kpc to SW of its nucleus in our GMOS-S image (Figure pks0442_online ). This shell has a surface brightness _V =25.7 mag arcsec ^ 2 . There are several galaxies within 20 , any of them possibly interacting with the radio galaxy. There are neither optical long-slit spectra nor infrared data suitable for assessing the degree of emission line contamination of this shell.

sources.summary: G4Jy 492 is described as an FR II radio galaxy with nearby companions, diffuse X-ray emission, detected radio core and northern hotspot, and hard X-ray emission.

quotes.quote: G4Jy 492: is a FR II radio galaxy at z =0.147 tadhunter93,diserego94,best99 with several nearby companion galaxies ramos13 and surrounding diffuse X-ray emission. G4Jy 492, also belonging to the 2 Jy sample see e.g., wall85,morganti93 , has been recently observed in the X-rays and both its radio core and the northern hotspot were detected mingo17 . It was also detected in hard X-ray band see e.g., cusumano10,oh18 .

gmw_00001839.name: G4Jy 77
gmw_00001839.overview: G4Jy 77 is an extended radio source located approximately 360 kiloparsecs from the cool core galaxy cluster Abell 85 [arXiv:2308.10071]. It is a radio phoenix containing fossil plasma from past active galactic nucleus activity and exhibits a very steep radio spectrum [arXiv:2308.10071].

sources.summary: G4Jy 77 is described as an extended radio source near Abell 85, with cluster-scale separation and radio-phoenix emission from fossil AGN plasma.

quotes.quote: G4Jy 77: is an extended radio source located at 6 \ from the X-ray position of the cool core galaxy cluster Abell 85 see e.g., for details about its X-ray emission durret05,ichinohe15 . At the redshift of the galaxy cluster i.e., z =0.0557; abell58,abell89,pislar97,oegerle01 the kpc scale is 1.1 kpc/arcsec, thus the distance between G4Jy 77 and Abell 85 is 360 kpc. It is a well-known radio phoenix kempner04 , example of fossil plasma present in galaxy clusters due to past AGN activity, with a very steep radio spectrum bagchi98,slee01,rahaman22 .

gmw_00001840.name: G4Jy 122
gmw_00001840.overview: G4Jy 122 is a classical double radio galaxy [arXiv:2308.10071] whose central active galactic nucleus is classified as a Seyfert 2 [arXiv:1801.01751] or narrow-line radio galaxy [arXiv:2308.10071]. The host galaxy appears connected to an early-type companion galaxy located 70 kpc to the northwest [arXiv:2308.10071].

sources.summary: The paper describes 3C32 as the largest source in its sample, a Seyfert-type radio source whose KDA EXT model is statistically preferred despite imperfect data fitting.

quotes.quote: 3C32 The radio source 3C32 is the largest source in the examined sample, with its lobes totalling 385.7 kpc in length. Its central AGN have been classified as Seyfert type 2 galaxy by Véron-Cetty Véron (2006). The spectra resulting from the predicted flux densities S_ MOD (KDA) and S_ MOD (KDA EXT) are shown in Figure 2. In this case, the preference of the KDA EXT model over KDA model is evident, with the value of 0.002 for a level of significance of 10 . However, the goodness of the fit to data points ( ^ 2 is not high and some observational data points are very far away from the trend line (compare Table 3)).

sources.summary: G4Jy 122 is described as a classical double narrow-line radio galaxy with X-ray Fe K emission and a possible connected companion galaxy.

quotes.quote: G4Jy 122: is a classical double radio galaxy at z =0.4 tadhunter93 , optically classified as a narrow line radio galaxy see also ramos11 and showing a prominent Fe K emission line in its \ observation mingo14 . This radio source is known as PKS 0105-16 and it is listed in the original 3C catalog as 3C 32 edge59 but not in the 3CR revised version spinrad85 . Archival optical images seems to indicate that the host galaxy of G4Jy 122 appears to be connected with an early-type galaxy of similar brightness located at 70 kpc in the north-western direction ramos11 .

gmw_00001841.name: G4Jy 162
gmw_00001841.overview: G4Jy 162 is a high-redshift radio galaxy located at a redshift of approximately 2.35 [arXiv:1610.02057, arXiv:2308.10071]. It features very extended radio jets [arXiv:1610.02057] and a fairly regular emission-line morphology that is roughly aligned with its radio axis [arXiv:2308.10071].

sources.summary: BLR 0128 264 is described as a powerful high-redshift radio galaxy with extended jets, resolved emission-line gas, compact continuum emission, and a large velocity gradient.

quotes.quote: BLR 0128-264 BLR 0128 264 at z = 2.35 has very extended radio jets with a projected size of 35.8 , corresponding to 294 kpc at z = 2.35, and is one of our most powerful radio galaxies, with P _ 500 =29.1 W Hz ^ 1 at 500 MHz in the rest-frame. With SINFONI we detect [ OIII ] 4959,5007, H and H , as well as [ SII ] 6716,6731, and [ NII ] 6583 (Fig. fig:intspec ). The lines are well fit with a single component of FWHM = 1136 20 km s ^ 1 (the error is that measured for [ OIII ] 5007).

Line emission in BLR 0128-264 is spatially resolved but not very extended with a size of 3.2 1.5 \ along the major and minor axis, respectively. Compact continuum emission is detected roughly in the center of the emission line region. The emission line morphology is fairly regular, albeit with higher [ OIII ] surface brightness towards the north (Fig. fig:maps ). The velocity field of BLR 0128 264 has a remarkably large gradient of v 1500 km s ^ 1 .

sources.summary: G4Jy 162 is described as a clear optical-counterpart radio galaxy, the most distant source in the sample, with regular emission-line morphology aligned with the radio axis.

quotes.quote: G4Jy 162: has a clear optical counterpart, while the lack of a mid-IR associated source is mainly due to the artifacts present in the WISE image created by a nearby bright star located in the south-western direction. In the literature G4Jy 162 is classified as a radio galaxy with a z =2.34665 best99,nesvadba17 being the most distant source listed in the \ sample to date. G4Jy 162 also shows a fairly regular emission line region morphology, albeit an increasing of the [ OIII ] surface brightness towards the northern side, and roughly aligned with the radio axis in the north-western side nesvadba17 .

gmw_00001842.name: G4Jy 524
gmw_00001842.overview: G4Jy 524 is a radio galaxy characterized by a lobe-dominated radio structure [arXiv:2308.10071]. It is classified as an ultra-steep-spectrum radio source and is surrounded by several nearby companion galaxies [arXiv:2308.10071].

sources.summary: G4Jy 524 is described as a lobe-dominated radio galaxy with nearby companion galaxies and membership in an ultra-steep-spectrum radio-source sample.

quotes.quote: G4Jy 524: is a radio galaxy (a.k.a. MRC 0508-187 and TXS 0508-187) with a lobe dominated radio structure. The optical image shows the presence of several nearby companion galaxies. G4Jy 524 is also listed in a sample of ultra steep spectrum radio sources debreuck00 .

gmw_00001843.name: G4Jy 934
gmw_00001843.overview: G4Jy 934 is a Fanaroff-Riley II radio galaxy [arXiv:2308.10071]. It has a photometric redshift estimate of z = 0.67, but it lacks optical spectroscopic information [arXiv:2308.10071].

sources.summary: G4Jy 934 is described as an FR II radio galaxy whose mid-infrared counterpart matches catalog identifications, while optical spectroscopy remains unavailable.

quotes.quote: G4Jy 934: a FR II radio galaxy listed in the MS4 sample with a photometric redshift estimate of z =0.67 burgess06b . The mid-IR associated source listed in the G4Jy catalog is the one marked in the finding chart and in agreement with the MS4 identification burgess06a , which is based on the possible radio core detection proposed by Duncan Sproats (1992) as reported in White et al. (2020b). The MRC 1136-320 radio source position is also cospatial with the associated mid-IR counterpart. The lack of any optical spectroscopic information prevented us to associate it with its optical counterpart, also because the closest source to the radio centroid is the brightest one, as visible in our finding chart.

gmw_00001844.name: G4Jy 1677 G4Jy 1678
gmw_00001844.overview: G4Jy 1677 G4Jy 1678, also known as NGC 7016, is a radio galaxy [arXiv:1312.5311, arXiv:2308.10071] located in the Abell 3744 galaxy cluster [arXiv:2308.10071]. It is an interacting system [arXiv:2308.10071] that hosts an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1312.5311] and exhibits X-ray synchrotron emission from an inner radio jet [arXiv:1312.5311].

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 7016 as an isolated radio galaxy with cooler X-ray atmosphere, active-nucleus power-law emission, and X-ray synchrotron emission from an inner radio jet.
sources.paper_coordinate_text: radio-core position of 21^ h 07^ m 16^ s . 28, -25^ 28' 08'' . 5

quotes.quote: NGC 7016 is a relatively isolated galaxy (Fig.  fig:n7016X-2MASS ). The 886 net counts from a 12-arcsec-radius source-centered circle (Fig.  fig:n7016 ) give a poor fit to a thermal model alone ( ^2/ dof = 79.9/30), but the fit is acceptable when a power law (with no excess absorption) is added to account for emission from the active nucleus. The abundance is poorly constrained and was fixed at 0.3 times solar in the two-component fit. Results are given in Table  tab:gspectab . The temperature is cooler than that of the surrounding cluster, as expected for a galaxy atmosphere.

The power-law parameter values were consistent with those from the larger extraction region, demonstrating an active-nucleus origin of the non-thermal emission. The power-law X-ray flux density (Table  tab:gspectab ) and 4.96-GHz core flux density of 58.3 mJy lie within the scatter of the correlation of unabsorbed nuclear emission components of evans , arguing in favor of a common non-thermal origin. An extension of the X-ray emission in the direction of the radio jet (Fig.  fig:n7016 ) strongly suggests X-ray synchrotron emission from an inner radio jet, found by \ to be a common feature of FR I radio galaxies worrall01 .

sources.summary: G4Jy 1677 and G4Jy 1678 are described as interacting cluster radio galaxies in Abell 3744, with separate redshifts and supporting optical-radio imaging.

quotes.quote: G4Jy 1677 G4Jy 1678: (a.k.a., NGC 7016 NGC 7018) both belong to the galaxy cluster Abell 3744 abell58,abell89,dacosta98,katgert98,postman95 and lie at z =0.036845 and z =0.03881, respectively garilli93,tadhunter93,smith00 . More details about these interacting galaxies are given in the literature see e.g., and references therein worrall14 while the optical image with a larger field of view retrieved from the Pan-STARRS archive and with radio contours overlaid is shown in Figure fig:G4Jy1678-G4Jy1677 . th

gmw_00001845.name: Ursa Major II
aliases_external: Ursa Major II dwarf spheroidal galaxy
gmw_00001845.overview: Ursa Major II is an ultra-faint dwarf spheroidal galaxy [arXiv:1712.03095] located approximately 30 to 34 kiloparsecs away [arXiv:0705.4622, arXiv:2110.15376]. It is characterized by a low star formation rate [arXiv:2010.05977], a highly metal-poor stellar population [arXiv:1409.4768, arXiv:1704.07656], and a massive, dark-matter-dominated halo [arXiv:0709.1510, arXiv:1712.03095].

sources.summary: The paper describes Ursa Major II as an extremely faint satellite associated with the Orphan Stream direction, lacking a clear velocity peak but having probable low-velocity members.

quotes.quote: The very faint Ursa Major II satellite is presented in zucker06c and was also discussed by grillmair06 . With a total magnitude of only M_V 3.8 , it would be one of the faintest known Milky Way satellites, though its structural parameters are not well constrained, with a distance of 30 5 and a half-light radius that could be between 50 and 120 . UMaII is peculiar in the sense that it lies spot-on the extrapolation of the orbit of the so-called Orphan Stream of belokurov06b and grillmair06 , a stellar stream that extends over 60 on the sky at distances ranging from 15 at its nearest extension to 30 where it is nearest to UMaII (located 10 away). UMaII also overlaps with the detection of Complex A, an H i high velocity cloud.

The most probable UMaII members (filled circles) are distributed in two small groups of stars : one group has v_r 120 , and is composed of stars with a similar metallicity ( 1.8 ). Since it is clearly distinct from Galactic contaminants (hollow circles), and has the preferred metallicity value of zucker06c for UMaII, it seems more likely that this group of stars belongs to the UMaII structure. Therefore, we henceforth only consider the group of stars with the lowest radial velocity as UMaII members ( 130<v_r<-90 ; the dashed lines in Figure 7) and assume the group with a higher velocity ( v_r 50 ) to be made of Galactic contaminants. Ursa Major II is the only observed satellite that does not show a clear radial velocity peak in our survey, even though it is not the smallest of our samples.

sources.summary: VERITAS devoted a large post-upgrade exposure to Ursa Major II, treating it as a high-J-factor dwarf spheroidal and deriving gamma-ray flux and annihilation limits.

quotes.quote: As shown in Table 2, a large amount of the total dSph observing time after the camera upgrade has been devoted to Ursa Major II. Ursa Major II was discovered in SDSS data with follow-up observations from Subaru . It is one of the faintest known dSphs, with velocity dispersion measurements made with only 20 stars , but with a large mass-to-luminosity ratio. J factors for Ursa Major II typically indicate that it is one of the highest, often similar to or even exceeding Segue 1 (e.g. .

An excess of 1 above the background was found at the Ursa Major II position. We report a flux upper limit of 1.4 10 ^ 8 m ^ 2 s ^ 1 above 200 GeV at a 95 confidence level using the method of Rolke assuming a powerlaw with index of -2.4. Figure 2 shows preliminary annihilation cross section limits for Ursa Major II. A version of the full maximum likelihood introduced by Aleksi et. al. was used for the search for DM annihilations and for determining the limit to the velocity-averaged cross section.

sources.summary: The paper presents Ursa Major II as a dark-matter-dominated ultra-faint dwarf spheroidal observed by MAGIC, deriving gamma-ray non-detection limits for dark matter annihilation.

quotes.quote: Ursa Major II (UMaII) is believed to be one of the most DM dominated ultra-faint dSphs, with a Mass--to--Light ratio M/L 4000 ^ +3700 _ 2000 M_ /L_ . It has an absolute magnitude of M_V 3.8 , and a distance of 30 kpc (at RA (J2000) = 8 ^h 51 ^ 30.0 ^ and Dec (J2000) = +63 ^ 07'48''). According to kinematic studies , the maximum containment angle of DM emission is _ max 0.53^ In the spherically symmetric model of dSph, the DM density profile is a function of the halo-centric radius. A scale radius is representative of the extension of the innermost DM density profile. According to the kinematic data of member stars it sets a limit beyond of which the density profile is steeply falling and even the expected DM emission.

The MAGIC telescopes conducted a deep observation campaign at VHE toward the UMaII dSph, a promising target for indirect DM searches. The source was observed between December 2014 and April 2016, resulting in 94.8 hours of excellent-quality data. This campaign represents an important step toward an optimal ``target diversification strategy'' at VHE aimed at enhancing the chances of discovery of DM signals and reducing possible biases in target selection.

sources.summary: The paper models Ursa Major II as an ultrafaint dwarf spheroidal dark-matter target, quantifying its D-factor uncertainties and using it in XRISM/Athena and CTA sensitivity projections.

quotes.quote: The resulting D factor distributions for the different prior assumptions studied here are presented in Fig. fig:D_distribution_UMa_II for the ultrafaint dSph, Ursa Major II. We find that physically motivated priors yield narrower posterior distributions with smaller median than the uninformative prior, as found in Ref. . Median and 68 credible regions for (D_ dSphs / [ GeV cm^ 2 ] ) integrated out to 0.5 ^ with these posteriors are 18.25_ 0.26 ^ +0.20 ( V_ peak >14 km s ^ 1 ), 18.32_ 0.25 ^ +0.17 ( V_ peak >18 km s ^ 1 ), 18.38_ 0.28 ^ +0.16 ( V_ peak >22 km s ^ 1 ), and 18.58_ 0.38 ^ +0.30 (log-uniform prior). We also note that, for the Ursa Major II, the Milky-Way halo contribution gives 60 of the total D factor.

Figure fig:groupC_Athena_XRISM shows the 95 CL upper limits on ^2 2 as a function of sterile neutrino masses m_ _s both for XRISM and Athena, which would be obtained by observing two dSphs, Draco (left) and Ursa Major II (right). The thick and thin bands show 68 and 95 containment regions, respectively, around the medians shown with curves in the middle. The uncertainties come from both those of D factor estimates and the Poisson fluctuation of the X-ray photon count. We also show the best-fit parameter region of claimed 3.5-keV X-ray line , and this study clearly shows that the claim can be definitely assessed with these future instruments by looking at promising dSphs.

sources.summary: The paper finds a larger Ursa Major II distance than earlier RRab work, cannot reproduce that quoted value, and argues the stated calibration better matches the Gaia-based distance.

quotes.quote: dallora_stellar_2012 report the detection of a single RRab star in Ursa Major II. They find =17.70 0.13 , whereas, with the same data, we find =18.29 0.15 . In trying to reconcile the difference, we found we could not replicate the distance quoted in dallora_stellar_2012 . Specifically, they use the RR Lyrae calibration of clementini2003 : M_V & = (0.214 0.047) ( Fe/H + 1.5) & + (0.54 0.017 0.085) for Fe/H =-2.47 . This results in M_V = 0.34 .

The distance we derive using the data from dallora_stellar_2012 and calibration as stated in the paper is in much better agreement with our Gaia based distance. Though we are not able to reproduce the literature RR Lyrae distance using their same data and calibration, we do note that the above clementini2003 calibration is only valid for [ Fe/H ] >-2.1 , whereas Ursa Major II is 0.4 dex more metal-poor. Other literature distances to Ursa Major II are from fitting CMDs of the very shallow discovery SDSS data, or follow up data, and report a range of distances from =17.34 to =17.90 e.g., zucker2006, liu2008 .

sources.summary: The paper identifies Ursa Major II as one of three dwarf spheroidals dominating the Fermi excess; nearby cataloged radio/blazar sources are unlikely to explain much of that signal.

quotes.quote: In Table tab:BZCAT we list the dSphs used in the Fermi-LAT analysis which are located within 1^ of at least one BZCAT or CRATES source. Of most interest are the three dSphs which dominate the excess observed by Fermi: Segue 1, Usra Major II, and Willman 1 Ackermann:2013yva . Although, these three dSphs each have one BZCAT or CRATES source within this radius, none of these sources are particularly nearby (all are >0.7^ away). In order to test the impact of these three sources, we utilize the Fermi tools and calculate their TS values to be 0.00, 4.23 and 9.71 for J100955+160223 (0.70 ^ from Segue 1), J0854+6218 (0.91 ^ from Ursa Major II) and J1048+5009 (0.87 ^ from Willman 1), respectively. In order to estimate the TS value of these sources as evaluated at the location of the dSphs under investigation, we produce 50 simulations for a location 0.7 ^ from a simulated TS=10 source.

This indicates that these three BZCAT and CRATES sources are unlikely to be responsible for a significant fraction of the dSph excess. However, we note that a more thorough re-analysis of the dSph population should investigate the potential for low-TS emission from this population.

sources.summary: The paper revises Ursa Major II membership, removes a foreground dwarf, finds little impact from removals, and uses it to illustrate halo-prior effects on J and D posteriors.

quotes.quote: We have made slight membership changes to the Simon2007ApJ...670..313S data for the following dSphs: Canes Venatici I, Coma Berenices, Leo IV, Ursa Major I, and Ursa Major II. We have removed RR Lyrae from the following (identified after the original spectroscopic analysis): Canes Venatici I 5 RR Lyrae identified in Kuehn2008ApJ...674L..81K , Coma Berenices 1 star; Musella2009ApJ...695L..83M , Leo IV 1 star; Moretti2009ApJ...699L.125M , and Ursa Major I 3 stars; Garofalo2013ApJ...767...62G . RR Lyrae are pulsating stars with variable velocity; these stars are dSph members, however, without additional phase information we cannot determine the star's bulk velocity. In Ursa Major II, a foreground dwarf has been removed that was identified in follow-up high-resolution spectroscopy analysis Frebel2010ApJ...708..560F . These removals do not have a large impact as the removed stars tend to have large error bars.

To quantify the effect of this prior, we re-analyze several galaxies (Bo o tes I, Carina II, Reticulum II, Ursa Major II, Draco, and Leo II) without this prior and expand the r_s prior range ( 3 < _ 10 r_s/ kpc < 1.4 ). While there is no change for the Draco values (the posterior favors larger r_s values) all other galaxies show an increase in the J-Factor and most show a decrease in the D-Factor. This affect is generally larger in the ultra-faint galaxies as the halo properties are more likely to be prior dominated. As an example, Figure fig:rsprior2 shows a corner plot comparing the posteriors of r_s , J(0.5 ) , and D(0.1 ) for Ursa Major II.

sources.summary: The paper treats Ursa Major II as a very low-luminosity Local Group dwarf and adds HST photometry of its sparse cluster to the globular-cluster system analysis.

quotes.quote: Ursa Major II, at a distance of 34 kpc dall'ora+2012 , is one of the lowest-luminosity dwarfs in the Local Group at M_V = -4.2 . Zucker+2006 noted that a small clump of stars near its center could be an embedded star cluster, but the cluster has not been studied further and it does not appear in the compilations of simon2019 and drlica-wagner+2020 . The MAST Archive of HST data contains a pair of images of UMaII (from a survey program GO-14734 for Local Group dwarfs) that fortunately includes the cluster. We downloaded these images and carried out photometry in order to characterize the color-magnitude diagram (CMD) and radial profile of the cluster and its surroundings.

UMaII itself is an extremely sparse object, but it extends across a very much larger area than does the HST/ACS field measured here, and martin+2008 give M_* = 5.4 10^3 M_ for it. The specific mass ratio is then S_M = 1.1 , though we emphasize this is very uncertain. An alternate interpretation for the UMaII cluster, which is very near the galaxy center, is that it may be the remnant of an NSC (Nuclear Star Cluster).

sources.summary: The paper targets Ursa Major II for Subaru-IRCS ALP dark-matter decay searches, gives its coordinates and distance, finds a cusped DM profile, and predicts high differential D-factor sensitivity.

quotes.quote: Here our main targets are Draco  (central sky coordinates _ 2000 = 17:20:12, _ 2000 =+ 57:54:55, distance  D_ =76 6  kpc) and Ursa Major II  ( _ 2000 = 08:51:30, _ 2000 =+ 63:07:48, D_ =32 4  kpc) .

In the analysis of the differential D factor in this paper following Hayashi:2020jze,Hayashi:2022wnw , both Draco and Ursa Major II favor a cusped inner slope of their DM density profiles. Using their resultant DM density profiles, we calculate _ D distributions for Draco and Ursa Major II. Focusing on the center of the dSphs, the values of _ 10 ( _ D/( GeV cm^ 2 sr^ 1 )= 22.60^ +0.27 _ 0.26 for Draco and = 22.50^ +1.02 _ 0.52 for Ursa Major II, respectively, at arcsec .

By concentrating on the decay of dark matter into two particles, one of which is a photon, in the Draco and Ursa Major II dwarf spheroidal galaxies, we demonstrated that an (1) nights observation by IRCS in Subaru Telescope yields sensitivity for the decay rate at a world-record level. In terms of axion-like particles, this surpasses the star cooling bounds in the mass range of 1 eV to 2 eV, aligning with various hints related to the cosmic-infrared background and theoretical predictions of some well-motivated models. Our study strongly advocates for the actual observations of the corresponding dSphs and offers an applicable approach for the indirect detection of dark matter using high-energy and high-angular-resolution detectors.

sources.summary: The paper treats Ursa Major II as an extreme Local Group case: the least massive galaxy with a globular cluster, yet not anomalous in model-constrained stellar mass-to-light ratio.

quotes.quote: Ursa Major II is by far the least massive galaxy to host a globular cluster. Its luminosity is only 10^ 3.704 L_ , and its stellar mass is found to be 10^ 3.703 M_ . Its singular globular cluster is a central cluster that has a stellar mass (lower limit) of 52M_ . The cluster is discussed in detail in Eadie2022 , and we refer interested readers to Appendix A of that paper for an in-depth discussion of the cluster.

Since the cluster is a central cluster, it may not be best defined as a globular cluster. However, we have defined globular clusters purely based on age and not position, and so we include the cluster in our sample.

The stellar mass of Ursa Major II is well constrained by the model compared to the broad prior, and does not have a stellar mass (or M/L ratio) that deviates significantly from that expected from the mean M/L .

sources.summary: The paper tests Ursa Major II as a possible Orphan Stream progenitor and rejects that association using its radial velocity, distance, and the stream-density trend.

quotes.quote: Ursa Major II In Figure 1, Ursa Major II zetal06 is visible at the right hand side of the diagram near (l,b) = (152^ ,37^ ) , not far from the extension of the projected Sgr orbit. Kinematics of UMa II metal07 , show it to have a v_ radial = -115 km s^ 1 (following the body and figures of that paper rather than the abstract) which corresponds to v_ gsr 35 km s^ 1 .
