This is in strong disagreement with the Orphan model predictions of v_ gsr +60 km s ^ 1 at the approximate location of UMa II.

The distance d_ to the object is also in disagreement with the models in Figures 12 and 13. Additionally, the density of Orphan Stream stars, were UMa II the Orphan progenitor, should increase as one gets closer to UMa II, not decrease. For these reasons, given the improved SEGUE data points and implied Orphan Stream kinematics, the current data do not support the hypothesis that UMa II is the progenitor of the Orphan Stream.

sources.summary: The paper presents Ursa Major II photometry, finds an RR Lyrae distance of about 34.7 kpc, and reports evidence for a subgiant-branch metallicity spread and multiple stellar populations.

quotes.quote: We present a B,V color-magnitude diagram (CMD) of the Milky Way dwarf satellite Ursa Major II (UMa II), spanning the magnitude range from V 15 to V 23.5 mag and extending over a 18 18 arcmin ^2 area centered on the galaxy. Our photometry goes down to about 2 magnitudes below the galaxy's main sequence turn-off, that we detected at V 21.5 mag. We have discovered a bona-fide RR Lyrae variable star in UMa II, which we use to estimate a conservative dereddened distance modulus for the galaxy of (m-M)_ 0 =17.70 0.04 0.12 mag, where the first error accounts for the uncertainties of the calibrated photometry, and the second reflects our lack of information on the metallicity of the star.

The corresponding distance to UMa II is 34.7 ^ +0.6 _ 0.7 (^ +2.0 _ 1.9 ) kpc. Our photometry shows evidence of a spread in the galaxy subgiant branch, compatible with a spread in metal abundance in the range between Z =0.0001 and Z =0.001. Based on our estimate of the distance, a comparison of the fiducial lines of the Galactic globular clusters (GCs) M68 and M5 ( [ Fe/H ] = 2.27 0.04 dex and 1.33 0.02 dex, respectively), with the position on the CMD of spectroscopically confirmed galaxy members, may suggest the existence of stellar populations of different metal abundance/age in the central region of UMa II.

sources.summary: The paper uses Green Bank Telescope radio observations of Ursa Major II to model its dark-matter halo and constrain WIMP annihilation through predicted synchrotron emission.

quotes.quote: We focus on models of the Ursa Major II data from Paper I: the large observed field provides several independent radial profile data points to work with, and the dark matter content of Ursa Major II is significantly better constrained than that of the potentially disrupting Willman I . We model the halo of Ursa Major II by a Navarro-Frenk-White (NFW) profile , and compute the expected synchrotron radiation for leptonic and hadronic channels.

As described in Paper I , four dwarf galaxies in the local group namely Draco, Ursa Major II, Coma Berenices, and Willman I, were targeted for observation with the Green Bank Telescope at 1.4 GHz. Of these galaxies, a large map was obtained for Ursa Major II, and it is minimally contaminated by foreground emission. We therefore analyze data from this field to constrain dark matter and astrophysical properties. t 0.8 The Robert C.
In this article, we used radio observations of the Ursa Major II dwarf galaxy to place constraints on WIMP dark matter annihilation.

sources.summary: The paper includes Ursa Major II in the joint dwarf-spheroidal likelihood analysis, finds no significant gamma-ray signal, and identifies it as a primary contributor to the deviation from the null hypothesis.

quotes.quote: Thus, we are left with a subset of 15 dwarf spheroidal galaxies as input to the joint likelihood analysis: Bootes I, Canes Venatici II, Carina, Coma Berenices, Draco, Fornax, Hercules, Leo II, Leo IV, Sculptor, Segue 1, Sextans, Ursa Major II, Ursa Minor, and Willman 1. No significant signal is found for any of the spectra tested in the combined analysis, prompting us to calculate 95 CL constraints on using the delta-log-likelihood procedure described above ( fig4 ).

The sensitivity of the combined analysis depends most heavily on the observations of the dwarf galaxies with the largest . Specifically, the results of the combined analysis are dominated by Coma Berenices, Draco, Segue 1, Ursa Major II, Ursa Minor, and Willman 1. Because the combined analysis is dominated by a few dwarf galaxies with high , any statistical fluctuation in the combined results will necessarily be associated to an excess coincident with these dwarf galaxies.
We find that the primary contributors to the deviation of the combined analysis from the null hypothesis are the ultra-faint dwarf galaxies Segue 1, Ursa Major II, and Willman 1.

sources.summary: The paper describes Ursa Major II as a disrupting dwarf spheroidal near the Orphan Stream, with similar distance and simulated tidal tails matching the stream.

quotes.quote: Very close to the great circle of the Orphan Stream and behind Complex A is the recently discovered, disrupting, dwarf spheroidal galaxy, Ursa Major II, or UMa II see also Grillmair (2006) who noted it as a stellar overdensity Zu06 . This object lies at Galactic coordinates = 152.5^ , b= 37.4^ . Its color-magnitude diagram exhibits a well-defined main sequence turn-off, from which its distance is estimated to be 30 kpc, comparable to the distance of the Orphan Stream as it fades from view. Its radial velocity has not yet been measured.

This is close to the estimated distance of the newly-discovered dwarf spheroidal galaxy UMa II, suggesting that the Orphan Stream may be physically associated with it. Fe06 have recently carried out N body simulations of the disruption of UMa II and show that its tidal tails match the observational data available on the Orphan Stream.

sources.summary: The paper describes Ursa Major II as an ultra-faint dwarf with no alpha-abundance decline, mostly metal-poor stars, and likely short star formation without Type Ia enrichment.

quotes.quote: Ursa Major II (dark blue in Figure f:vargasmetals ), which has a half-light radius mass of 7.9 ^ +5.6 _ 3.1 10^6 M _ wolf10 , also shows no evidence of a decline in [ /Fe ] . Given sufficient Type II SNe and the absence of Type Ia SNe, [ /Fe ] will eventually settle around 0.35 frebel12 . The stars in Ursa Major II are clustered around [ /Fe ] = 0.4, with all but one of the stars having [ Fe/H ] <-2.5 . The exception is a star with [ Fe/H ] = 1.1 and [ /Fe ] = 0.4; however this may be a foreground star rather than a member of the system frebel10 .
vargas13 found that Segue I and Ursa Major II do not show any low [ /Fe ] stars and, therefore, star formation likely lasted less than 100 Myr.

sources.summary: The paper treats Ursa Major II as an ultra-faint dwarf satellite in a homogeneous very metal-poor stellar-parameter sample for comparing dwarf-galaxy and Milky Way halo populations.

quotes.quote: We present a homogeneous set of accurate atmospheric parameters for a complete sample of very and extremely metal-poor stars in the dwarf spheroidal galaxies (dSphs) Sculptor, Ursa Minor, Sextans, Fornax, Bo otes I, Ursa Major II, and Leo IV.

The comparison between ultra-faint, classical dwarf spheroidal galaxies (UFDs, dSphs), and the Milky Way population offers a fantastic opportunity to probe different galaxy masses, star formation histories, levels of chemical enrichement. Both nucleosynthetic processes and galaxy formation models largely benefit from the diversity of the populations sampled that way.

We selected 36 stars in total in the classical dSphs Sculptor (Scl), Ursa Minor (UMi), Fornax (Fnx), and Sextans (Sex) and the ultra-faint dwarfs Bo otes I, Ursa Major II (UMa II), and Leo IV (Table Tab:spectra ). This sample covers the 4 Fe/H < -1.5 metallicity range.

sources.summary: The paper identifies Ursa Major II as one of the dwarf galaxies dominating indirect-detection limits, notes its small stellar sample, and shows larger J-factor uncertainty weakens dark-matter constraints.

quotes.quote: The three dwarfs from A17 that provide the strongest limits in the mass range relevant for the DM interpretation of the Galactic Center Excess (GCE) are Ursa Major II, Ursa Minor, and Draco. We first focus on Ursa Major II, which has a sample size of 20 stars. We find that increasing the uncertainty on _ 10 [ J(0.5^ )/ (GeV^2 cm^ 5 ) ] from 0.4 (which was assumed in the A17 analysis) to 1 weakens the limit by a factor of 5 -8 in the 10--100 GeV mass range for the b b annihilation channel, resulting in a limit that no longer excludes or is in tension with the regions of parameter space consistent with the GCE from .

In particular, we emphasize the importance of increasing the sample sizes for Ursa Major II, Ursa Minor, and Draco, which dominate the indirect detection limits. Our results in sec:location sec:jfac_selection suggest that measuring more stars spanning the entire spatial extent of the galaxies would be most effective at achieving more accurate estimates of their s (see bottom panel of fig:Jfacs_drop_stars ).

sources.summary: The paper identifies Ursa Major II as one of the strongest IceCube dSph constraints and the best single-source result after accounting for J-factor and declination.

quotes.quote: We have chosen to show only the 6 sources that offer the strongest constraints, namely Ursa Major II, Coma Berenices, Willman 1, Segue 2, Ursa Minor and Draco.

We choose the dSph Ursa Major II and the channel ^+ ^- for the presentation. In each simulation, a random R.A. is assigned and the Dec. is fixed to the one of Ursa Major II. With this new (R.A., Dec.) pair, we perform the analysis with the same procedure as above to derive upper limits.
Taking into account the magnitude of the J-factor and the declination at which the source is located, Ursa Major II gives the best result among all 18 sources for single-source analysis.

sources.summary: The paper models Ursa Major II as an extremely faint Milky Way dwarf with a massive dark matter halo, predicting dark-matter-annihilation gamma-ray fluxes comparable to brighter satellites.

quotes.quote: We use kinematic data from three new, nearby, extremely low-luminosity Milky Way dwarf galaxies (Ursa Major II, Willman 1, and Coma Berenices) to constrain the properties of their dark matter halos, and from these make predictions for the ray flux from annihilation of dark matter particles in these halos. We show that these 10^3 L _ dwarfs are the most dark matter dominated galaxies in the Universe, with total masses within 100 pc in excess of 10^6 M _ . Coupled with their relative proximity, their large masses imply that they should have mean ray fluxes comparable to or greater than any other known satellite galaxy of the Milky Way. Our results are robust to both variations of the inner slope of the density profile and the effect of tidal interactions.

sources.summary: The paper ranks Ursa Major 2 as a promising ultrafaint dwarf-spheroidal target but warns that tidal disturbance and large uncertainties make it less secure.

quotes.quote: Our updated analysis (compared to the results presented in 2011MNRAS.418.1526C ) still prefers UMi among all the `classical' dSph while Coma and Ursa Major 2 are the most promising `ultrafaint' dSphs (Segue I falls among the least interesting targets).

In particular, a number of authors have presented evidence that the UMa2 dSph, which occupies the top position in both the J and D factor rankings, is currently experiencing strong tidal disturbance by the Milky Way 2007MNRAS.375.1171F,2010AJ....140..138M,2013MNRAS.433.2529S . While it is not clear that this has inflated the velocity dispersion of UMa2, some caution is advisable before selecting UMa2 as a prime candidate for indirect dark matter detection surveys.
For decaying DM, Sextans appears as the brightest `classical' dSph at _ ^ D r_h /d , while UMa 2 remains the brightest `ultrafaint' target.

sources.summary: The paper identifies Ursa Major II as an ultra-faint dwarf spheroidal and a leading annihilation target, but notes that its J factor is highly uncertain because of sparse kinematics.

quotes.quote: Secondly, we find that among analysed dSphs, the ultra-faint dwarf galaxies Triangulum II and Ursa Major II are the most promising but large uncertain targets for dark matter annihilation while the classical dSph Draco is the most robust and detectable target for dark matter decay.

On the other hand, among ultra-faint dSphs, Triangulum II and Ursa Major II are the top and second largest J value, even when including classicals. However, since their kinematic sample size is limited, 1 uncertainties in these values are very much larger than those of all luminous dSphs. Therefore, all the ultra-faint dSphs, except for Canes Venatici I, have pronouncedly larger uncertainties of J than their classical counterparts.

sources.summary: The paper combines Martin and Simon classifications for Ursa Major II, keeps 15 members, finds a clear proper-motion clump, and agrees with Simon.

quotes.quote: Ursa Major II We use the catalogs of Martin_07 and, for stars not contained in that work, the catalog of Simon_07 . Both provide binary classifications. After our cuts we have 15 members. These stars form a clear clump in proper motion space supported by some stars w/o spectroscopy. Also in this case our motion agrees very well with the determination of Simon_18 .

sources.summary: The paper models Ursa Major II with an NFW profile and predicts above-threshold SKA1 radio flux, making it promising for dark-matter-annihilation searches.

quotes.quote: Here we have used the NFW profile for Draco and Ursa Major II , and the Einasto profile for Segue 1 , with the parameters of the profile chosen such that they are consistent with the kinematical observations of these galaxies.

We finally show in Figure Case_A1a_galaxy some predictions for galaxies nearer than Draco, namely, Ursa Major II and Segue 1, with B = 1.0 G , D_0 = 3 10^ 26 cm ^2 s ^ 1 , and appropriate values of . Benchmark A1a is used for illustration. While detectability is much above threshold here, a comparison with Draco tells us that Segue 1 and Ursa Major II hold high hopes for DM annihilation detection, even with larger m_ ^0_1 .

sources.summary: The paper uses Ursa Major II as a nearby ultra-faint dwarf spheroidal SKA target, emphasizing low star formation, high dark-matter content, proximity, and an assumed NFW profile.

quotes.quote: As a target for the MeV DM or PBH search in the context of SKA, we have mainly used the nearby ultra-faint dSphs such as Segue I and Ursa Major II in our work. These galaxies are appropriate for studying DM induced signals due to their low star formation rates which minimise the contribution of astrophysical processes. Their high DM contents (as inferred from their high mass-to-light ratios) and close proximity are of additional advantages.
For the other dSph Ursa major II and the globular cluster cen, we assume the Navarro-Frenk-White (NFW) DM profile following the parameterisations in references and , respectively.

sources.summary: The paper selects Ursa Major II as a northern dwarf spheroidal dark-matter target, including both 100-hour and 300-hour ASTRI Mini-Array simulated observations.

quotes.quote: In order to assess the capabilities of the ASTRI Mini-Array to search for DM in dSphs, in the context of the widely studied WIMP scenario (see Sect. indirectDM ), we consider three optimal targets observable from the Northern hemisphere: Ursa Minor (UMi), Coma Berenices (CBe), and Ursa Major II (UMa II). The targets were selected among the dSphs with the highest values of astrophysical factor, as reported in bon15b . The relevant kinematic (velocity dispersion _v , mass-to-light ratio M/L and ellipticity ) and brightness properties (half-light radius r_h and V band absolute magnitude M_V ) of the three selected targets are reported in Tab. tab:dsph .

We then simulate event lists for 100 h observations of UMi and CBe, and for both 100 h and 300 h in the case of UMa II; for this task, we assume the dSph halos to be point-like with respect to the ASTRI Mini-Array PSF of 0.1 deg.

sources.summary: The paper ranks Ursa Major II as the second most promising known dwarf spheroidal for annihilation flux and highlights it as a future ACT target.

quotes.quote: The most promising source is Segue 1, followed by Ursa Major II, Ursa Minor, Draco, and Carina.

Current ACT observations of dSph's are beginning to directly constrain DM models, and future longer exposure time observations of additional dSph's (in particular Segue 1 and Ursa Major II) with a lower threshold energy hold great potential.
According to this analysis, the recently discovered dSph Segue 1 should be the brightest of the known dSph's, followed by Ursa Major II, Ursa Minor, Draco, and Carina.

sources.summary: The paper includes Ursa Major II in the ultra-faint sample, finding high central J values and pairing it with Segue 1 as a comparable annihilation target.

quotes.quote: For Coma Berenices, Canes Venatici I, Canes Venatici II, Leo IV, Leo T, Ursa Major I and Ursa Major II, we utilize the Keck/Deimos velocity data of simon07 , generously provided by Marla Geha (private communication).
Among the ultra-faint dwarf galaxies, Segue 1 and Ursa Major II have central J values that are higher then those of any of the classical dwarfs.
Consider the pairs Draco/Ursa Minor (classical) and Segue 1/Ursa Major II (ultra-faint).

sources.summary: The paper groups UMa II with ultra-faint dwarfs showing low Sr and Ba ratios, subsolar Sr/Ba, and abundance behavior distinct from classical dwarf spheroidals.

quotes.quote: Our three UFDs, that is Bo otes I, UMa II, and Leo IV, are depleted in Sr and Ba relative to Fe and Mg, with very similar ratios of [ Sr/Mg ] 1.3 and [ Ba/Mg ] 1 on the entire range of their Mg abundances.

In the two UFDs, Bo otes I and UMa II, all the stars are depleted in Sr and Ba relative to Fe, with very similar ratios of [ Sr/Fe ] 1.1 and [ Ba/Fe ] 0.75 on the entire range of metallicity (Fig. Fig:sr_ba ).
Subsolar Sr/Ba ratios of the stars in Bo otes I and UMa II may indicate a common origin of Sr and Ba in the classical main -process, though the statistics of Sr/Ba measurements is poor.

sources.summary: The paper finds Ursa Major II has the largest ultra-faint dSph extension and one of the strongest changes between point-source and extended dark-matter limits.

quotes.quote: In particular, Sculptor among the classical dSphs and Ursa Major II among the ultra-faint ones show the largest extensions, 0.65 ^ and 0.74 ^ respectively.

In particular, the difference between using J_ tot vs J_ 05 is more relevant for the dSphs Ursa Major II, Sculptor, Ursa Minor, Coma Berenice, Bootes I, Fornax, Tucana III and Reticulum II, which are the dSphs with the largest _ 68 (see Tab. tab:jfactors ) and for which the difference between is consequently the most significant -- the ratio of in the Ext over PS case attains at least 0.55 for Ursa Major II that indeed is the source with the largest DM template (see Tab. tab:jfactors ).
For most dSphs, the ratio is between 1.0 and 1.3 at low DM masses, and increases up to 2.0 -- 3.0 for masses between 100 GeV -- 1 TeV, as for e.g. Sculptor, Ursa Minor, Fornax and Ursa Major II.

gmw_00001846.name: Coma Berenices
gmw_00001846.overview: Coma Berenices is an ultra-faint dwarf spheroidal galaxy [arXiv:1110.1086, arXiv:1708.04858] located approximately 44 kpc away [arXiv:1110.1086, arXiv:0902.4230]. It features an irregular, extended shape [arXiv:0902.4230, arXiv:astro-ph/0608448] and is dominated by an ancient, metal-poor stellar population [arXiv:1310.0824, arXiv:astro-ph/0608448].

sources.summary: The paper uses Coma Berenices/ComBer as a typical ultra-faint dwarf example, modeling its half-light radius, distance, density parameters, and LSST detectability at 44 and 400 kpc.

quotes.quote: To illustrate how our simulations can guide the data analysis for study of UFDs, we carry out mock observations for two example dwarf galaxies: Boo II M_ V =-2.7 0.9 , l=353^ .7 , b=68^ .8 ; martin08a and Coma Berenices ComBer, M_ V =-3.8 0.6 l=241^ .9 , b=83^ .6 ; munoz10a . The former is one of the smallest and faintest of the UFDs, while the latter is rather ``typical''.

For the case of ComBer, the results of martin08a give a half-light radius of 6.0 0.6 and a distance of 44 kpc, which results in _ 0 5 , R_ 1/2 =0.05 and N_ T =3310 . This combination of parameters gives _ R_ 1/2 20 and , _ L 50 , an improved value as compared to Boo II. In the case of ComBer, the uncertainties reported by martin08a in their measured half-light radii are somewhat lower that those from our simulations. To estimate their errors, these authors assumed that the likelihood function is well-described by a Gaussian distribution near its maximum, an assumption that probably breaks for the fainter objects.

To assess the expected performance of LSST we carry out mock observations of the UFD galaxy ComBer, placed at two different distances: its true distance of 44 kpc and farther away at 400 kpc. We estimate the number of UFD stars, N_ dwarf , detectable down to the full co-added LSST survey magnitude limit using a theoretical luminosity function typical of the UFDs so far discovered: a 13 Gyr old population with overall metallicity of [ Fe/H ] =-2.27 girardi04a , assuming a chabrier01a initial mass function.

sources.summary: The paper treats Coma Berenices dSph as one of four H.E.S.S.-observed dwarf spheroidals in a gamma-ray-line dark matter search, reporting no significant excess and using the galaxies to derive flux and cross-section constraints.

quotes.quote: Data were collected during observations from 2006-2012 for four dwarf galaxies: Fornax dSph, Coma Berenices dSph, Sculptor dSph and Carina dSph. Observations were carried out with the source offset from the centre of the field of view, such that the gamma-ray-like cosmic-ray background flux can be simultaneously measured.

There is no significant deviation from the expected background observed at the locations of the four dSphs. Figure fig1 shows the significance maps for each dSph and in Figure fig2 the significance distributions across the field of view are shown. The fitted significance distributions are compatible with a Gaussian with a centre of zero.

Limits on DM flux The calculated upper limits on the integrated flux in the range 5 _ E of M_ at the 95 confidence level (CL) are shown in Figure figflux as a function of M_ . The differences in flux limits from the four dwarf galaxies arise from the various observation conditions and number of hours of livetime for each galaxy. Constraints on the WIMP cross-section Figure figsigv shows the 95 CL constraints on the velocity weighted WIMP self-annihilation cross-section ( < v> ) versus M_ for the individual dSphs as well as a combined result for the four galaxies.

sources.summary: Deep HST infrared imaging of Coma Berenices probes its IMF to very low stellar masses and finds slopes broadly comparable to Milky Way IMF parametrizations.

quotes.quote: We use deep HST WFC3/IR imaging to study the Initial Mass Function (IMF) of the ultra faint dwarf galaxy Coma Berenices (Com Ber). Our observations reach the lowest stellar mass ever probed in a resolved galaxy, with 50 completeness at 0.17 M _ . Unresolved background galaxies however limit our purity below 0.23 M _ . If modeled with a single power law, we find that the IMF slope is 1.45^ +0.29 _ 0.3 (68 credible intervals), compared to a Milky Way value of 2.3 . For a broken power law, we obtain a low-mass slope of 1.18_ 0.33 ^ +0.49 , a high-mass slope of 1.88_ 0.49 ^ +0.43 and a break mass of 0.57_ 0.08 ^ +0.12 M _ , compared to 1.3 , 2.3 and 0.5 M _ for a Kroupa IMF.

ComBer is a very faint system, with M_V 3.8 mag. Its mean metallicity is [ Fe/H ] =-2.6 dex, with a spread of about 1 dex 2014ApJ...796...91B . From optical data its distance has been determined to be 43 kpc 2014ApJ...796...91B .

sources.summary: The paper treats Coma Berenices as an ultra-faint dwarf with old, metal-poor outer members, finding abundance patterns that favor SN Ia enrichment and relocation of outer stars after central formation.

quotes.quote: Coma Berenices (Com Ber) is a similar UFD to Tuc II, with an old age from stellar isochrones, low metallicity from spectroscopic analyses, and candidate members located at large half light radii, > 4 R _ h e.g., Frebel2012, Brown2014, McVenn2020 . Three stars in Com Ber have surprisingly low heavy element abundances; in particular, their s-process abundances (Y, Sr, Ba, Ce) are all lower than known stars of similar metallicity in the MW halo ( [ Fe/H ] 2.5 ; Frebel10 ). This unique chemical fingerprint has been interpreted as a result of enrichment by a single metal-poor supernova event ( >20 M _ ), with little to no later chemical evolution.

The [ /Fe ] abundances in Com Ber suggest this UFD has been enriched in iron from SN Ia. This is at odds with the previous suggestion that Com Ber is a fossil relic enriched by only one early core-collapse SN (i.e., the ``one-shot" model). A star formation model that includes contributions from SN Ia is supported by an elevated Ni in the least metal-poor star, possibly due to enrichment from sub- M_ Ch mass white dwarfs. The [ Ba/H ] ratios vary from star to star in Com Ber, which may be more consistent with inhomogeneous mixing of AGB yields in the gas during star formation. Finally, these conclusions include the outermost stars, which suggests those stars moved to their outer locations after formation in the central regions.

sources.summary: The paper describes Coma as a very faint Milky Way dwarf spheroidal with measured position, distance, shape, old metal-poor population, low metallicity, and variable-star distance indicators.

quotes.quote: The Coma galaxy ( R.A. = 12^ h 26^ m 59.0^ s , DEC=+23^ 54^ 15^ '' , J2000.0, l =241.9 ^ , b =83.6 ^ , B07), located at a heliocentric distance of 44 4 kpc with a position angle of 120^ and an absolute magnitude of M_V = -3.6 0.6 mag, is among the faintest of the SDSS new discoveries. It has half light radii: r_h (Plummer) = 5.0 arcmin, and r_h (exponential) = 5.9 arcmin, respectively, and an irregular and extended shape (B07). The i , g-i CMD obtained by B07 goes down to i 25 mag, is consistent with a single, old stellar population of metallicity [ Fe/H ] 2 , and shows a poorly populated horizontal branch (HB).

Metallicities of radial velocity members of the Coma dSph were obtained by sg07 and confirm the low metal abundance of the galaxy. These metallicities were re-evaluated by ky08 , who derived for the galaxy an average metallicity Fe/H = -2.53 0.05 dex with dispersion _ Fe/H =0.45 dex. In this Letter we present the first study of the variable stars in the Coma dSph, which led us to identify two RR Lyrae stars and one short period variable in the galaxy. We provide multiband light curves and properties of the variables, and use the magnitude of the HB stars to measure the distance to Coma.

sources.summary: The paper includes Coma Berenices in the joint dwarf-spheroidal likelihood analysis, finds no significant gamma-ray signal, and notes that its lack of excess reduces the combined-analysis deviation.

quotes.quote: Thus, we are left with a subset of 15 dwarf spheroidal galaxies as input to the joint likelihood analysis: Bootes I, Canes Venatici II, Carina, Coma Berenices, Draco, Fornax, Hercules, Leo II, Leo IV, Sculptor, Segue 1, Sextans, Ursa Major II, Ursa Minor, and Willman 1. No significant signal is found for any of the spectra tested in the combined analysis, prompting us to calculate 95 CL constraints on using the delta-log-likelihood procedure described above ( fig4 ).

The sensitivity of the combined analysis depends most heavily on the observations of the dwarf galaxies with the largest . Specifically, the results of the combined analysis are dominated by Coma Berenices, Draco, Segue 1, Ursa Major II, Ursa Minor, and Willman 1. Because the combined analysis is dominated by a few dwarf galaxies with high , any statistical fluctuation in the combined results will necessarily be associated to an excess coincident with these dwarf galaxies.
On the other hand, the lack of any ray excess coincident with Coma Berenices, Draco, or Ursa Minor decreases the significance of the deviation in the combined analysis.

sources.summary: The paper compares Coma Berenices to 200 Myr models, finding its metallicity observations fit well and suggesting a short history influenced by Type Ia supernova enrichment.

quotes.quote: In the vargas13 sample, nine stars are observed in Coma Berenices, of which seven show [ Fe/H ] <-2.5 , with six of these clustering around [ /Fe ] 0.8 . The other two stars show [ Fe/H ] <-2 and [ /Fe ] 0.2. This could be explained by a few high-mass stars affecting the system in the first 100 Myr, followed by a small number of Type Ia SNe, after which star formation quickly ends. The observed stars in Coma Berenices (dark green in f:vargasmetals ) have a mean [ Fe/H ] of 2.7 and we therefore compare it to our 200 Myr plot in Figure f:vargasmetals , which has mean [ Fe/H ] = 2.9 . The metallicity observations fit well, suggesting that Coma Berenices may have formed stars for 200 Myr.
The luminosity of 3700 L _ suggests 5-10 times more star formation than for our model if this is the case.

sources.summary: The paper identifies Coma Berenices as a strong low-mass dwarf-spheroidal target whose ranking weakens after profiling because of its larger J-factor uncertainty.

quotes.quote: As an example, the factor of Segue I is higher than the factor of Coma Berenices, but the fact that the background underestimates the counts at the former whereas for Coma Berenices it coincides with the observed value causes the bound from the latter to be stronger. We find that, at low DM masses, the four most constraining dSphs according to case 1 are: Willman 1, Coma Berenices, Segue I and Draco. This ranking is determined by a subtle interplay between background estimate and factor central value, which both enter in the determination of the Poisson term of the likelihood.

In this case, Coma Berenices gives a weaker limit than Sculptor -- while for case 1 it was the opposite. This can be understood by looking at the uncertainty on _ 10 J , which is larger for Coma Berenices, enabling more freedom in adjusting the factor preferred value for this dSph during the profiling procedure. The results of the stacking procedure with profiling over factor, case 3 , is depicted by the black solid line:Draco dominates by far the combined constraint at all masses, additionally, at low masses, its interplay with Sculptor and Coma Berenices becomes important.

sources.summary: Coma Berenices is included among the nearby HST ultra-faint dwarf targets; the paper finds the surveyed dwarfs have old, metal-poor populations and broadly synchronized histories.

quotes.quote: Our survey observed six UFD galaxies using two cameras on HST : the Advanced Camera for Surveys (ACS) and the Wide Field Camera 3 (WFC3). We used the F606W (broad V ) and F814W ( I ) filters on each camera, with ACS surveying an area centered on each galaxy ( 202^ 202^ per tile), and WFC3 observing in parallel in the galaxy outskirts ( 162^ 162^ per tile). For the nearby ( < 100 kpc) satellites in the sample (Bootes I, Coma Berenices, Ursa Major I), we surveyed a relatively wide area (5--12 tiles in each camera) to a shallow depth (2 or 3 orbits, faint limit of V 28 mag).

It is clear that the CMD of each UFD looks like the CMD of an ancient globular cluster, and that each is dominated by an old (age > 12 Gyr) population of metal-poor ( [ Fe/H ] < -2 ) stars. A prominent blue straggler sequence (BSS) is present in each galaxy -- ubiquitous in old populations.

sources.summary: The paper forecasts HAWC sensitivity to dark-matter annihilation in Coma Berenices, uses an NFW halo profile, and finds its favorable declination makes sensitivity similar to Draco.

quotes.quote: In this work, we perform a forecast of the HAWC sensitivity to signatures of dark matter annihilation in the Coma Berenices dwarf galaxy, the Draco dwarf galaxy, the Segue 1 dwarf galaxy, the M31 galaxy, the Virgo galaxy cluster, and the Galactic center (GC). The dwarf galaxies provide sources with low gamma-ray backgrounds over a range of declinations and dark matter densities.
For the dwarf galaxies Draco and Coma Berenices, the dark matter profiles are done using an NFW profile with parameters given by  .

Figure  HAWCdwarfs demonstrates the HAWC sensitivity to dark matter annihilation in single dwarf spheroidal galaxies. While the Draco dwarf galaxy has a dark matter J factor that is larger than Coma Berenices by a factor of 4 , Coma Berenices has a declination culminating closer to the zenith at Sierra Negra than Draco (see Table  haloparams ). Therefore, the sensitivity to dark matter annihilations in Coma Berenices is similar to that from Draco, indicating the importance of observational losses due to zenith angle.

sources.summary: The paper describes Coma Berenices as an ultra-faint dwarf whose single RRab star leads to a discrepant RR Lyrae distance, while CMD fitting gives another distance under higher extinction.

quotes.quote: Coma Berenices is an ultra-faint dwarf galaxy in the V I sample with a single RRab variable star musella_pulsating_2009 . They report an RR Lyrae-based distance of =18.13 0.08 , while also noting that the RR Lyrae appears `over-luminous' by 0.2 mag compared to the mean HB magnitude. This can explain a large part of the 2.7 discrepancy with our distance =17.63 0.16 . Coma Berenices is too faint for an HB or TRGB distance determination. brown2014 fit isochrones to deep HST-based CMDs and find =17.95 0.06 , though with an extinction that is 3 times larger than schlafly2011 .

sources.summary: The paper identifies Coma Berenices as a new probable dwarf galaxy satellite with an old metal-poor population, 44 kpc distance, irregular extended shape, and a roughly 70 pc half-light radius.

quotes.quote: The first row of Figure  fig:objsa refers to a satellite in Coma Berenices (henceforth Com), the second to a satellite in Canes Venatici (henceforth CVn II), the third to a probable globular cluster (henceforth Segue 1), the fourth to a satellite in Hercules (henceforth Her) and the fifth to a satellite in Leo (henceforth Leo IV)  There is already a dwarf galaxy known in the constellation of Canes Venatici  Zu06a , and two known in Leo. Leo III is an alternative name for the Leo dwarf irregular, also called Leo A  Be00 . .

Based on their sizes and shapes, our working hypothesis is that Com, CVn II, Her, and Leo IV are new dwarf galaxies, whilst Segue 1 is an extended globular cluster. Com is located at a heliocentric distance of 44 4 kpc. It has a half-light radius of 70 pc, although this may be an underestimate given its irregular and extended shape. Its CMD is consistent with that of a single, old stellar population of metallicity [ Fe/H ] 2 .

sources.summary: The paper models Coma Berenices as an extremely faint Milky Way dwarf with a massive dark matter halo, predicting dark-matter-annihilation gamma-ray fluxes comparable to brighter satellites.

quotes.quote: We use kinematic data from three new, nearby, extremely low-luminosity Milky Way dwarf galaxies (Ursa Major II, Willman 1, and Coma Berenices) to constrain the properties of their dark matter halos, and from these make predictions for the ray flux from annihilation of dark matter particles in these halos. We show that these 10^3 L _ dwarfs are the most dark matter dominated galaxies in the Universe, with total masses within 100 pc in excess of 10^6 M _ . Coupled with their relative proximity, their large masses imply that they should have mean ray fluxes comparable to or greater than any other known satellite galaxy of the Milky Way. Our results are robust to both variations of the inner slope of the density profile and the effect of tidal interactions.

sources.summary: The paper reports a metallicity lag and suppressed alpha abundances in Coma Berenices, arguing the two-burst model needs more second-burst stars or first-burst enrichment.

quotes.quote: Coma Berenices shows a lag of 0.4 dex in [ Fe/H ] for the best-fitting model compared to the observations. As shown in Fig. f:afefeh , two of the nine stars for which [ /Fe ] is available show suppressed [ /Fe ] abundances, suggesting that they belong to the second burst. Using the same method as for Canes Venatici II, this is reasonably unlikely ( p=0.05 ) given that brown14 determine that 96 of the stars belong to the first burst. If the two-burst model is to explain Coma Berenices and Canes Venatici II, it requires more stars in the second burst, or enrichment within the first burst.

sources.summary: The paper revises the Coma Berenices spectroscopic membership sample, removes one RR Lyrae with variable velocities, and says the removals have little impact because the affected stars have large errors.

quotes.quote: We have made slight membership changes to the Simon2007ApJ...670..313S data for the following dSphs: Canes Venatici I, Coma Berenices, Leo IV, Ursa Major I, and Ursa Major II. We have removed RR Lyrae from the following (identified after the original spectroscopic analysis): Canes Venatici I 5 RR Lyrae identified in Kuehn2008ApJ...674L..81K , Coma Berenices 1 star; Musella2009ApJ...695L..83M , Leo IV 1 star; Moretti2009ApJ...699L.125M , and Ursa Major I 3 stars; Garofalo2013ApJ...767...62G . RR Lyrae are pulsating stars with variable velocity; these stars are dSph members, however, without additional phase information we cannot determine the star's bulk velocity. In Ursa Major II, a foreground dwarf has been removed that was identified in follow-up high-resolution spectroscopy analysis Frebel2010ApJ...708..560F . These removals do not have a large impact as the removed stars tend to have large error bars.

sources.summary: The paper uses Simon membership classifications for Coma Berenices I, keeps 17 members, finds a clear proper-motion clump, and agrees with Simon proper motion.

quotes.quote: Coma Berenices I We use the catalog of Simon_07 with its binary membership classifications. After all cuts this leads to 17 members in our final selection. They form a clear clump in proper motion space supported by a few stars without spectroscopic information. Our proper motion agrees within 1 with the motion of Simon_18 .

sources.summary: The paper selects Coma Berenices as a high-factor northern dwarf spheroidal target for ASTRI Mini-Array dark-matter searches and simulated 100-hour observations.

quotes.quote: In order to assess the capabilities of the ASTRI Mini-Array to search for DM in dSphs, in the context of the widely studied WIMP scenario (see Sect. indirectDM ), we consider three optimal targets observable from the Northern hemisphere: Ursa Minor (UMi), Coma Berenices (CBe), and Ursa Major II (UMa II). The targets were selected among the dSphs with the highest values of astrophysical factor, as reported in bon15b . The relevant kinematic (velocity dispersion _v , mass-to-light ratio M/L and ellipticity ) and brightness properties (half-light radius r_h and V band absolute magnitude M_V ) of the three selected targets are reported in Tab. tab:dsph .

We then simulate event lists for 100 h observations of UMi and CBe, and for both 100 h and 300 h in the case of UMa II; for this task, we assume the dSph halos to be point-like with respect to the ASTRI Mini-Array PSF of 0.1 deg.

sources.summary: The paper treats Coma Berenices as an extended dSph whose J-factor uncertainty strongly affects extended-source versus point-source dark-matter limits.

quotes.quote: In particular, the difference between using J_ tot vs J_ 05 is more relevant for the dSphs Ursa Major II, Sculptor, Ursa Minor, Coma Berenice, Bootes I, Fornax, Tucana III and Reticulum II, which are the dSphs with the largest _ 68 (see Tab. tab:jfactors ) and for which the difference between is consequently the most significant -- the ratio of in the Ext over PS case attains at least 0.55 for Ursa Major II that indeed is the source with the largest DM template (see Tab. tab:jfactors ).

However, the ranking of the dSphs with the largest ratio between the Ext and PS scenarios is driven mainly by the objects for which the difference of _ J is the largest, i.e. Reticulum II and Coma Berenices.
It might seem surprising that for objects such as Reticulum II or Coma Berenices the change from _ J_ 05 to _ J_ tot is much more important than the change in the average observed J factor.

gmw_00001847.name: HE0512-3329
gmw_00001847.overview: HE0512-3329 is a gravitationally lensed quasar at a redshift of z = 1.58 [arXiv:astro-ph/0606309]. It is doubly imaged with a separation of 0.644 arcseconds [arXiv:astro-ph/0606309, arXiv:0804.2668], and its lensed components are affected by both differential extinction and microlensing [arXiv:astro-ph/0606309, arXiv:0804.2668].

sources.summary: The appendix describes HE 0512--3329 as a probable doubly imaged lensed quasar at z=1.58, with wavelength-dependent image flux ratios, differential reddening, microlensing, and an unresolved acquisition image.

quotes.quote: HE 0512--3329 was discovered as a probable gravitationally lensed quasar with the Space Telescope Imaging Spectrograph (STIS). It is a doubly imaged QSO with a source redshift of z = 1.58 \ and an image separation of 0''.644. The flux ratio A B of the lensed images shows a strong dependence on wavelength. In the R and I bands, A is brighter than B by about 0.45 mag while the two are almost equal in the band B. For smaller wavelengths, especially close to the limit near 2000 , B becomes much brighter than A by about 1.3 mag.

A natural explanation for this effect is differential reddening caused by different extinction effects in the two lines of sight greggetal00 . Microlensing by stars and other compact object in the lensing galaxy also plays a role in this source wucknitzetal03 . Unfortunately, the small separation does not allow to us to distinguish component A and B on the ISAAC spectrum.

sources.summary: The paper treats HE0512-3329 as a lens candidate with separated quasar images, undetected lensing galaxy, metal absorption, extinction, and microlensing effects relevant to MOND modeling.

quotes.quote: HE0512-3329 The system HE0512-3329 was discovered as a gravitational lens candidate in the course of a snapshot survey with the Space Telescope Imaging Spectrograph (STIS), with the images of the quasar source being separated by 0.644 and references therein microextinction . Although the lensing galaxy has not been detected yet, measurements of strong metal absorption lines at redshift z = 0.93 , identified in the integrated spectrum, hint towards a damped Ly system intervening at this redshift. Analyzing separate spectra of both image components, point out that both differential extinction and microlensing effects significantly contribute to the spectral differences and that one cannot be analyzed without taking into account the other. For lens modeling purposes, the observed flux ratio can therefore only be used after correcting for both effects. Thus, the large discrepancy between predicted and observed flux ratio might be a consequence of neglecting the above mentioned effects, rather than being intrinsic to MOND/TeVeS.

sources.summary: The paper gives HE0512 3329 one of the first black-hole mass estimates, places it among lower-luminosity lensed quasars, and includes it in the new Balmer-line mass expansion.

quotes.quote: We obtain M _ BH using the single-epoch method from the H and/or H broad emission lines for 14 lensed quasars, including the first estimates for QJ0158 4325, HE0512 3329 and WFI2026 4536. The masses are typical of non-lensed quasars of similar luminosity, and the implied Eddington ratios are typical. We have increased the sample of lenses with estimates of M _ BH by 60 .

There are the first M _ BH estimate obtained for the systems QJ0158 4325 (log _ 10 ( M _ BH /M _ ) = 8.05 0.58, 8.51 0.25 and 8.32 0.46 for C IV , Mg II and H , respectively), HE0512 3329 (log _ 10 ( M _ BH /M _ ) = 8.14 0.25) and WFI2026 4536 (log _ 10 ( M _ BH /M _ ) = 8.28 0.25 and 7.83 0.35, for H and H , respectively). In particular, we were able to obtain estimates for the lower luminosity systems QJ0158 4325, SDSS0924+0219, HE0512 3329 and HE0047 1756 (from 10 ^ 44 to 10 ^ 46.5 ).

gmw_00001848.name: SDSS0924+0219
gmw_00001848.overview: SDSS0924+0219 is a radio-quiet quasar at redshift 1.524 that is quadruply lensed by a foreground early-type galaxy [arXiv:2005.13612, arXiv:2305.05173]. The host galaxy features a compact star-forming disk [arXiv:2005.13612] and clumpy, kiloparsec-scale thermal dust emission [arXiv:2009.01277].

sources.summary: The paper observes SDSS J0924+0219 as a z=1.524 radio-quiet quadruply lensed quasar, finding extended radio/CO emission from a compact star-forming disk and flux ratios consistent with microlensing.

quotes.quote: The quasar SDSS J0924+0219 SDSS J0924+0219 was discovered by Inada2003 as part of the Sloan Digital Sky Survey Quasar Lens Search (SQLS) and consists of a radio-quiet quasar at z = 1.524 quadruply imaged by an early-type galaxy at z=0.394 Eigenbrod2006COSMOGRAIL:Rings . It was selected for observation due to an unusually large flux anomaly: it is a fold-configuration lens, but the pair of merging images, A and D in Fig. fig1 , has a brightness ratio in the optical waveband of 12:1 Inada2003 . Spectroscopy using the Hubble Space Telescope Keeton2006 revealed the presence of flux anomaly in the broad emission line flux ratios, with the continuum and broad line emission flux ratios in the merging images being 19 and 10, respectively.

In the VLA map of SDSS J0924+0219 (Fig. fig1 ) we detect all four lensed images in the system, and it is clear that the ratio of the fluxes in the A and D images is less than the value of 12 which is measured in the optical waveband, but that the flux densities of the two images do not look equal.

The structure is of physical dimension approximately 100--300 mas ( 850 -2500 pc) , consistent with a compact star-forming disk in the centre of the host galaxy of SDSS 0924+0219. We have performed simple modelling of the radio data (which is all that is warranted given the signal-to-noise and resolution of the data) and derive inconsistency with a point source, and a preferred size of about 100 mas , consistent with the size of the CO-line source. From Fig. vlaalma we see that the lensed structures in the radio and CO lines are consistent with each other. Although not conclusive, the most natural explanation is therefore that the two components of the source originate from a region which is of the same size and physical origin.

sources.summary: The paper treats SDSS J0924+0219 as a lensed quasar system, measuring the lensing galaxy redshift, finding quasar spectral-line variability, and resolving Einstein rings from the quasar host and a bluer source.

quotes.quote: We measure here the redshift of the lensing galaxy, we show the spectral variability of the source, and we resolve the lensed host galaxy of the source. We present our VLT/FORS1 deep spectroscopic observations of the lensed quasar , as well as archival HST/NICMOS and ACS images of the same object. The two-epoch spectra, obtained in the Multi Object Spectroscopy (MOS) mode, allow for very accurate flux calibration and spatial deconvolution. This strategy provides spectra for the lensing galaxy and for the quasar images A and B, free of any mutual light contamination.

We determine the redshift of the lensing galaxy in : = 0.394 0.001 . Only slight spectral variability is seen in the continuum of quasar images A and B, while the C III ] , Mg II and Fe II emission lines display obvious changes. The flux ratio between the quasar images A and B is the same in the emission lines and in the continuum. One of the Einstein rings found using deconvolution corresponds to the lensed quasar host galaxy at z=1.524 and a second bluer one, is the image either of a star-forming region in the host galaxy, or of another unrelated lower redshift object.

sources.summary: The paper models the lens galaxy toward SDSS J0924+0219, finding a significant disk-like component and evidence that Object L and a southern extension are part of a bar, not the flux-ratio perturber.

quotes.quote: With our adaptive optics observations we find a luminous object, Object L, located 0.3 \ to the North of the lens galaxy, but we show that it can not be responsible for the anomalous flux ratios. Object L as well as a luminous extension of the lens galaxy to the South are seen in the archival HST/ACS image in the F814W filter. This suggests that Object L is part of a bar in the lens galaxy, as also supported by the presence of a significant disk component in the light profile of the lens galaxy. Finally, we do not find evidence for any other luminous substructure that may explain the quasar images flux ratios.

We find evidence in the HST ACS/F814W images that Object L could be part of a bar in the lensing galaxy, oriented about along the N-S axis. This is consistent with our finding that the light profile of the lens galaxy can only be modeled with a 2-components distribution that includes a significant disk. We find no compact and luminous substructure that explains the flux ratio anomaly in .

sources.summary: The paper reconstructs SDSS J0924+0219's lensed quasar host, finding thermal dust continuum, extended clumpy kiloparsec-scale dust, and compact molecular gas relative to dust.

quotes.quote: SDSS J0924+0219 SDSS J0924+0219 is a radio-quiet quasar at z_ s = 1.525 that is lensed by a galaxy at z_ l =0.39 Inada:2003,Hewett:2010 . This lens system was discovered at optical wavelengths and has a characteristic fold-configuration. Observations with ALMA at 358 GHz, which also targeted the CO (8--7) line, were taken as part of our own observing programme. The continuum emission from SDSS J0924+0219 is detected with a total flux density of 9.5 mJy at 358 GHz, consistent with the expectations for thermal dust emission from SED fitting (see Fig. fig:seds1 ). We achieve an rms noise of 28 Jy beam ^ 1 with natural weighting of the visibilities.
For SDSS J0924+0219 and WFI J2033 4723, the dust emission is extended and resolved into clumpy features on kpc-scales.
For the four objects where we observe one of these lines (SDSS J0924+0219, H1413+117, WFI J2026 4536 and WFI J2033 4723), the molecular gas is a similar size or more compact than the dust continuum.

sources.summary: SDSS0924+0219 has previous Mg II estimates, new Balmer-line estimates, low luminosity, the lowest average black-hole mass in the discussed set, and the smallest inferred accretion disk size.

quotes.quote: The systems HE0047 1756, HE0435 1223, SDSS0924+0219, SDSS1226 0006, LBQS1333+0113, Q1355 2257 and WFI2033 4723 have previous estimates of M _ BH using the Mg II emission lines ( 2006Peng,2012Sluse, 2017DingI ) which we compare to our Balmer lines estimates in Figure fig:halphavsmgii . Lensed quasars that have one or both M _ BH estimates presented in this work are shown in color. In general, our estimates are well correlated after we apply the offset of 2016Mejiarestrepo (0.16 dex for M _ BH measured with H , and 0.25 dex using Mg II ).

In particular, we were able to obtain estimates for the lower luminosity systems QJ0158 4325, SDSS0924+0219, HE0512 3329 and HE0047 1756 (from 10 ^ 44 to 10 ^ 46.5 ). The systems QJ0158 4325 and SDSS0924+0219 have the lowest luminosities (log _ 10 ( L _ ref ) < 44.60 L _ ), and the latter has the lowest M _ BH , log _ 10 ( M _ BH /M _ ) = 7.43 0.05 (this is the average of the H and H estimates).

SDSS0924+0219 has the smallest accretion disk size (mean value between H and H emission line of r_s = 10^ 14.78 2.62 cm, an error in dex of 5.99. These spectra had very low signal-to-noise ( 5.9 and 3.9 in H and H lines, respectively).

sources.summary: The paper describes SDSS J0924+0219 as a cross-like lens with an enormous, robust flux-ratio anomaly and no current evidence for a spiral lens.

quotes.quote: SDSS J0924+0219 The lens SDSS J0924+0219 is not a typical cusp configuration but rather more like a cross configuration. Nevertheless, it does have what seems to be an enormous flux ratio anomaly. The anomaly appears as such a strong suppression of one of the negative-parity images that the system was initially thought to have just three images, and the fourth image (D) was identified only after subtraction of the three main images and the lens galaxy (Inada et al. \ 2003). The fact that SDSS J0924+0219 is not a tight cusp configuration means that smooth potentials can produce a fairly broad distribution of values (see joint ). Nevertheless, the observed value of is so large that it still lies well outside the predicted distribution.

In other words, the anomaly in SDSS J0924+0219 is so strong that it is identified by the cusp relation analysis even though the lens is not a proper cusp configuration. However, the strength of the anomaly would require significant structure, which would probably affect not just image D but all four images; and there is no current evidence that the lens is a spiral (Inada et al. \ 2003).

sources.summary: The paper treats SDSS J0924+0219 as a lens whose smooth dark matter fraction agrees with both a Salpeter IMF and the paper's calibrated Salpeter fundamental plane.

quotes.quote: Using optical data, Bate et al. (2011) have determined projected smooth (dark) matter fractions for the systems Q2237+0305, MG 0414+534 and SDSS J0924+0219. The limits for the first two of these are quite broad, but for SDSS J0924+0219 they obtain a projected smooth (dark) matter fraction at the Einstein ring, 1.97 r_e of 80 10 . The dark matter fraction at this radius for our fiducial lens is found from Figure 4 to be 82 . The prediction for a Salpeter IMF stellar mass fundamental plane can be found by dividing the stellar fraction in Figure 4 by our calibration factor F = 1.229 , yielding a dark matter fraction of 85 . The predictions for a lens with a proxy dispersion of 214 km/s and an effective radius of 6.94 kpc are, by coincidence, identical to those for our fiducial lens.
The Bate et al. (2011) results for SDSS J0924+0219 are therfore consistent both with a Salpeter IMF and with our calibrated Salpeter IMF.

sources.summary: The paper describes J0924+0219 as a quadruply lensed SDSS quasar with known source and lens redshifts, adding a first deflector velocity dispersion.

quotes.quote: J0924+0219 This quadruply lensed quasar was discovered using Sloan Digital Sky Survey SDSS; SDSS_survey data. Inada_2003_J0924_discovery first reported this quad along with the measured source redshift \ =1.524 , while Ofek_2006_J0924_zd and Eigenbrod_2006_J0924 measured the lens redshift, \ =0.393 . Time-delay data between the two brightest images is available Millon_2020_Time_delay_data . Lens modeling is presented by Eigenbrod_2006_J0924 using HST imaging and recently by Geoff_2021_J0924_AO_modeling using AO-based imaging.

Made the first measurements of the stellar velocity dispersions for six of the eight lensing galaxies in our sample. The new velocity dispersion measurements were made for the deflectors in B0445+123, B0631+519, J0659+1629, J0818-2613, J0924+0219 and J1817+2729 systems.

sources.summary: The paper applies external-convergence methods to the SDSS J0924+0219 strong-lens field, finding a fairly typical line of sight and noting the system is a quadruply lensed quasar.

quotes.quote: We apply these techniques to the SDSS J0924+0219 strong lens field. We infer the relative density of the SDSS J0924+0219 field by computing weighted number counts for all galaxies in the field, and comparing to weighted number counts computed for a large number of fields in a reference survey. We then compute weighted number counts in the Millennium Simulation and compare these results to infer the external convergence of the lens field. Our results show the SDSS J0924+0219 field is a fairly typical line of sight, with median _ ext = -0.012 and standard deviation _ = 0.028 .
The Lens Field SDSS J0924+0219 is a quadruply lensed quasar first reported in j0924_discovery . The quasar itself is at redshift z = 1.523 , while the lensing galaxy is located at z = 0.384 lens_z .

sources.summary: SDSS J0924+0219 shows weak differential microlensing without clear line deformation in VLT-FORS spectra, while ACS anomalies can be explained by microlensing simulations.

quotes.quote: (n) SDSS J0924+0219 ( z_l = 0.394 , z_s = 1.524 ): The VLT-FORS spectra of images A and B , obtained in Jan. and Feb. 2005 by , show only a weak differential ML between the continuum and the emission lines ( and ) but no clear ML deformation of the lines (Fig.  fig:MmD ). The spectral ratio is flat over the spectral range of our spectra. On the other hand, identified flux ratios in disagreement with macro model predictions in both the continuum and emission lines ( , and ) based on low resolution spectra obtained with the Advanced Camera for Survey (ACS) on 29.05.2005. They demonstrated, based on microlensing simulations, that the observed anomalies in the image pair A-D could easily be explained by ML. Unfortunately, their spectra have insufficient spectral resolution to allow a proper investigation of the line profile differences.

sources.summary: The paper presents SDSS J0924+0219 as an extreme anomalous lensed quasar whose optical and X-ray D-image faintness matches the authors' worst-case microlensing estimate.

quotes.quote: Quad Lens SDSS J0924+0219 call SDSS J0924+0219 ``the most anomalous lensed quasar''. Using the Keeton et al. model, with the HST V band source flux determined from the B and C images, we find the D image is 2.79 magnitudes fainter than predicted, compared with a _ - excursion of 3.1 mag found from Figure 3. Using the X-ray fluxes from again calibrated by B and C, we find image D is 2.58 magnitudes fainter than predicted. One might wonder whether the faintness of image D is due to substructure milli-lensing, but have found radio flux ratios that are consistent with the model predictions, ruling out the milli-lensing hypothesis. SDSS J0924+0219 would appear to be a quad in which our worst case estimate is borne out.

sources.summary: The review summarizes static microlensing analysis of SDSS 0924 + 0219, giving soft-X-ray, UV, and optical sizes and placing most soft X-ray emission within 30 gravitational radii.

quotes.quote: In MacLeod et al. 2015 we analyze the light-curves of z = 1.524 quasar SDSS 0924 + 0219 using static microlensing magnification patterns. SDSS J0924 + 0219 has been observed at a variety of wavelengths ranging from the near-infrared to X-ray. Our microlensing analysis in this system constrains the soft-X-ray, UV, and optical half-light radii to be 2.5 ^ +10 _ 2 10 ^ 14 cm, 8 ^ +24 _ 7 10 ^ 14 cm, and 5^ +5. _ 2.5 10 ^ 15 cm, respectively. Assuming the MgII based black-hole estimate of M_ BH = 2.8 10 ^ 8 M _ the majority of the soft X-ray emission of SDSS 0924 + 0219 originates within 30 r_ g .

sources.summary: The paper says the SDSS0924+0219 lens galaxy resembles a typical elliptical at z=0.4, with external shear and microlensing important for fitting the observations.

quotes.quote: SDSS0924+0219 Estimated colors and magnitudes of the lens galaxy are consistent with those of a typical elliptical galaxy at z_ l =0.4 and references therein quadlens3 . Although the lens environment does not show any nearby objects perturbing the system, quite an amount of external shear is needed to obtain a satisfying fit to observations, with the lens being typically modeled by a (flattened) singular isothermal sphere (SIS). Additionally, microlensing plays an important role in explaining the observed flux ratios within GR/CDM, which is likely to be true in TeVeS as well.

sources.summary: The paper includes SDSS 0924+0219 among the lens galaxies with prior observations and reports no detected absorption, excluding it from the successful dust-to-gas measurements.

quotes.quote: In this paper, we analyze \ observations of 10 gravitational lenses, HE 0047 1756 (Wisotzki et al. \ 2004; Ofek et al. \ 2006), QJ 0158 4325 (Maza et al. \ 1995; Morgan et al. \ 1999; Faure et al. \ 2009), SDSS 0246 0805 (Inada et al. \ 2005; Eigenbrod et al. \ 2006), HE 0435 1223 (Wisotzki et al. \ 2002; Ofek et al. \ 2006), SDSS 0924+0219 (Inada et al. \ 2003a; Ofek et al. \ 2006), SDSS 1004+4112 (Inada et al. \ 2003b), PG 1115+080 (Weymann et al. \ 1980; Kundi et al. \ 1997), Q 1355 2257 (Morgan et al. \ 2003; Eigenbrod et al. \ 2006), HE 1104 1805 (Wisotzki 1993; Lidman et al. \ 2000), and Q 2237+0305 (Huchra et al. \ 1985), to measure the differential X-ray absorption between the lensed images, the metallicity, and the dust-to-gas ratio of lens galaxies.
Observations of QJ 0158 4325, HE 0435 1223, SDSS 0924+0219, SDSS 1004+4112, HE 1104 1805, and Q 2237+0305 were reported in Chen et al. \ (2012).
We detected no absorption in HE 1104 1805 and SDSS 0924 + 0219, and we had to drop QJ 0158 4325 and SDSS 0246 0825 since the differential extinction E(B-V) and absorption N_H are of opposite sign.

sources.summary: SDSS J092455.79+021924.9 is a radio-quiet quasar quadruply imaged by a foreground early-type galaxy, with ALMA CO/continuum detections and HST broad-line flux-ratio anomalies.

quotes.quote: SDSS J092455.79+021924.9, is a redshift 1.524 radio-quiet quasar quadruply imaged by a z=0.394 early-type galaxy. The four images are clearly visible in the targeted CO(5-4) line as well as in the band 6 continuum badole20 . Broad line flux ratios anomalies detected by HST are most likely caused by microlensing effects morgan06, macleod15 .

sources.summary: The paper classifies SDSS 0924+0219 as a fold-lens anomaly because image D is extremely faint, violating the fold relation in broad-band and emission-line flux ratios.

quotes.quote: Comparing these predictions with the data, we find that five of the the 12 known lenses with fold configurations have flux ratio anomalies: B0712+472, SDSS 0924+0219, PG 1115+080, B1555+375, and B1933+503.

SDSS 0924+0219: Although AD is not a particularly close pair (making the predicted distribution fairly broad), image D is so faint that there is a gross violation of the fold relation in both broad-band and broad emission line flux ratios. Differences between the flux ratios plus time variability suggest the presence of microlensing csk0924,hst0924 .

sources.summary: The paper describes SDSS0924 + 021 as a quadruply lensed quasar with an extreme flux-ratio anomaly and an E-template lens galaxy at z=0.393.

quotes.quote: SDSS0924 + 021 : Discovered as part of the Sloan Digital Sky Survey by Inada et al. (2003). This is a quadruply lensed quasar with a maximum image separation of 1 8 , in which the merging pair (images A D in Fig. AllFindingCharts ) has a broad-band flux ratio of 10 - the most extreme known case of a ``flux ratio anomaly'' (see Keeton et al. 2005, for implications). The lensing galaxy spectrum, shown in Fig. SDSS0924 , is well matched by an E galaxy template, with a redshift of 0.393 .

gmw_00001849.overview: The high-redshift gigahertz-peaked spectrum quasar 0858 279 exhibits a core-jet structure [arXiv:2104.08544, arXiv:2401.03603] with a weak, compact core [arXiv:2104.08544] and a bent jet [arXiv:2401.03603]. It displays broad-band radio variability [arXiv:0901.1187] and flux density changes that suggest recurrent plasma ejections [arXiv:2401.03603].

sources.summary: The paper studies 0858 279 as a high-redshift gigahertz-peaked spectrum quasar, resolving a core-jet structure, strong jet magnetic field, Faraday rotation, and shock-wave evidence.

quotes.quote: We performed multi-frequency studies on the gigahertz-peaked spectrum high-redshift quasar 0858 279. Initially, the source presented itself at early VLBI images as a very peculiar resolved blob. We observed the quasar with the VLBA at 1.4 24 GHz in dual-polarization mode. The high spatial resolution and spectral index maps enabled us to resolve the core-jet structure and locate a weak and compact core by its inverted spectrum. The dominant jet component 20 parsecs away from the core was found to be optically thin above 10 GHz and opaque below it.

We also estimated an uncommonly strong magnetic field in the bright jet feature which turned out to be around 3 G. Faraday rotation measure maps revealed high RM values over 6000 rad/m ^ 2 . Additionally, these maps allowed us to follow the magnetic field direction in the bright jet feature being perpendicular to the propagation direction of the jet.

sources.summary: The paper studies PKS 0858 279 as a GPS quasar with a weak core, bent jet, shock-cloud interaction, stable parsec-scale structure, flux variability, recurrent jet ejection, and a sharp rotation-measure drop.

quotes.quote: firstpage - lastpage We conducted multi-epoch, multi-frequency parsec-scale studies on the gigahertz-peaked spectrum quasar PKS 0858 279 with the Very Long Baseline Array (VLBA). Our observations on 2005-11-26 elucidated a weak core, characterized by an inverted spectrum, and a distinctly bent jet that exhibited a notable bright feature in its Stokes I emission. Through comprehensive analysis of polarization and spectral data, we inferred the formation of a shock wave within this feature, stemming from interactions with a dense cloud in the ambient medium. In this paper, VLBI- Gaia astrometry further reinforces the core identification.

With a deep analysis of six additional VLBA epochs spanning from 2007 to 2018, we observed that while the quasar's parsec-scale structure remained largely consistent, there were discernible flux density changes. These variations strongly imply the recurrent ejection of plasma into the jet. Complementing our VLBA data, RATAN-600 observations of the integrated spectra suggested an interaction between standing and travelling shock waves in 2005. Moreover, our multi-epoch polarization analysis revealed a drastic drop in rotation measure values from 6000 rad/m ^2 to 1000 rad/m ^2 within a single year, attributable to diminishing magnetic fields and particle density in an external cloud.

sources.summary: The paper presents PKS 0858 279 as an unusual high-redshift GPS quasar with strong radio variability, resolved VLBA core-jet morphology, high Doppler factor, and synchrotron self-absorption at low frequencies.

quotes.quote: The high redshift GPS quasar PKS 0858 279 exhibits the following properties which make the source unusual. Our RATAN-600 monitoring of 1--22 GHz spectrum has detected broad-band radio variability with high amplitude and relatively short time scale. In the same time, the milliarcsecond scale structure observed in a snapshot VLBA survey turned out to be very resolved which is not expected from the fast flux density variations. We performed 1.4--22 GHz VLBA observations of this quasar in 2005--2007. It has revealed a core-jet morphology.

A high Doppler factor is suggested for the jet, its nature is discussed in this report on the basis of the multi-frequency VLBA and RATAN data collected. Synchrotron self-absorption was confirmed to be dominating at low frequencies, the magnetic field strength of the dominating jet feature is estimated of an order of 0.1 mG.

gmw_00001850.name: B 1834+620
gmw_00001850.overview: B 1834+620 is a double-double radio galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/9910448] classified as a Fanaroff-Riley II source [arXiv:astro-ph/0310045]. It features nested inner and outer radio lobes [arXiv:astro-ph/9910448] that indicate interrupted jet activity [arXiv:astro-ph/0310045, arXiv:1510.00577] driven by a supermassive binary black hole [arXiv:astro-ph/0310045].

sources.summary: The paper models B1834+620 as a double-double FR II radio source whose interrupted jets result from an elliptical supermassive binary black hole and constrained accretion-disk parameters.

quotes.quote: We applied the model to a particular double-lobed radio source B1834+620, which has an interruption time scale 1 Myr . We showed that the orbit of the secondary in B1834+620 is elliptical with a typical eccentricity e 0.68 and the mass ratio q of the secondary and the primary is 0.01 q 0.4 . The accretion disk is a standard disk with 0.01 0.04 and the ratio of disk half height H and radius is 0.01 . The model predicates that double-lobed radio structure forms only in FR II or borderline FR I/FR II radio galaxies and the detection rate of double-lobed radio sources among FRII radio sources is about one percent.

For typical disk parameters = 0.01 and = 0.01 , Fig. fig:qe shows the relation of the eccentricity e and the mass ratio q for B1834+620. For a circular orbit e=0 , q > 1 is required. Therefore, the orbit of the binary system in B1834+620 is not circular but elliptical. On the other hand, Fig. fig:qe shows that if q 10^ 2 , e > 0.7 .

sources.summary: The paper presents LOFAR 144 MHz imaging of double-double radio galaxy B1834+620, resolving four components, detecting polarized northern-lobe emission, and interpreting inner-lobe features as restarted jet activity.

quotes.quote: We present a high resolution image of the DDRG B1834+620 obtained at 144 MHz using LOFAR commissioning data. Our image covers about 100 square degrees and contains over 1000 sources. The four components of the DDRG B1834+620 have been resolved for the first time at 144 MHz.
Polarized emission was detected at +60 rad m ^ 2 in the northern outer lobe.

The spectral fit of the four components is consistent with a scenario in which the outer lobes are still active or the jets recently switched off, while emission of the inner lobes is the result of a mix-up of new and old jet activity. From the presence of the newly extended features in the inner lobes of the DDRG, we can infer that the mechanism responsible for their formation is the bow shock that is driven by the newly launched jet.

sources.summary: The paper presents B 1834+620 as a double-double radio galaxy candidate, with nested lobes implying interrupted central jet activity on a constrained timescale.

quotes.quote: Radio contour plots of two of these, B 1834+620 and B 1450+333, are shown in Figs. fig:schoenmakers3 and fig:schoenmakers4 , respectively. Both cases are clearly different from `standard' FRII-type radio galaxies. Since they consist of an inner double-lobed radio structure as well as a larger outer double-lobed structure, we have called these sources `double-double' radio galaxies (DDRGs; , , ).

The observed two-sidedness and symmetry in the morphology of the inner structures strongly suggests a central cause for this phenomenon, and we believe that an interruption of the central jet-forming activity is the most likely one. For the source B 1834+620 (Fig. fig:schoenmakers3 ) we are able to constrain the time-scale of the interruption to 6 Myr ( , ).

gmw_00001851.name: ESO 113-G010
gmw_00001851.overview: ESO 113-G010 is a Seyfert 1.8 active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1210.7874, arXiv:1204.2826] with a structurally complex host galaxy that may be barred or highly inclined [arXiv:1511.00904, arXiv:1310.0272]. The system exhibits X-ray variability [arXiv:1210.7874, arXiv:astro-ph/0510551] and contains a warm absorber consisting of two distinct ionization phases [arXiv:1204.2826].

sources.summary: The paper identifies ESO 113-G010 as an AGN lacking a prior black-hole mass estimate, then derives a variability-plane mass consistent with earlier single-epoch and McHardy-relation estimates.

quotes.quote: Among the objects in our sample, ESO 113-G010 is a good example of no BH mass estimate reported before. ESO 113-G010 do not seem to show any variations on the absorber, which seems to be low, considering that we detect the absorption for only one of the segments. Thus, the break-mass-luminosity relation seems to apply in this case (Eq. eq:finalbhnh ).

We computed the BH mass estimate using the 41 break frequencies found for this object We excluded one of the detected PSD frequency breaks to avoid segments where absorption along the line of sight was detected. . For each of these breaks, we used the Monte-Carlo method to include the errors on the parameters of the break-mass-luminosity relation and the errors on the luminosity and break frequencies. We found a BH mass estimate of log(M_ BH )=6.8 0.2 . Optical reverberation mapping has established a scaling relationship between the BLR radius and AGN luminosity Kaspi00,Bentz09 which allows single-epoch BH mass estimates when combined with the measurement of the broad line width. This method was used by Cackett13 to estimate a BH mass of log(M_ BH ) =6.8 for ESO 113-G010, fully consistent with our result.

sources.summary: ESO 113-G010 is analyzed as a highly variable Seyfert AGN; the paper finds hard low-frequency lags, a 325 s high-frequency soft lag, and interprets the soft lag as accretion-disk reverberation.

quotes.quote: Here, we analyze a 100 ksec archival \ observation of the highly variable AGN, in order to search for lags between hard, 1.5 -- 4.5 keV, and soft, 0.3 -- 0.9 keV, energy X-ray bands. At the lowest frequencies available in the lightcurve ( 1.5 10^ 4 Hz), we find hard lags where the power-law dominated hard band lags the soft band (where the reflection fraction is high). However, at higher frequencies in the range (2-3) 10^ 4 Hz we find a soft lag of 325 89 s. The general evolution from hard to soft lags as the frequency increases is similar to other AGN where soft lags have been detected. We interpret this soft lag as due to reverberation from the accretion disk, with the reflection component responding to variability from the X-ray corona.

In this manuscript, we present an X-ray timing analysis of \ ( z = 0.0257 ), using observations, which we find to show the same lag-frequency evolution as has been observed in other AGN. \ is a Seyfert 1.8 displaying a strong soft excess and X-ray variability pietsch98,porquet04,porquet07 which are the two basic components needed for tracing negative reverberation time delays.

sources.summary: HE0103--5842 is presented as a likely barred, structurally complex Seyfert 1.8 galaxy whose NIR spectrum lacks resolved broad components and is dominated by stellar light.

quotes.quote: HE0103--5842 (also known as ESO 113-10) has a redshift of z=0.0257 ( D_L=112.2 ). At first view, it looks like a barred galaxy. However, the morphological study 2014A&A...561A.140B reveals a more complex underlying structure.

The galaxy has been classified as Seyfert 1.8 by . No broad components could be resolved in our NIR spectra. The blue continuum and deep stellar absorption features indicate that the nuclear non-stellar component is weak in comparison to the stellar component.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes ESO 113-G010 as a Seyfert 1.8 galaxy with two-phase X-ray warm absorption, optical-UV reddening, and likely dusty ionized absorber-driven nuclear obscuration.

quotes.quote: We present the first analysis of the X-ray warm absorber and nuclear obscuration in the Seyfert 1.8 galaxy ESO 113-G010 . We used archival data from a 100 ks observation made in 2005. From high resolution spectroscopy analysis of the RGS data, we detect absorption lines originating from a warm absorber consisting of two distinct phases of ionisation, with 3.2 and 2.3 respectively. The higher-ionised component has a larger column density and outflow velocity ( 1.6 10^ 22 cm ^ 2 , v 1100 \ ) than the lower-ionised component ( 0.5 10^ 22 cm ^ 2 , v 700 \ ). The shape of the optical-UV continuum and the large Balmer decrement ( / 8 ) indicate significant amount of reddening is taking place in our line of sight in the host galaxy of the AGN; however, the X-ray spectrum is not absorbed by cold neutral gas intrinsic to the source.

sources.summary: The paper describes HE0103-5842 as an irregular host whose apparent bar may be an inclined spiral structure, with possible small-scale bar-like residuals and unclear large-scale structure.

quotes.quote: 11 HE0103-5842 At first sight a barred galaxy. More precise inspection reveals a spiral structure inside the bar-like feature. This could be interpreted as a highly inclined spiral structure. In the residuum, a smaller-scaled elongated structure can be seen (possibly a bar?). Furthermore, additional large-scale structure - first interpreted as spiral arms - is seen.

sources.summary: The paper highlights ESO 113-- G 010 as a Seyfert 1.8 with short-timescale X-ray variability, an extreme soft excess, and a possible narrow feature near 5.4 keV.

quotes.quote: ESO 113-- G 010, a Seyfert 1.8, demonstrated fluctuations of about 15 during the short ( < 5 ) observation. In combination with the extreme soft excess and 5.4 emission feature (see Section sect:unf ), the variability in this Seyfert 1.8 certainly adds to its intrigue.

Two AGN which stand out are ESO 113-- G 010, with a possible narrow line at 5.4 ; and UGC 3973, with a potential line at E = 7.99 0.06 . Both spectra are presented in detail in other works (Porquet 2004, for ESO 113-- G 010; Gallo 2005 for UGC 3973) and we will not discuss them further here.

gmw_00001852.name: Hercules A
gmw_00001852.overview: Hercules A is a giant cD elliptical radio galaxy [arXiv:0806.4150, arXiv:astro-ph/0011455] that hosts an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:astro-ph/0504350]. It features a double nucleus [arXiv:astro-ph/0204475, arXiv:1305.4935] and a complex network of dusty filaments [arXiv:1305.4935], indicating a past galaxy merger [arXiv:astro-ph/0204475].

sources.summary: The paper applies a magnetic-tower MHD model to Hercules A, explaining its radio jet-lobe transition, wiggling, shocks, and projected magnetic-field structure.

quotes.quote: Here we examine our model's applicability to an individual source, Hercules (Herc) A (3C 348), by performing three-dimensional MHD simulations. Herc A is one of the most powerful double-lobed radio sources in the sky. Its total energy content is approximately 3 10^ 60 erg and it is identified with the central compact diffuse (cD) galaxy of a cluster SH93 at a low redshift of z=0.154 S99 . The X-ray luminosity of 4.8 10^ 44 erg s ^ 1 GL04 is thought to be due to bremsstrahlung of the very high temperature intracluster medium (ICM).

Herc A has a peculiar radio structure; the morphology looks like a classical double-lobed Fanaroff-Riley type II (FR II) radio source FR74 , but it has no compact hotspots, instead showing an unusual jet-dominated morphology as in an Fanaroff-Riley type I (FR I) radio source, which was first revealed by DF84 . Furthermore, the two jets in Herc A are quite different in appearance. The eastern jet appears to have continuous twisting flows, while the western jet leads to a unique sequence of ``rings''. The jets in Herc A are well-collimated initially but they flare suddenly at a certain distance from the nucleus.

sources.summary: The paper presents HST imaging of Hercules A, finding extended dusty filaments, blue continuum and H emission, jet-aligned structures, and a cD radio-galaxy host in a poor cluster.

quotes.quote: We present U, V, and I-band images of the host galaxy of Hercules A (3C 348) obtained with HST/WFC3/UVIS. We find a network of dusty filaments which are more complex and extended than seen in earlier HST observations. The filaments are associated with a faint blue continuum light (possibly from young stars) and faint H emission. It seems likely that the cold gas and dust has been stripped from a companion galaxy now seen as a secondary nucleus.
