Hercules A (3C 348) has the fourth highest flux density at 178 MHz. The two radio jets, extended over 400 kpc, have remarkably different morphology dreher84,gizani03,cotton13 with the Eastern side showing a twisting jet, and the Western side showing a series of ring-like features.
The host galaxy is the dominant cD in a poor cluster at redshift z=0.154 greenstein62,owen89, allington93, zirbel96 and contains a secondary nucleus e.g. smith89,sadun93,baum96, ramos11 .

sources.summary: The paper presents Hercules A as a giant cD radio galaxy with lobes and jets, embedded in a group environment, whose BeppoSAX/ROSAT/ASCA X-rays are dominated by thermal plasma.

quotes.quote: This source is a giant cD elliptical (Spinrad et al. 1985, Zirbel 1997) embedded in an environment that Zirbel (1997) has classified as a group of the first Bautz-Morgan class, with Hercules A as the main member. At radio frequencies, 3C 348 shows two large and symmetric lobes connected by twin jets and without the presence of any hot spot (Dreher Feigelson 1984). The radio emission originates in the extended lobes mainly, with a weak contribution of a compact core (S _ , 4.8 GHz = 10 mJy, corresponding to < 0.1 of the total emission, Morganti et al. 1993). Polarization maps show a strong asymmetry in the fractional polarization, with the most prominent jet lying in the more strongly polarized lobe (Harvanek Hardcastle 1998, Gizani Leahy 1999).

The X-ray properties of 3C 348, derived by ROSAT and ASCA observations, are discussed by Siebert et al. (1999). The flux detected by ASCA is fit to a thermal model with temperature T 4.6 keV and metallicity 0.38 (with respect to the standard cosmic values). The presence of an additional non thermal component, with a flat slope (photon index 1.7 ), is also consistent with the data.

sources.summary: The paper describes Hercules A as a powerful radio galaxy whose AGN outburst drives a strong cluster-scale shock, large cavities, and energetically important ICM heating.

quotes.quote: The radio source Hercules A resides at the center of a cooling flow cluster of galaxies at redshift z = 0.154 . A \ X-ray image reveals a shock front in the intracluster medium (ICM) surrounding the radio source, about 160 kpc from the active galactic nucleus (AGN) that hosts it. The shock has a Mach number of 1.65, making it the strongest of the cluster-scale shocks driven by an AGN outburst found so far. As for the other large AGN outbursts in cooling flow clusters, this outburst overwhelms radiative losses from the ICM of the Hercules A cluster by a factor of 100 .

Analysis of a \ X-ray image of the Hercules A cluster shows that it has cavities and a shock front associated with the powerful radio source. Unusually, the cavities show no clear connection to the radio source. The shock front is elongated in the direction of the radio lobes and appears to be its cocoon shock.

sources.summary: The paper maps Hercules A from 144 MHz to 7 GHz and argues that its lobe rings are synchrotron-aged material from intermittent core activity rather than shocks.

quotes.quote: The prominent radio source Hercules A features complex structures in its radio lobes. Although it is one of the most comprehensively studied sources in the radio sky, the origin of the ring structures in the Hercules A radio lobes remains an open question. We present the first sub-arcsecond angular resolution images at low frequencies ( < 300 MHz) of Hercules A, made with the International LOFAR Telescope.

We explore the origin of the rings within the lobes of Hercules A, and test whether their properties are best described by a shock model, where shock waves are produced by the jet propagating in the radio lobe, or by an inner-lobe model, where the rings are formed by decelerated jetted plasma. From spectral index mapping our large frequency coverage reveals that the curvature of the different ring spectra increases with distance away from the central active galactic nucleus. We demonstrate that the spectral shape of the rings is consistent with synchrotron aging, which speaks in favor of an inner-lobe model where the rings are formed from the deposition of material from past periods of intermittent core activity.

sources.summary: The paper models Hercules A as a radio galaxy whose lobes contain ring-like shocks, turbulent jets, merger-related optical distortions, and X-ray/radio interactions.

quotes.quote: The radio lobes of the radio galaxy Hercules A contain intriguing ring-like structures concentric with the jet axis. To investigate the occurrence of such features, we have used hydrodynamic simulations of jets with a range of Mach numbers (from M=2 to 50 ) and densities (down to a ratio of 10^ 4 relative to the background) to generate ray-traced images simulating synchrotron emission from the time-dependent shock structures. We compare these images with observations of Hercules A, and consider the physical nature and temporal evolution of the most plausible configurations. We find that the observed ring-like structures are well explained as nearly annular shocks propagating in the backflow surrounding the jet.

In optical observations, the elliptical galaxy is found to have a double nucleus (with a separation 4'' ) and tail-like distortions of the stellar isophotes implying a merger of galaxies minkowski1957,sadun1993 .
The diffuse X-ray distribution is elongated in the direction of the radio source axis, suggesting an interaction with the radio lobes.

sources.summary: The paper studies the east and west lobes of Hercules A, detects its east jet, and finds Chandra spectra give zero best-fitting inverse-Compton power-law normalization for both lobes.

quotes.quote: Her A For Her A we investigated both the E and W lobes. Because of the symmetry of the source we were able to use identical off-lobe regions for both lobes. There is a clear detection of the E jet in this Chandra observation, corresponding to the radio structures denoted E5, E6 and E11 by Gizani Leahy (2003), which we mask out in our observation of the E lobe. The best-fitting temperature for the off-lobe regions was 4.9 0.1 keV, with abundance 0.48 0.06 solar, which is consistent with the earlier Chandra measurements of Nulsen \ (2005), bearing in mind that our regions necessarily span the strong surface brightness drop described in that paper. For both lobes and for both N_ H values, a fit with fixed temperature and abundance gave a best-fitting power-law normalization of zero.

sources.summary: 3C 348 is the only FR I source in the sample with substantially extended line emission, reaching 35 kpc and showing central rotation plus filamentary velocity growth.

quotes.quote: 3C 348: FR I/LEG, 1 = 2.71 kpc. A close companion, at the same redshift, is seen at 9 kpc to the NW. This is the only FR I source showing substantially extended emission line, reaching a distance of 35 kpc on the west side. The gas is mainly in rotation in the central regions, but the kinematic axis (at PA 20^ ) is almost perpendicular to the geometrical one. Following the large scale gas filaments, the gas velocity increases steadily toward larger radii, reaching an amplitude of 400 .

sources.summary: The paper describes PKS 1648+05 as a powerful radio source whose GMOS-S morphology shows disturbed structure, companions, shells, fans, dust, tails, bridges, or multiple nuclei indicating interaction or merger activity.

quotes.quote: This WLRG/FRI source is at the center of a cooling flow cluster of galaxies at z=0.154. A recent analysis of Chandra X-ray data showed that the cluster has cavities and a shock front associated with the radio source Nulsen05 . Heckman86 did not report the existence of any feature for this galaxy from their optical imaging. However, our Gemini data (Figure pks1648_online ) reveal patchy dust features crossing the galaxy nucleus in a roughly East-West direction, out to a radius of 6.5 kpc to the East.

sources.summary: The paper estimates the kinematic SZ contribution for Hercules A, finding an optical depth near 10^-4 and arguing the kSZ shift can be ignored for this source.

quotes.quote: For an order of magnitude estimate of temperature shift due to kSZ , we consider the source Hercules A from Table tab:table2 with highest y_ NT parameter ( y_ NT =9 10^ 6 ). The optical depth of this source turns out to be 10^ 4 . With v / 10^ 3 , we see that the distortion due to kSZ are of the order v 10^ 7 10^ 6 . Therefore, we can safely ignore the kSZ temperature shift and consequently will be able to differentiate non-thermal SZ from other distortions, for this source.

gmw_00001853.name: Dwarf 3
gmw_00001853.overview: Dwarf 3 is a dwarf companion galaxy of Centaurus A [arXiv:2205.12277]. It is currently undergoing disruption, which has produced an associated stellar stream within the Centaurus A halo [arXiv:2205.12277].

sources.summary: The paper models Dwarf 3 as Centaurus A's disrupting dwarf companion, fitting its stellar stream while linking inferred Cen A halo properties to Dw3 mass, velocity, and distance.

quotes.quote: As a first attempt to look at these questions, we model Magellan/Megacam imaging of the Centaurus A's (Cen A) disrupting dwarf companion Dwarf 3 (Dw3) and its associated stellar stream, to find out what can be learned about the Cen A dark-matter halo. We develop a novel external galaxy stream-fitting technique and generate model stellar streams that reproduce the stream morphology visible in the imaging. We find that there are many viable stream models that fit the data well, with reasonable parameters, provided that Cen A has a halo mass larger than M _ 200 >4.70 10^ 12 M _ . There is a second stream in Cen A's halo that is also reproduced within the context of this same dynamical model. However, stream morphology in the imaging alone does not uniquely determine the mass or mass distribution for the Cen A halo.

To explore the Dw3 stream evolved in the fiducial potential of Cen A (see Figure fig:potential , solid black line), we first fix Dw3's position and the relative radial velocity between Dw3 and CenA (see Table tab:cena ). Thus, the only free parameters in our initial search for orbits that can reproduce the Dw3 stream are the two other Dw3 velocity components.

gmw_00001854.name: 3C 48A
gmw_00001854.overview: Located approximately one arcsecond northeast of the quasar 3C 48, 3C 48A is a highly reddened object detected in the near-infrared [arXiv:astro-ph/0311074]. It may represent a companion second nucleus or a region of interaction between the radio jet and the interstellar medium [arXiv:astro-ph/0311074].

sources.summary: The paper reports a near-infrared detection of 3C 48A, describing it as a highly reddened potential second nucleus whose nature may be a companion nucleus or jet-ISM interaction.

quotes.quote: For the first time we clearly identify the highly reddened potential second nucleus 3C 48A about 1'' northeast of the quasar position in the NIR. In order to study the nuclear region, especially 3C 48A, the nuclear contribution has to be removed. Furthermore there appears to be an alignment of the radio jet with 3C 48A in the NIR.

Our NIR imaging and spectroscopy result in a first detection of 3C 48A in J , H , and Ks . The host galaxy, but especially 3C 48A, are strongly reddened. Both scenarios, a second nucleus or an interaction of the ISM with the radio jet, are still possible to explain the nature of 3C 48A.

gmw_00001855.name: COSMOS J100038+020822
gmw_00001855.overview: COSMOS J100038+020822 is an obscured, millimeter-bright quasar at a redshift of z=1.8275 [arXiv:0809.2337]. The galaxy exhibits a complex optical morphology that suggests a possible merger or interaction, and it contains molecular gas with a relatively low excitation level [arXiv:0809.2337].

sources.summary: The paper detects multiple CO lines from COSMOS J100038+020822 and describes a gas-rich, dusty starburst-QSO composite with SMG-like molecular gas and merger-like optical morphology.

quotes.quote: Using the IRAM 30m telescope, we have detected the ^ 12 CO J=2-1 , 4-3 , 5-4 , and 6-5 emission lines in the millimeter-bright, blank-field selected AGN COSMOS J100038+020822 at redshift z=1.8275 . The MAMBO source J100038.01+020822.4 (hereafter: J100038) is one of the strongest millimeter selected sources in COSMOS (boosting corrected S_ 1.2 \ mm = 4.6 0.9 mJy). It hosts an X-ray luminous ( L_ X 10^ 44 erg s ^ 1 ), absorbed (log N( H ) 22-23 cm ^ 2 ) AGN that classifies it as an obscured QSO Brusa2007,Mainieri2007 , and its optical spectrum is typical of a broad line (BL) AGN Trump2007 .

The observations of the CO lines in J100038 show that its molecular gas is somewhat less excited than observed in local starburst or high-redshift QSOs. The lower excitation of the molecular gas observed in these cases, and in particular towards J100038, is likely produced by the relatively low H _ 2 density values ( n( H _ 2 )<10^ 4 cm ^ 3 ). The optical morphology of J100038+020822 is complex, showing evidence of a possible interaction or merger.

gmw_00001856.name: NGC 1359
gmw_00001856.overview: NGC 1359 is a Magellanic barred spiral galaxy currently in the coalescence stage of a minor merger [arXiv:2303.00626, arXiv:2112.06564]. This interaction has produced a distorted stellar disk [arXiv:2108.04414], a disturbed atomic gas morphology with massive tidal debris fields [arXiv:2303.00626, arXiv:2108.04410], and enhanced star formation [arXiv:2303.00626, arXiv:2108.04414].

sources.summary: NGC 1359 is treated as a Magellanic barred spiral in a minor-merger coalescence stage, with enhanced sSFR, accreted gas, starburst-tracing radio emission, PAH deficit, and disturbed debris.

quotes.quote: NGC 1359 NGC 1359 is classified as a Magellanic S-shaped barred spiral galaxy in the coalescence stage of a minor merger Omar2005 . Murugeshan2021 measures NGC 1359 to have enhanced specific SFR (sSFR) while its atomic gas fraction is higher than expected making it HI-normal for its measured integrated disk stability as compared to other Eridanus group members. This enhanced atomic gas fraction and sSFR is likely a result of the accretion of fresh gas during the merger which leads to increased SF. Wangs2022 find this galaxy is the least affected by tidal interactions and has the highest size ratio (between HI and stellar disk) in our sample; however, as the tidal interaction measurement only takes into account perturbations from galaxies outside this system, it does not take into account the tidal impact of the past minor merger.

The radio continuum emission does trace the starburst sites extremely accurately. The W3PAH emission traces the g band emission however it does not extend beyond the stellar disk unlike most of the galaxies in this sample. There is a significant flux deficit in measured W3PAH emission suggesting that dust may have been removed and PAHs destroyed during the merger and subsequent starbursts; however, we see no evidence of extended W3PAH emission tracing the HI gas as we do in NGC 1367 or NGC 1415.
We do however see that NGC 1359 has an extremely disturbed stellar and HI morphology with a massive debris field to the south as a result of the minor merger that this galaxy has undergone.

sources.summary: The paper singles out NGC 1359 as the only clearly coalescing merger, an outlier with low tidal strength and high H I size ratio, and a ram-pressure-stripping candidate.

quotes.quote: However, from inspecting the optical and images of the sample, there is only one system (NGC 1359, ID 69) clearly identified to be in the coalescence stage of a merger.

The outlier NGC 1359 (ID 69) has the lowest S_ sum and highest size ratio of the high-mass subsample. NGC 1359 is a merger system in the stage of coalescence. Because the whole system is treated as one galaxy, tidal strength S_ sum only considers the perturbation from galaxies outside this system. The correlation coefficient between S_ sum and R_ HI / R_ 25, g for the high-mass galaxies becomes consistent with 0 if NGC 1359 is excluded from the subsample.

NGC 1390 (ID 21), ESO 548-65 (ID 23) and NGC 1359 (ID 69), out of 36 galaxies are identified to be candidates for ram pressure stripping, which experience higher ram pressure than the gravitational restoring forces at R_ HI . Among these three candidates, NGC 1359 is a merging pair, thus the ram pressure stripping could be assisted by tidal effects when the is tidally shifted to regions of low restoring forces.

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 1359 as an ongoing minor merger with disturbed H I, tidal plumes and tails, a distorted stellar disk, and enhanced star formation.

quotes.quote: We first discuss candidates in our sample that show clear signs of ongoing tidal interactions, which is evidenced in their 1 and optical morphology, as in the case of NGC 1359 and NGC 1385. NGC 1359 is in the process of a minor merger ( Omar05a ). The 1 morphology of NGC 1359 is observed to be disturbed, with tidal plumes and tails associated with the galaxy. The optical image of the galaxy also shows a distorted and irregular stellar disc, indicative of an ongoing merger. NGC 1385 also shows signs of interaction. We observe an extended tidal tail to the south-west of the galaxy. The SFRs of both of these galaxies is significantly enhanced compared to the rest of the Eridanus galaxies.

sources.summary: The WALLABY paper identifies NGC 1359 as an interacting galaxy pair with prominent resolved H I tidal debris, though it lies in a noisier edge beam.

quotes.quote: Two massive \ clouds are identified and large \ debris fields are seen in the NGC 1359 interacting galaxy pair, and the face-on spiral galaxy NGC 1385. For example, NGC 1359 is an interaction galaxy pair, which WALLABY has resolved into tidal debris (see Figure combine ). Both NGC 1398 and NGC 1359 are located in edge beams, where the noise level is significantly higher than in central beams.
Among the three detections in the NGC 1407 group, a large and spectacular \ tidal debris field is seen around the NGC 1359, an interacting galaxy pair ( 41).

gmw_00001857.name: NGC 1385
gmw_00001857.overview: NGC 1385 is a face-on barred spiral galaxy [arXiv:2303.00626] undergoing a merger in the Eridanus group [arXiv:2108.04414]. It exhibits a highly distorted neutral hydrogen morphology [arXiv:2303.00626, arXiv:2108.04410], a southern tidal debris field [arXiv:2303.00626, arXiv:2108.04410], and enhanced starburst activity [arXiv:astro-ph/0503542, arXiv:2108.04414].

sources.summary: NGC 1385 is described as a high-SFR barred spiral with distorted HI, a southern tidal debris field, enhanced gas and sSFR from accretion, and radio/IRRC signs of pre-processing.

quotes.quote: NGC 1385 NGC 1385 is classified as an almost face-on i = 44^ , P.A = 181^ , Leroy2021 barred spiral galaxy with S-shaped bars and is the highest SFR galaxy in our sample (see Table. tab:SFRtab ). NGC 1385 is located in the south-west of the Eridanus group, is 0.6^ south of WALLABY J033723--235753 (C2 in Wong2021 ), an HI cloud with no detected stellar component. For2021 measure this galaxy to be HI deficient as compared to its expected HI mass as estimated by its stellar mass. The HI morphology of NGC 1385 is extremely distorted with clear compression and extensions in the north-east and south-west respectively. This galaxy also exhibits a southern tidal debris field (which is partially cut off in Fig fig:Murphy1 ) with no optical counterpart suggesting HI gas has been displaced likely due to tidal interactions with its nearby companion WALLABY J033723--235753 .

Murugeshan2021 measures NGC 1385 to have enhanced sSFR while its atomic gas fraction is higher than expected for the measured integrated disk stability as compared to other Eridanus group members. This means that compared to other galaxies within the Eridanus group this galaxy is one of two that is actually HI-normal for their integrated disk stability, likely due to the accretion of HI from its interacting companion. This accretion of fresh gas for NGC 1385 leads to increased HI mass and SF which is consistent with the findings of Ellison2018 and Murugeshan2020 .

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 1385 as a FIR-luminous Eridanus starburst following the radio-FIR correlation, with optical signs of tidal interaction, disturbed spiral arms, molecular gas, and stellar winds.

quotes.quote: The most FIR luminous galaxies in the group are NGC 1482 ( L_ FIR 4.3 10^ 10 L _ ) and NGC 1385 ( L_ FIR 2.5 10^ 10 L _ ). Although these galaxies are 'star-bursts' (Table 3), they are called 'normal' since they follow the radio-FIR correlation. Both of these galaxies have signatures of recent tidal interactions in their optical images. NGC 1385 (SBcd) has a faint stellar envelop to the south and disturbed spirals arms.

The large amounts ( M_ H_ 2 10^ 9 M _ ) of molecular Hydrogen in the centres of these two galaxies have been inferred from the CO observations (Sanders, Scoville Soifer 1991, Elfhag et al. 1996). Strong stellar winds indicative of a star-burst were detected in H _ (Hameed Devereux 1999, Veilleux Rupke 2002).

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 1385 as a face-on spiral with one of the most prominent H I debris fields, distorted H I morphology, and no optical counterpart for the debris.

quotes.quote: Two massive \ clouds are identified and large \ debris fields are seen in the NGC 1359 interacting galaxy pair, and the face-on spiral galaxy NGC 1385. The most prominent tidal debris field is seen in NGC 1359 (an interacting pair) and NGC 1385. NGC 1385 ( 20) is a face-on spiral galaxy that is located 0.6 \ south of WALLABY J033723 235753.
Its \ morphology is very distorted and there is a southern tidal debris field. There is no optical counterpart for the \ debris.

sources.summary: The paper says NGC 1385 is interacting, has a southwest tidal tail, and shows enhanced star formation and gas properties consistent with merger-driven fresh gas accretion.

quotes.quote: NGC 1385 also shows signs of interaction. We observe an extended tidal tail to the south-west of the galaxy. The SFRs of both of these galaxies is significantly enhanced compared to the rest of the Eridanus galaxies.

In the Eridanus sample, NGC 1359 and NGC 1385 are currently undergoing a merger with their companions, and exhibiting clear signs in the form of tidal tails and debris, which are associated with the two galaxies.

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 1385 as a low-mass outlier with a strongly positive metallicity gradient, an almost perfectly flat trend, and positive age and metallicity radial trends.

quotes.quote: The only exceptions are NGC 1087 and NGC 1385, which are both at the low-mass end of our sample (log M _ * /M_ of 9.9 and 10.0 , respectively).

Interestingly, NGC 1385 shows a strongly positive metallicity gradient. This is consistent with the findings from Zhuang2019 , who reported that low-mass and late morphological types commonly show positive metallicity slopes.
The most remarkable exception is NGC 1385, that shows an almost perfectly flat trend. This, together with its positive age and metallicity radial trends make NGC 1385 a very peculiar system.

gmw_00001858.name: ESO 482 G035
gmw_00001858.overview: ESO 482 G035 is a face-on, barred spiral galaxy [arXiv:2108.04410]. It exhibits a distorted and elongated neutral hydrogen morphology, and it may host a nearby low surface brightness dwarf galaxy as a satellite [arXiv:2108.04410].

sources.summary: The paper presents ESO 482 G035 as a face-on SBab galaxy with distorted, elongated H I morphology and a nearby LSB dwarf that could be its satellite.

quotes.quote: ESO 482 G035 ( 29) is a face-on SBab galaxy with a distorted and elongated \ morphology, which is in contrast to the non-fully developed \ disk as shown in OD05b . There is also a known LSB dwarf, F482 018 ME99 , south of ESO 482 G035, which does not have an \ detection. Given the elongation of the , this dwarf could potentially be a satellite of ESO 482 G035.

gmw_00001859.name: NGC 1398
gmw_00001859.overview: NGC 1398 is a large barred spiral galaxy situated at the edge of the Eridanus group [arXiv:2108.04410]. It is classified as a flocculent spiral galaxy that features a bar, an inner ring, an outer pseudo-ring, and ordered rotational motion [arXiv:2304.00515].

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 1398 as a large SBab galaxy at the field edge with extended H I, a ring-like H I feature, and location far from the group centre.

quotes.quote: Both NGC 1398 and NGC 1359 are located in edge beams, where the noise level is significantly higher than in central beams. Both NGC 1398 ( 21) and NGC 1415 ( 27) are large bright galaxies with B band optical isophotal diameter measured at 25 mag arcsec ^ 2 of 425 \ and 208 , respectively. The \ traces various star-forming sites and is extended beyond the optical disk.

NGC 1398 (SBab galaxy) also has a ring like \ that resembles a similar feature as seen in NGC 1533 RW03 . NGC 1398 is located at the edge of the observed field and furthest from the Eridanus group centre.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 1398 as a flocculent barred spiral with inner and outer pseudo-rings, ordered rotation, and HI/SFR enhancement at the oUHR.

quotes.quote: NGC 1398: The centre right panels in Fig. fig:mosaic1 show the optical image and velocity map of NGC 1398. NGC 1398 is classified a flocculent spiral (REF). From the optical image, we see that the galaxy posses a prominent bar, an inner ring and an outer pseudo-ring, which is formed by the tightly winding spiral arms emanating from the ends of the bar. We observe a ring of 1 gas surrounding the outer pseudo-ring . The velocity maps show ordered rotational motion. We observe that the oUHR coincides with an 1 ring as well as the stellar ring-like structure. In addition, a clear enhancement in both _ SFR and _ 1 is also observed at the location of the oUHR for NGC 1398.

gmw_00001860.name: ESO 548 G036
gmw_00001860.overview: ESO 548 G036 is a face-on spiral galaxy located southwest of IC 1953, with which it is weakly interacting [arXiv:2303.00626, arXiv:2108.04410]. The galaxy is hydrogen-deficient but exhibits star formation throughout its stellar disk, alongside a detached neutral hydrogen cloud to its north [arXiv:2303.00626, arXiv:2108.04410].

sources.summary: ESO 548-G036 is presented as a face-on HI-deficient spiral near IC 1953, with disturbed HI, weak tidal perturbation, disk-wide radio star formation, and W3PAH beyond the stellar disk.

quotes.quote: ESO 548-G036 ESO 548-G036 is classified as a face-on spiral galaxy and is measured to be HI deficient with respect to its expected HI mass (see appendix Table. tab:HIprops ). ESO 548-G036 lies south-west of IC 1953 and shows a disturbed HI morphology with a HI cloud detected north of the galaxy. This galaxy is also classified as weakly perturbed by tidal interactions in Wangs2022 .

ESO 548-G036 has radio emission throughout the stellar disk suggesting SF is occurring throughout the galaxy. Flux does not appear to be resolved out for this galaxy due to its small angular size giving us comparable total flux values to NVSS. The W3PAH emission extends beyond the stellar disk and roughly coincides with the HI distribution.

sources.summary: The paper says ESO 548 G036 lies near IC 1953, appears to interact with it through an H I extension, and has a detected H I cloud north of it.

quotes.quote: ESO 548 G036 ( 11) is located south-west of IC 1953 ( 12). There is strong evidence that these two galaxies are interacting with an \ extension seen in IC 1953 towards ESO 548 G036. An \ cloud is also detected north of ESO 548 G036.

gmw_00001861.name: LEDA 798516
gmw_00001861.overview: LEDA 798516 is a low surface brightness background galaxy that lies slightly above the star-forming main sequence [arXiv:2108.04410]. It is possibly interacting with several galaxies to its south, which may trigger a burst of star formation, and it is accompanied by another neutral hydrogen detection to its northeast [arXiv:2108.04410].

sources.summary: The paper discusses LEDA 798516 as an LSB background galaxy slightly above an SFMS relation, possibly interacting with nearby galaxies and accompanied by another H I detection.

quotes.quote: The background galaxies are in general following the SFMS relation of S17 with one (LEDA 798516) slightly above MG17's but it is still within the scatter. LEDA 798516 is possibly interacting with surrounding galaxies and this could result in bursting of star formation. LEDA 798516 ( 48) is a LSB galaxy and is possibly interacting with a few galaxies south of it, including LEDA 798377 (unresolved).
Another \ detection is also seen at the north-east side of LEDA 798516.

gmw_00001862.name: NGC 6086
gmw_00001862.overview: NGC 6086 is an elliptical brightest cluster galaxy located at the center of the Abell 2162 galaxy group [arXiv:1310.5310, arXiv:2305.18077]. It hosts a dying radio source, B2 1610+29, which features two generations of remnant radio lobes and lacks a bright radio core [arXiv:1011.0567, arXiv:2305.18077].

sources.summary: The paper classifies B2 1610+29, associated with NGC 6086, as a dying radio source with relaxed lobes, no bright core, and spectral evidence that only fading lobes remain visible.

quotes.quote: B2 1610+29 The morphology of B2 1610+29 is exactly the one we would expect for a dying radio source: two symmetric and relaxed radio lobes sit on the opposite side of the host galaxy, there are no jets nor hot-spots in the lobes, and the host galaxy lacks a bright radio core. All these features are strong indications that B2 1610+29 is a dying source where only the fading lobes are still visible. The source has an overall linear size of about 100 kpc with a small offset of the source major axis to south-est with respect to the position of the galaxy.

The host galaxy of B2 1610+29 has been recently studied with the VLBA at 5 GHz by Liuzzo et al. (2010). They did not detected any compact radio structure in their high resolution images. Also this results suggests that most likely B2 1610+29 is a dying radio source.

sources.summary: The paper presents broadband radio observations of NGC 6086, finding extended remnant radio lobes, two generations of remnant activity, spectral ages, duty-cycle constraints, and filamentary radio emission.

quotes.quote: We present, for the first time, a spatially resolved radio analysis of the radio galaxy associated with the galaxy NGC 6086 down to 144 MHz and constraints on the spectral age of the diffuse emission to investigate the duty cycle and evolution of the source. In this paper, we present deep, low-frequency radio observations performed with the LOw-Frequency ARray (LOFAR, vanhaarlem2013 ) and the upgraded Giant Metrewave Radio Telescope (uGMRT, gupta2017 ) of the radio galaxy B2 1610+29, which is associated with the galaxy NGC 6086, located at the centre of the galaxy group Abell 2162. NGC 6086 is the central galaxy of the galaxy group Abell 2162, which is one of the 37 members abell1989 of the Hercules supercluster einasto2001 (see Fig. fig:6086sdss ).

Because of the observed morphology, we suggest that the newly detected, large-scale emission might represent old AGN remnant plasma produced during a past phase of jet activity of NGC 6086. What makes NGC 6086 unique is that it shows two generations of remnant lobes, while in most other cases we see a pair of active lobes and a pair of remnant lobes. The filaments in NGC 6086 were observed in the three low-frequency images and we were able to characterise their spectrum between 144-675 MHz (see Fig. fig:filaments_fit ).

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 6086 as a BCG with an appendix light profile and a multicomponent fit whose outer component plausibly traces more elliptical outer isophotes.

quotes.quote: The derivation of light profiles of NGC 3379, NGC 3842, NGC 6086 and NGC 7768 is described in the Appendix.
Some of the galaxies in our sample are BCGs (e.g., NGC 4472 and NGC 6086), which suggests that we should consider the possibility of outer envelopes.

In the end we found that approximately one fourth of our sample galaxies were best fit using multiple components: NGC 1399, NGC 1407, NGC 4552, NGC 5516, NGC 5813, and NGC 6086. The first two galaxies were special cases where fitting the envelope required two additional components. For NGC 1399, we used the sum of a \ and an exponential, while for NGC 1407 we used the sum of two \ functions. In at least two cases -- NGC 5813 and NGC 6086 -- we can plausibly associate the outer \ component with outer isophotes which are significantly more elliptical than the inner part of the galaxy (where the \ component dominates the light).

sources.summary: The paper cites published surface-brightness and HST imaging for NGC6086, notes an earlier KDC claim, and finds only unconfirmed evidence of substructure.

quotes.quote: NGC6086: The surface brightness profile was published by Schombert (1986), and HST imaging by Laine et al. \ (2003). Carter et al. \ (1999) found this galaxy to have a KDC. Even though some evidence of substructure can be seen, this core cannot be confirmed since the profiles measured here do not cover the same radial extend as those in Carter et al. \ (1999).

gmw_00001863.name: PG 1302-102
gmw_00001863.overview: PG 1302-102 is a radio-loud blazar and quasar [arXiv:1502.03113, arXiv:2105.15194] that exhibits a smooth, periodic optical and infrared variability cycle of approximately 5.2 years [arXiv:1501.01375, arXiv:1511.01515]. This periodic behavior is widely interpreted as a candidate subparsec supermassive binary black hole system [arXiv:1501.01375, arXiv:1509.04301], potentially driven by relativistic Doppler boosting [arXiv:1509.04301, arXiv:2105.15194].

sources.summary: The paper identifies PG 1302-102 as a periodically variable quasar and discusses a possible subparsec SMBH binary in an elliptical host with merger-related companions and radio structure.

quotes.quote: Here we report on the detection of a strong, smooth periodic signal in the optical variability of the quasar PG 1302-102 with a mean observed period of 1,884 88 days. It was identified in a search for periodic variability in a data set of light curves for 247,000 known, spectroscopically confirmed quasars with a temporal baseline of 9 years. While the interpretation of this phenomenon is still uncertain, the most plausible mechanisms involve a binary system of two supermassive black holes with a subparsec separation. Such systems are an expected consequence of galaxy mergers and can provide important constraints on models of galaxy formation and evolution.

PG 1302-102 has a median V band magnitude of 15.0 and a redshift of 0.2784 which gives an absolute V band magnitude of M_V = -25.81 , assuming the 9-year WMAP cosmology . It is outside the footprint of the Sloan Digital Sky Survey but is associated with bright infrared and X-ray sources. It is also a very bright (720 mJy at 4.86 GHz), core-dominated flat spectrum radio source. Its optical/NIR spectrum (see Fig. 3) shows broad emission lines (H , H , Pa- , Pa- ) with an inferred mass of (M/M_ ) = 8.3 - 9.4 and the object appears to be radiating at or close to its theoretical Eddington limit ( (L/L_ Edd ) = 0 ).

sources.summary: The paper presents HE 1302-1017 as the only radio-loud quasar host in the sample, with disputed morphology, Sc-like stellar population, low-metallicity gas, high SFR, and merger-linked disturbance.

quotes.quote: HE 1302-1017 (or PKS 1302-102): This object is the only radio loud quasar in the sample. It cannot be properly classified concerning morphology. bahc97 classify the host as elliptical because of its smooth and elliptical shape from HST imaging, although they find an exponential profile to fit their data slightly better. In kuhl02 it is classified as disc from 2D modelling on NIR images, but presents an irregular radial profile. It is the most luminous object of the sample.

The stellar population of the host galaxy is typical of Sc disc population. It has low gas metallicity and the highest SFR of the HII subsample. From bahc97 , it has two small companions merging in, perturbing the radial velocity curve presented in this paper and probably explaining the irregular radial profile, but without high excitation of the large amount of gas present in this host.

sources.summary: The paper studies PG1302 as a radio-loud quasar and SMBH-binary candidate, finding no compelling secondary periodicity beyond the known 5.2-year optical modulation.

quotes.quote: Recently, hereafter G15 Graham+2015 reported the detection of strong periodic variability in the optical flux of quasar PG1302-102. PG1302 is a bright (median V-band magnitude 15), radio-loud quasar at redshift z=0.2784 , with inferred black hole (BH) mass of 10^ 8.3-9.4 M_ . The light curve in optical bands shows a quasi-sinusoidal modulation, with a best-fit period of ( 5.2 0.2 ) yr and amplitude of 0.14 mag.

The presence of multiple periodic components in the variability of SMBHBs is predicted by hydrodynamical simulations of circumbinary disks. The detection of multiple periodicity would provide additional indication that PG1302 is a SMBHB. We analysed the LS periodogram of the optical light-curve of the SMBHB candidate quasar PG1302 to search for multiple periodic components, and assessed the statistical significance of secondary peaks by simulating the variability of PG1302. Our analysis returned a peak with FAP=6 , below our detection threshold.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes PG 1302--102 as a quasar with a possible supermassive binary black hole, using VLBA and VLA radio structure to constrain jets and mass ratio.

quotes.quote: We investigate the pc-scale kinematics and kpc-scale radio morphology of the quasar PG 1302--102, which may harbour a sub-pc separation supermassive binary black hole system at its centre as inferred from optical variability. High-resolution radio interferometric measurements obtained with the Very Long Baseline Array (VLBA) in the Monitoring Of Jets in Active galactic nuclei with VLBA Experiments (MOJAVE) programme at 15 GHz at 20 epochs spanning 17 years were analysed to investigate the pc-scale radio structure. Archival observations with the Very Large Array (VLA) at 1.4 GHz and 5 GHz were obtained to study the kpc-scale morphology. We find that the pc-scale jet is inclined within 2.2 to the line of sight and has a half-opening angle of 0.2 .

The parameters derived from the pc-scale radio jet are qualitatively consistent with those obtained from the analysis of the optical light curve of PG 1302--102. We obtain at least 0.08 for the mass ratio of the two black holes in the system. We find some indication for a helical jet structure on kpc-scale, but the directions of the inner and the extended radio jets are significantly different, obstructing a straightforward connection of the pc- and kpc-scale jets within the binary scenario.

sources.summary: The paper tests PG1302-102 as a candidate supermassive black hole binary, using simultaneous Swift UV/optical monitoring to evaluate whether its multi-wavelength variability matches Doppler-boost predictions.

quotes.quote: The bright quasar PG1302-102 has been identified as a candidate supermassive black hole binary from its near-sinusoidal optical variability. While the significance of its optical periodicity has been debated due to the stochastic variability of quasars, its multi-wavelength variability in the ultraviolet (UV) and optical bands is consistent with relativistic Doppler boost caused by the orbital motion in a binary. However, this conclusion was based previously on sparse UV data which were not taken simultaneously with the optical data. Here we report simultaneous follow-up observations of PG1302-102 with the Ultraviolet Optical Telescope on the Neil Gehrels Swift Observatory in six optical + UV bands. The additional nine Swift observations produce light curves roughly consistent with the trend under the Doppler boost hypothesis, which predicts that UV variability should track the optical, but with a 2.2 times higher amplitude.

We find that the data are consistent with the Doppler boost hypothesis when we compare the the amplitudes in optical B band and UV light curves. However, the ratio of UV to V band variability is larger than expected and is consistent with the Doppler model, only if either the UV/optical spectral slopes vary, the stochastic variability makes a large contribution in the UV, or the sparse new optical data underestimate the true optical variability.

sources.summary: The paper compares PG1302 102 as a low-redshift blazar and binary-SMBH candidate with a 5.2-year optical periodic signal, X-ray detection, Doppler-boosting interpretation, and strong radio emission.

quotes.quote: Comparison to Previous Candidate Periodic Quasars OJ287 and PG 1302 102 are two prototypical candidate periodic quasars that have been proposed as candidates for binary SMBHs Lehto1996,valtonen08,Graham2015a .

PG1302 102 is also a low-redshift ( z = 0.278 ) blazar Massaro2015 . It was detected by the Swift satellite in the X-rays Swift70 . PG 1302 102 shows a 5.2-yr periodic signal with 1.5 cycles covered by the Catalina Real-Time survey see also Liutt2018,Zhu2020 Graham2015a . Doppler boosting has been proposed to explain the optical light curve periodicity DOrazio2015,Xin2020a,Song2021 . Unlike our targets of DES--SDSS-selected candidate periodic quasars, OJ287 and PG 1302 102 have strong radio emission with flux densities on the order of hundreds of mJy.
They share a similar core-jet structure as seen in both the optical and radio observations Hutchings1994,Benitez1996 .

sources.summary: The paper studies PG 1302-102 as a periodic quasar whose optical and infrared variability supports a subparsec binary black hole and dusty torus echo interpretation.

quotes.quote: Infrared Time Lags for the Periodic Quasar PG 1302-102 Hyunsung D. Jun 1,2 , Daniel Stern 1 , Matthew J. Graham 3 , S. G. Djorgovski 3 , Amy Mainzer 1 , Roc M. Cutri 4 , Andrew J. Drake 3 Ashish A. Mahabal 3 1 Jet Propulsion Laboratory, California Institute of Technology, 4800 Oak Grove Drive, Pasadena, CA 91109, USA [ e-mail: hyunsung.jun@jpl.nasa.gov ] 2 NASA Postdoctoral Program Fellow 3 California Institute of Technology, 1216 E. California Blvd., Pasadena, CA 91125, USA 4 Infrared Processing and Analysis Center, California Institute of Technology, Pasadena, CA 91125, USA The optical light curve of the quasar \ at z = 0.278 shows a strong, smooth 5.2 yr periodic signal, detectable over a period of 20 yr. Although the interpretation of this phenomenon is still uncertain, the most plausible mechanisms involve a binary system of two supermassive black holes with a subparsec separation. At this close separation, the nuclear black holes in \ will likely merge within 10^ 5 yr due to gravitational wave emission alone.

Compiling data from \ and , we confirm that the periodic behavior reported in the optical light curve from Gra15a is reproduced at infrared wavelengths, with best-fit observed-frame 3.4 and 4.6 m time lags of (2219 153, 2408 148) days for a near face-on orientation of the torus, or (4103 153, 4292 148) days for an inclined system with relativistic Doppler boosting in effect. The periodicity in the infrared light curves and the light-travel time of the accretion disk photons to reach the dust glowing regions support that a source within the accretion disk is responsible for the optical variability of , echoed at the further out dusty regions. The implied distance of this dusty, assumed toroidal region is 1.5 pc for a near face-on geometry, or 1.1 pc for the relativistic Doppler boosted case.

sources.summary: The paper models PG1302-102 as a quasar and supermassive black hole binary candidate whose optical light curve shows periodicity plus a disk perturbation.

quotes.quote: The photometric light curve of PG1302-102 shows periodic variability which makes this object one of the most plausible supermassive black hole binary candidate. Interestingly, the most recent study of its updated optical light curve reports a decrease in a significance of periodicity which may suggest that the binary model is less favorable. Here, we model the PG 1302-102 light curve, spanning almost 20 years, with a supermassive black hole binary system in which a perturbation in the accretion disk of more massive component is present.

A particularly appealing recent case is the quasar PG1302-102 2015Natur.518...74G . By comparison with other SMBHB candidates, PG1302-102 photometric light curve resembles rather sinusoidal structure. Still, its light curve is not strictly periodic 2015Natur.525..351D .

sources.summary: The paper uses quasar PG 1302 102 as a prominent periodic binary-AGN candidate example, applying outlier mitigation to its CRTS, ASAS-SN, and LINEAR photometric lightcurves.

quotes.quote: We further adapt our outlier modeling to photometric AGN lightcurves, and deploy it on the binary-AGN candidate PG 1302 102 using datasets from CRTS, the All-Sky Automated Survey for Supernovae (ASAS-SN), and the Lincoln Near-Earth Asteroid Research (LINEAR).

SMBHB candidate: PG 1302 102 We choose one of the most prominent binary candidates among periodically-variable quasars: PG 1302 102 . This z=0.2784 quasar with median V band magnitude of 15.0 was the most significant periodic candidate identified by Graham_2015a among the 111 found in the 243,500 quasar Catalina Real-time Transient Survey (CRTS) dataset http://crts.caltech.edu/ http://crts.caltech.edu/ 2009ApJ...696..870D . With a 1884 day period, this system has been interpreted as a binary with Doppler-boosted emission from the secondary black-hole's mini-disk 2015Natur.525..351D,Graham_2015b,2015MNRAS.454L..21C . However, there is ongoing discussion over whether a binary model is truly favored over just intrinsic stochastic variability that appears periodic by happenstance over a short baseline e.g., 2016MNRAS.461.3145V,2020MNRAS.496.1683X,2018ApJ...859L..12L . Regardless of its true nature, we employ this binary candidate as an example to showcase our outlier mitigation approach embedded within the photometric data likelihood.

sources.summary: The paper describes PKS 1302-102 as a radio-loud flat-spectrum quasar in a possibly disturbed elliptical host, with nearby companions and Fundamental Plane properties between radio-loud and radio-quiet hosts.

quotes.quote: PKS 1302-102 PKS 1302-102 (labeled 5 in our plots) is a radio-loud, flat-spectrum, quasar at M _ V = 25.9 and L _ 5 GHz =9.5x10 ^ 42 erg s ^ 1 . The host galaxy, at M _ V = 22.9, is classified as an elliptical that may be slightly disturbed by two companions within 2.3 hutchings92,bahcall97, hamilton02, guyon06 . Bahcall et al. visually classify this galaxy as an E4, however they find that an exponential disk profile best fits the PSF-subtracted image. guyon06 comment on the difficulty of decoupling the host and its closer companion, but find that their 2-D fit favors an ^ 1/4 model. On the Fundamental Plane this host occupies a transition region between our radio-loud and radio-quiet objects. Its velocity dispersion (388 km s ^ 1 ) is on the high end of our radio-loud hosts, however the surface brightness (19.56 mag arcsec ^ 1 ) is higher than all other radio-loud hosts, and near that of our radio-quiet QSO hosts.

sources.summary: The paper models PG 1302-102 as a compact unequal-mass binary black hole whose optical sinusoidal variability can be explained by relativistic Doppler boosting.

quotes.quote: Although they cannot be imaged, periodicities in the light curves of quasars have been interpreted as evidence for binaries , most recently in PG 1302-102, with a short rest-frame optical period of 4 yr . If the orbital period matches this value, then for the range of estimated black hole masses the components would be separated by 0.007-0.017 pc, implying relativistic orbital speeds. There has been much debate over whether black hole orbits could be smaller than 1 pc . Here we show that the amplitude and the sinusoid-like shape of the variability of PG 1302-102 can be fit by relativistic Doppler boosting of emission from a compact, steadily accreting, unequal-mass binary. We predict that brightness variations in the ultraviolet light curve track those in the optical, but with a 2-3 times larger amplitude.

sources.summary: The paper presents PG 1302-102 as the first periodic quasar and describes Doppler-boost modeling in which an unequal-mass binary can fit its optical light curve and UV-to-optical variability amplitudes.

quotes.quote: Several such studies followed the discovery of the first quasar with periodic variability (PG 1302-102; Graham2015Nature ), including the search for multiple periodic components in the optical variability, as expected from hydrodynamical simulations Charisi2015,Dorazio2015 , the analysis of the helical structure in the radio jet of PG 1302-102 Kun2015,Mohan2016 , and the detection of periodic variability in the infrared Jun2015,Dorazio2017 .

Dorazio2015Nature proposed this model to explain the variability of the periodic candidate PG 1302-102. In particular, they found that if PG 1302-102 hosts an unequal mass binary ( q 0.05 ), orbiting not too far from edge-on ( 30^ ), the Doppler boost should dominate the variability. The model can successfully fit the observed optical light curve. Additionally, with archival data from the Galaxy Evolution Explorer (GALEX) and the Hubble Space Telescope (HST), they showed that the variability amplitudes in the near-UV (nUV) and far-UV (fUV) bands are A_ nUV /A_ opt 2.13 and A_ fUV /A_ opt 2.63 , respectively, consistent with the prediction from eq. ( eq:DooplerBoost ).

sources.summary: The paper describes PG1302-102 as a quasar SMBHB candidate with optical and infrared periodicity, estimating its strain and suggesting SKA2 targeted searches may detect it.

quotes.quote: Another prominent object is an unequal-mass SMBHB candidate in quasar PG1302-102. It emerged from a systematic search in CRTS for its quasi-sinusoidal optical variability Graham2015a possibly due to the fact that the emissions from its mini-disc are Doppler boosted Dorazio2015Nature,Xin2019 .

However in the case of PG1302-102, we allow ourselves to be cautiously optimistic. The strain of the candidate SMBHB in this galaxy is h 9 10^ 18 at 14 nHz Graham2015a . Using the detection strategies we describe here, complemented by a targeted search for the additional factor of two Arzoumanian2020 from performing a targeted GW search, may make it detectable with SKA2.

sources.summary: The paper treats PG1302-102 as a BBH quasar candidate, analyzes archived UV broad emission lines, finds Gaussian non-periodic profiles, and infers a face-on BLR misaligned with the putative binary plane.

quotes.quote: PG1302-102 is thought to be a supermassive binary black hole (BBH) system according to the periodical variations of its optical and UV photometry, which may be interpreted as being due to the relativistic Doppler boosting of the emission mainly from the disk around the secondary black hole (BH) modulated by its orbital motion. In this paper, we investigate several broad emission lines of PG1302-102 using archived UV spectra obtained by IUE, GALEX, and Hubble, to reveal the broad-line region (BLR) emission properties of this BBH system under the Doppler boosting scenario. We find that the broad lines Ly , , , and \ all show Gaussian profiles, and none of these lines exhibits obvious periodical variation. Adopting a simple model for the BLR, we perform Markov chain Monte Carlo fittings to these broad lines, and find that the BLR must be viewed at an orientation angle of 33^ , close to face-on. If the Doppler boosting interpretation is correct, then the BLR is misaligned with the BBH orbital plane by an angle of 51^ , which suggests that the Doppler boosted continuum variation has little effect on the broad-line emission and thus does not lead to periodical line variation.

sources.summary: The paper presents PG 1302-102 as a debated periodic quasar and BBH candidate whose sinusoidal light curves may reflect Doppler boosting or accretion-rate modulation.

quotes.quote: Among the BBH candidates, one of the currently hotly debated ones is PG 1302-102, with periodic optical/UV/radio variations on a period of 5 yr see but 2018ApJ...859L..12L Graham15, 2018A&A...615A.123Q, 2020MNRAS.496.1683X . Its sinusoid-like light curves can be well explained by the relativistic Doppler-boosted/-weakened emission mainly from the accretion disk around the secondary component of a BBH system 2015Natur.525..351D . However, there may be some deviations of the observed light curves (or line profiles) from those predicted by the Doppler-boosting interpretation as pointed out by 2018A&A...615A.123Q and or 2020MNRAS.491.4023S, 2021A&A...645A..15S 2019ApJ...871...32K .

2018ApJ...859L..12L even doubted the periodicity in the light curves because of some subsequent observations, which pose a significant challenge to the BBH interpretation of PG 1302-102. Furthermore, it has not been ruled out that the ``periodicity'' of optical/UV variation may also occur in a single BH accretion system for some unknown reasons.

sources.summary: The paper uses PG 1302-102 as a periodic AGN case, noting CRTS/ASAS-SN monitoring, a recent flare, gravitational-wave relevance, and a smooth sinusoidal light curve.

quotes.quote: For simulating underlying periodic signal, we assumed that the inferred SMBH mass is the total mass of the hypothetical binary system at mutual distance of 10 ld and that the amplitude is about 14 resembling the case of PG1302-102 2015Natur.525..351D .

For example, we analyzed periodicities for PG 1302 -102 10.3847/1538-4357/aaf731 and Mrk 231 10.1093/mnras/staa737 which were observed photometrically by Catalina Real-time Transient Survey (CRTS) and All-Sky Automated Survey for Supernovae (ASAS-SN). Analyzing Mrk 231 photometric curve, we found that adequate data sampling of ASAS-SN survey is more suitable for periodicity detection 10.1093/mnras/staa737 . Sudden changes in the target light curves can occur, as was the case of PG 1302-102 when unexpected flare appeared recently. This object has been considered as one of the best targets for the next generation of gravitational wave surveys.
Their accretion rates are less bursty; and the cases for q = 0.075 and q = 0.1 binaries in 10.1093/mnras/stt1787 are very similar to PG 1302-102's light-curve, which has smooth sinusoidal appearance.

sources.summary: The paper uses PG 1302-102 as an MBHB candidate whose published optical polarization was too imprecise for variability, but might be testable with modern polarimetry.

quotes.quote: Berriman+1990 presents a compilation of the optical polarization fraction and polarization angles of the PG quasars. The MBHB candidate host PG 1302-102 is contained in this compilation. Two measurements were taken by StockMoorePols:1984 : P=0.18 0.15 P_ = 26 24 deg. (May 21, 1979); P=0.08 0.18 P_ = 55 67 deg. (April 12, 1980). Because this study only aimed for 0.2 uncertainties in the polarization fraction, variability could not be discerned for PG 1302-102. However, modern polarimetry, aiming for precise polarization fraction measurements at the _P 0.01 level could allow a determination of the polarization fraction variability of a candidate such as PG 1302-102, assuming it is indeed powered by a binary MBH.

sources.summary: PG 1302 102 shows a possible merger-remnant northern plume, compact central projected sources, and a two-component Fourier-mode host fit.

quotes.quote: PG 1302 102: There is a plume of extended emission to the north that may be remnant of a merger. Two small, compact sources are projected toward the center of the galaxy. Apart from accounting for the projected sources, we fit the quasar host with two \ components including Fourier modes.

gmw_00001864.name: GLEAM 0917 0012
gmw_00001864.overview: GLEAM 0917 0012 is a high-redshift radio galaxy [arXiv:2203.01349]. Imaging of the host galaxy has detected a companion galaxy located one arcsecond away [arXiv:2203.01349].

sources.summary: The paper presents HST grism and photometry of the GLEAM 0917 0012 host, finding a weak 1.12 micron line and competing redshift solutions near z=2 or z=8.21.

quotes.quote: Our F105W and F0986M imaging observations detect the host of and a companion galaxy, one arcsec away. The G102 grism observations reveal a single weak line in each of the spectra of the host and the companion. To help identify these lines we utilised several photometric redshift techniques including template fitting to the grism spectra, fitting the ultraviolet (UV) to-radio photometry with galaxy templates plus a synchrotron model, fitting of the UV-to-near- infrared (IR) photometry with EAZY , and fitting the radio data alone with RAiSERed .

Including the 1.12 m line seen in the WFC3 spectrum, the host galaxy of GLEAM 0917 0012 most likely lies at either z=2.01 or 8.21 . The EAZY fitting supports the lower of these two solutions and the RAiSERed fitting supports the higher. The best-fitting UV-to-radio templates at z 8 , which have a strong Lyman break around 1.1 m, are more consistent with the strength of the faint feature seen in the grism spectra than the best-fitting templates at z 2 .

gmw_00001865.name: companion galaxy
gmw_00001865.overview: The companion galaxy is located one arcsecond from GLEAM 0917 0012 [arXiv:2203.01349]. Photometric fitting of this emission line galaxy generally favors a low-redshift solution below z=3 [arXiv:2203.01349].

sources.summary: The companion galaxy one arcsecond from GLEAM 0917 0012 has a weak 0.98 micron line, optical detections, and favored low-redshift solutions below z=3.

quotes.quote: For the companion galaxy the results of this fitting (Fig. fig:compfit ) for the different templates are also broadly consistent between the top three panels. These solutions generally favour a `low'-redshift ( z<3 ) result compared to the potential high-redshift solution for the host galaxy. In these panels, there is a broad distribution of high likelihoods across 0<z<3.5 . A local maxima is highlighted in Fig. fig:compfit with the corresponding template overlaid on the data. Likewise with the LBG templates there is broad region of high likelihood.

These results on their own do not further constrain the redshift of the companion. EAZY We are able to run EAZY on both the host and companion using only the optical to near-IR measurements . The results are reported in Table tab:z . For the companion, EAZY finds a low-redshift solution of z 2.6 , compatible with the EAZY result from D21 within the uncertainties.

gmw_00001866.name: Ton202
gmw_00001866.overview: Ton202 is a radio-loud quasar [arXiv:astro-ph/0510858, arXiv:2308.16780] that exhibits a Fanaroff-Riley type II radio structure on kiloparsec scales [arXiv:2308.16780] and signatures of a warm absorber in its soft X-ray spectrum [arXiv:astro-ph/0510858]. The active nucleus shows multi-band flux variability [arXiv:1604.04626], tentative short-timescale X-ray line variability [arXiv:astro-ph/0510858], and real variations in its polarization [arXiv:astro-ph/0408105].

sources.summary: The paper reports Ton202 lacked its earlier dramatic Balmer-edge feature in 2003 and interprets the two-epoch difference as real polarization variability.

quotes.quote: Ton202 This object was found to show a Balmer edge feature in the polarized flux, when observed in May 2002 (Paper I). However, we do not find such a dramatic feature in our data taken in May 2003, as we show in Fig. res-Ton202 . The polarized flux looks quite featureless at 4000 . (At the red end wavelengths longer than 6400 , the polarized flux shape become convex, which could be due to second-order light.)

This difference in two epochs seems to be due to a real variation in polarization. The upper panel of Fig. Ton202-dif shows the difference in polarization in these two epochs (more accurately, difference in normalized Stokes q rotated to the object's polarization PA at each epoch; note that the sky PA of the May 2002 observation could not be determined due to some calibration problem; see Paper I).

sources.summary: The paper reports XMM-Newton evidence that PG 1425+267 has a double-peaked Fe K line, tentative short-timescale variability, and warm absorber signatures.

quotes.quote: We report results from the XMM--Newton observation of the radio--loud quasar PG 1425+267 (z=0.366). The EPIC--pn data above 2 keV exhibit a double--peaked emission feature in the Fe K band. The higher energy peak is found at 6.4 keV and is consistent with being narrow, while the lower energy one is detected at 5.3 keV and is much broader than the detector resolution.

We also perform a time--resolved analysis searching for short timescale variability of the emission line. Results tentatively suggest that the line is indeed variable on short timescales (at the 97.3 per cent confidence level according to simulations) and better quality data are needed to confirm it on more firm statistical grounds. We also detect clear signatures of a warm absorber in the soft X--ray energy band.

sources.summary: The paper monitors Ton 202 as one of four polarization-decreasing quasars, finding five-band flux variability and variable spectra much bluer than the polarized flux spectra.

quotes.quote: To examine whether the disk continuum spectral shapes revealed as the polarized flux and as the variable component spectra are consistent with each other, we carry out multi-band photometric monitoring observations for a sample of four polarization-decreasing quasars of kis04 (4C09.72, 3C323.1, Ton 202, and B2 1208+32) to derive the variable component spectra and compare the spectral shape of them with that of the polarized flux spectra. Contrary to expectation, we confirm that the two spectral components of these quasars have totally different spectral shape in that the variable component spectra are significantly bluer compared to the polarized flux spectra.

We carried out u , g , , i , and z bands photometric monitoring observations for four of the five quasars with polarization dip in <4000 \ in kis04 's sample (4C09.72, B2 1208+32, Ton 202, and 3C323.1; see Table obstarget ) from April 2015 to February 2016, using the 1.05-meter Kiso Schmidt telescope being operated by the Kiso Observatory of the University of Tokyo.

We confirm that all of the four quasars show flux variability in all five bands during our observations, although the variability amplitude in B2 1208+32 is relatively small compared to the others. The observed variability amplitudes of g 0.1-0.4 mag within 200 days in the quasar rest-frame are consistent with the general property of the SDSS quasars (e.g., ).

sources.summary: The paper reports a Balmer edge in Ton 202's polarized quasar flux, arguing that the feature reveals intrinsic, thermal, optically thick Big Blue Bump emission.

quotes.quote: We have found for the first time a Balmer edge feature in the polarized flux spectrum of a quasar (Ton 202). The edge feature is seen as a discontinuity in the slope, rather than as a discontinuity in the absolute flux. Since the polarized flux contains essentially no broad emission lines, it is considered to arise interior to the broad emission line region, showing the spectrum with all the emissions outside the nucleus scraped off and removed. Therefore, the polarized flux spectrum is likely to reveal features intrinsic to the Big Blue Bump emission. In this case, the existence of the Balmer edge feature, seen in absorption in the shorter wavelength side, indicates that the Big Blue Bump is indeed thermal and optically thick.

sources.summary: The paper presents near-infrared polarimetry of Ton202, finding a blue polarized-flux slope, consistent optical and near-infrared polarization angles, and declining polarization toward longer wavelengths.

quotes.quote: We present the results of near-infrared polarimetry for such three quasars (Ton202, 4C37.43, B2 1208+32). The data for Ton202 have the highest S/N, and the near-infrared polarized flux in this case is measured to have quite a blue shape, F_ ^ +0.42 0.29 , intriguingly consistent with the simple multi-temperature black body, bare disk prediction of ^ +1/3 .

For one of these three objects, Ton202, we detected near-IR polarization with S/N larger than 3 in three bands in 2001, and the data obtained in 2005 in J and K' bands show consistent polarization with much higher S/N (we note that K' band total flux might have varied - see Table tab-pol ). The polarization degree in the near-IR is generally lower than in the optical, and it seems to be decreasing toward longer wavelengths in the near-IR at least in the highest S/N case of Ton202. The improved data for Ton202 taken in 2005 show that the polarized flux slope in the near-IR alone, F_ ^ +0.42 0.29 , is also quite blue compared to the shape in the optical.

sources.summary: PG 1425+267 is one of the radio-loud quasars with FR II kiloparsec structure and resolved parsec-scale core-jet emission with high brightness temperature and variability.

quotes.quote: We have also studied the radio-emitting properties of the four RLQs. On kiloparsec scales, all four RLQs display Fanaroff-Riley type II radio structures 1993MNRAS.263..425M,1994AJ....108.1163K . On parsec scales, three RLQs (PG 1004+130, PG1048-090, PG 1425+267) exhibit resolved structures, featuring a bright and compact component located at one end of an elongated structure (Figure fig:RLQ ). This brightest component exhibits a high brightness temperature ( >10^9 K), a flat spectrum ( 0.40< ^ 7.6 _ 1.6 <-0.25 ) (Table table:Spectral_Index ), and shows significant variability (Figure fig:SED-RLQ ).

gmw_00001867.name: 4C37.43
gmw_00001867.overview: The active galaxy 4C37.43 is a radio-loud quasar [arXiv:astro-ph/9712009] that exhibits unpolarized broad lines [arXiv:astro-ph/0509341] and variable Balmer-line structure [arXiv:astro-ph/9712009]. It features extended double-lobed radio emission [arXiv:astro-ph/9712009] and spatially extended oxygen emission [arXiv:astro-ph/0509341].

sources.summary: The paper presents PG 1512+370 as a radio-loud quasar with variable Balmer-line structure, aliases B2 1512+37 and 4C 37.43, and extended double-lobed radio emission.

quotes.quote: We present spectroscopic observations of the quasar PG 1512+370, covering the \ line spectral range and collected at moderate resolution (2-7 \ FWHM) from 1988 to 1996. The observations show that the blue wing of the \ broad profile component has changed significantly in flux and shape between 1988 and 1990 and between 1995 and 1996. A displaced blue peak on the \ profile, visible in 1988, but not in the 1990-1995 spectra, is revealed again in one of the spectra obtained in 1996. The blue peak (in both the 1988 and 1996 spectra) is centered at v_r 3000^ +500 _ 500 \ from the rest frame defined by the narrow component of , and the [ O iii ] 4959,5007 lines. We discuss several conflicting interpretations of the data. We find that the variability of the \ blue wing is consistent with Balmer line emission from regions whose motion is predominantly radial, if variations of the blue wing are a response to continuum changes. Alternatively, we note that observed \ line profile variations are consistent with a variable line component as in a ``binary black hole'' scenario. More frequent observations of \ are needed to distinguish among these hypotheses.

At 20 cm, on a FIRST map ( ), PG 1512+370 appears as a double-lobed radio source, with lobes extending up to a projected linear distance of 400 kpc from the central core, at P. A. 105 ^ . Integral field spectroscopy revealed two \ emitting regions approximately aligned along the East-West direction with the nucleus ( ). Our 1996 spectrum also revealed the eastern emitting region as a ``blob'', at v_r 300 , in excellent agreement with the results of Durret et al. (1994).

sources.summary: The paper includes 4C37.43 among quasars with unpolarized broad lines, scales its spectra using nuclear oxygen emission, and notes extended [OIII] flux and polarization alignment.

quotes.quote: We present the results of near-infrared polarimetry for such three quasars (Ton202, 4C37.43, B2 1208+32). Quasars with unpolarized broad lines Results The primary objects here are the ones which have been found to have unpolarized broad lines Ki03,Ki04 , namely Ton202, 4C37.43, and B2 1208+32 in our present sample.

Therefore, for Ton202 and 4C37.43, we have scaled the flux by comparing the flux of the nuclear [ OIII ] 4959 and 5007 lines in our spectra with those of Sh03 (the [ OIII ] flux is known to be spatially extended in these objects; SM87 ). (For our 4C37.43 data, we re-extracted the spectrum with a smaller window to measure the nuclear [ OIII ] flux.) The resulting nuclear [ OIII ] flux for each object was further checked with the nuclear [ OIII ] flux measurements of SM87 . However, the uncertainty in this scaling might be somewhat larger than this, especially for 4C37.43 which has quite a large extended [ OIII ] flux. The PA is roughly the same as those measured in the optical in all three objects, and are all roughly parallel (within about 30 ) to the radio structural axis PAs which are 53 , 109 , and 3 \ for Ton202, 4C37.43, and B2 1208+32, respectively (see Ki04 for references).

sources.summary: The paper presents 4C37.43 as the lower-S/N comparison quasar, finding possible weak broad H emission and only an unclear, slight polarized-flux slope change.

quotes.quote: The data for 4C37.43 ( z=0.371 ) are shown in Figures 4C37.43_qu and 4C37.43_PF in the same manner as for Ton 202. The data are of lower S/N compared to those for Ton 202, but there might be a weak broad H emission line in the polarized flux in this object. This means that there might also be some contribution from the small blue bump in the polarized flux of this object, making our scraping method incomplete. Apparently, there does not seem to be a clear slope change in the polarized flux, but the plot in F_ (Fig. 4C37.43_nFn ) might suggest a very slight slope change again around 4000 . This is still unclear in the slope measurements with a broken power law: = -0.17 0.11 where F_ ^ in the blue side (2800-4000 \ in the rest frame) and = +0.06 0.23 in the red side (4000-4700 , excluding the possible broad line component and the red-end portion).

gmw_00001868.name: B2 1208+32
gmw_00001868.overview: B2 1208+32 is a radio-loud quasar [arXiv:astro-ph/0509341] that exhibits unpolarized broad emission lines [arXiv:astro-ph/0509341] and a polarized flux feature in the Balmer-edge region [arXiv:astro-ph/0408105]. The active nucleus shows flux variability across five photometric bands [arXiv:1604.04626], with its variable spectral component being significantly bluer than its polarized flux spectrum [arXiv:1604.04626].

sources.summary: The paper finds B2 1208+32 has only small likely ISP effects and a familiar Balmer-edge polarized-flux feature with a pronounced 3600 upturn.

quotes.quote: B2 1208+32 ISP effect ^1 In this object, a small PA rotation shortward of 4000 \ is again seen (Fig. res-B2_1208+32 ) and the data points on the q u plane also roughly align linearly (Fig. res-B2_1208+32-qu ), though not too cleanly. We do not have our own observation of a Galactic star in the field, but the data in the literature indicate that stars with estimated distances of 400-500pc show a small polarization ( 0.25 ) at PA 0-25 . If there is any effect of ISP around at this PA, it is expected to be small, judging from the location of the data points on the q u plane. However, we note that the continuum PA rotation direction and the line polarization direction are not in the same sense, which suggests that one of them is intrinsic.

Uncorrected spectra Fig. res-B2_1208+32 shows the measurements without any ISP correction, and the intrinsic polarization is expected to be qualitatively the same, since the ISP effect is thought to be small. The measured small line polarization (polarized at different PA from the continuum) could either be intrinsic or entirely due to an ISP. The polarized flux shows a similar, now-familiar feature in the Balmer edge region, and the up-turn at 3600 \ in this object looks more pronounced.

sources.summary: The paper reports near-infrared polarimetry of B2 1208+32, noting limited prior K-band data, low signal-to-noise measurements, coherent position angles, and radio-axis alignment.

quotes.quote: We present the results of near-infrared polarimetry for such three quasars (Ton202, 4C37.43, B2 1208+32). The sample was selected mainly based on the known polarization properties: they are all known to be polarized in the optical with 1-2 level except for 3C206 St84,Be90 , and all have previous polarization measurements at K band except for B2 1208+32 although with rather large uncertainties SZ91 . Quasars with unpolarized broad lines Results The primary objects here are the ones which have been found to have unpolarized broad lines Ki03,Ki04 , namely Ton202, 4C37.43, and B2 1208+32 in our present sample.

For B2 1208+32, we did not make any adjustment due to the unavailability of such data. For the other two objects, especially for B2 1208+32, the measurements are still of low S/N, but the data show coherent PAs in multiple bands. The PA is roughly the same as those measured in the optical in all three objects, and are all roughly parallel (within about 30 ) to the radio structural axis PAs which are 53 , 109 , and 3 \ for Ton202, 4C37.43, and B2 1208+32, respectively (see Ki04 for references).

sources.summary: The paper monitors B2 1208+32 as one of four polarization-decreasing quasars, finding five-band flux variability and variable spectra much bluer than the polarized flux spectra.

quotes.quote: To examine whether the disk continuum spectral shapes revealed as the polarized flux and as the variable component spectra are consistent with each other, we carry out multi-band photometric monitoring observations for a sample of four polarization-decreasing quasars of kis04 (4C09.72, 3C323.1, Ton 202, and B2 1208+32) to derive the variable component spectra and compare the spectral shape of them with that of the polarized flux spectra. Contrary to expectation, we confirm that the two spectral components of these quasars have totally different spectral shape in that the variable component spectra are significantly bluer compared to the polarized flux spectra.

We carried out u , g , , i , and z bands photometric monitoring observations for four of the five quasars with polarization dip in <4000 \ in kis04 's sample (4C09.72, B2 1208+32, Ton 202, and 3C323.1; see Table obstarget ) from April 2015 to February 2016, using the 1.05-meter Kiso Schmidt telescope being operated by the Kiso Observatory of the University of Tokyo.

We confirm that all of the four quasars show flux variability in all five bands during our observations, although the variability amplitude in B2 1208+32 is relatively small compared to the others. The observed variability amplitudes of g 0.1-0.4 mag within 200 days in the quasar rest-frame are consistent with the general property of the SDSS quasars (e.g., ).

gmw_00001869.name: 3C234
gmw_00001869.overview: 3C234 is a high-excitation Fanaroff-Riley II radio galaxy [arXiv:0712.0595, arXiv:1210.6027] containing a hidden Type 1 quasar nucleus [arXiv:0712.3445, arXiv:2308.04621] and a compact scattering region [arXiv:astro-ph/0207654]. The host galaxy features a tidal arm [arXiv:0712.0595, arXiv:1210.6027], extended patches of disturbed ionized gas [arXiv:1811.04946], and a mix of dust at various temperatures [arXiv:1508.03287].

sources.summary: The paper reports XMM-Newton spectroscopy of 3C 234, finding a hidden broad-line region, strong absorption, a neutral emission line, line-rich soft X-rays, and a hidden quasar.

quotes.quote: We report results on a 40 ks observation of the Type 2 quasar 3C 234. Optical spectropolarimetric data have demonstrated the presence of a hidden broad-line region in this powerful (M _V 24.2 after reddening and starlight correction) narrow-line FRII radio galaxy. Our analysis is aimed at investigating the X-ray spectral properties of this peculiar source which have remained poorly known so far.

The X-ray spectrum of this radio-loud quasar is typical of a local Compton-thin Seyfert 2 galaxy. It exhibits strong absorption ( 3.5 10 ^ 23 2) and a narrow, neutral emission line with an equivalent width of 140 40 eV. Our observation also reveals that the soft portion of the spectrum is characterized by strong emission lines with a very low level of scattered primary continuum.

This X-ray observation has definitively confirmed the presence of a hidden quasar in 3C 234. The line-rich spectrum and the steepness of the hard X-ray continuum ( 1.7) found in this source weaken the hypothesis that the bulk of the X-ray emission in radio-loud AGNs with high excitation optical lines arises from jet non-thermal emission.

sources.summary: The essay describes 3C234 as the radio galaxy where spectropolarimetry revealed polarization orthogonal to the jet, polarized broad H emission, and a hidden Type 1 nucleus.

quotes.quote: The 3C234 radio galaxy has a jet position angle of 68^ degrees, to which I'd assigned an uncertainty of 5^ , which was somewhat arbitrary and a bit generous. We measured a polarization position angle of 159 1^ , almost exactly orthogonal to the radio axis, and we eventually established a ``perpendicular" class of AGN.

The Stokes spectra have been smoothed obviously, but it's very clear that the [ O III ] 5007 \ line hardly shows up in the difference spectrum and is thus unpolarized. But the H line behaves differentlyIt is highly polarized, and accounting for unpolarized narrow components and starlight, the broad component polarization is a whopping 20 .

Emission lines are only intrinsically polarized in very special circumstances, so I realized this meant we went looking at a hidden broad line (high density; permitted lines only) region, through a periscopic mirrorIn scattering polarization, physics tells us that the perpendicular to the position angle is the direction of the photon's last flight before being scattered into the line of sight . Thus these photons could have escaped the hidden nucleus only if they were travelling parallel to the radio axis. Since line emission is intrinsically quite isotropic, it follows that the other directions, the equatorial directions, must be blocked. That is, an opaque equatorial structure must be hiding the Type 1 nucleus.

sources.summary: For 3CR 234, the paper reports aperture photometry and K-band nuclear luminosity consistent with previous work, and connects its polarized optical/NIR light to scattering plus torus extinction.

quotes.quote: 3CR 234 : Our 3 aperture photometry is about 0.4 mag larger than the value reported in SWW00. However, using their best fit parameters for the host+nucleus decomposition, we estimate a 3 aperture photometry of K =13.49 0.05, perfectly consistent with our value. SWW00 measured L_ K =10 ^ 44.32 , a factor 1.8 larger than our measurement, while taylor96 measured L_ K =10 ^ 44.2 . These two values are consistent within the errors, and this is not surprising as this source is dominated by the nuclear emission, and are also consistent with our value.
