This source is the radio galaxy 4C-00.62 (Aperture 010), which motivated the original search by kajisawa06 and kodama07 for a protocluster at this location. The X-ray luminosity of 4C-00.62 is L_X [ 2-10 keV ] = 2 10^ 45 erg s ^ 1 .
The one exception is that the field was selected to contain the radio galaxy 4C-00.62. If we remove this object, then the AGN fraction is 1.0^ +2.3 _ 0.8 .

gmw_00001884.name: MG 1747 + 182
gmw_00001884.overview: MG 1747 + 182 is a radio galaxy at a redshift of z = 2.29 [arXiv:1610.02057]. It hosts warm ionized gas extending over tens of kiloparsecs, which is well aligned with its radio lobes and exhibits a highly irregular velocity gradient [arXiv:1610.02057].

sources.summary: MG 1747 + 182 has warm ionized gas over tens of kiloparsecs, multiple detected lines, a large irregular velocity gradient, broad line widths, and radio lobes aligned with the gas.

quotes.quote: 3C 362 (MG 1747 + 182) The warm ionized gas in MG 1747 + 182 at z = 2.29 extends over 4.0 2.2 along the major and minor axis, respectively (corresponding to a deconvolved size of 31 15 kpc at z = 2.29). The emission-line gas extends along a north-east to south-west axis. The integrated spectrum (Fig. fig:intspec ) shows [ OIII ] 4959,5007, H , H , [ NII ] 6583, and [ SII ] 6716,6731. The two components of the [ SII ] doublet are blended because of the large line width of 980 km s ^ 1 in the integrated spectrum.

The total velocity gradient of [ OIII ] 5007 in MG 1747 + 182 is about 600 km s ^ 1 , and the velocity field is not very regular. We find a redshifted peak at about 8 kpc from the center of the emission-line region, and velocity minima are found near the north-eastern and south-western edge of line emission with velocities of about -300 km s ^ 1 each. The line widths are highest in the south-west (FWHM 1500 km s ^ 1 ), and about 1000 km s ^ 1 in other parts of the gas, with two dips (FWHM 500 km s ^ 1 ) north and south from the center. The source has two radio lobes that are detected outside of the emission-line gas, delineating a jet axis that is well aligned with the major axis of the emission-line gas (Fig. fig:maps3 ).

gmw_00001885.name: MP J1758-6738
gmw_00001885.overview: MP J1758-6738 is a galaxy at z = 2.03 with an emission-line morphology dominated by a resolved, nearly circular central region and a fainter eastern extension [arXiv:1610.02057]. The galaxy exhibits a large velocity offset of about 800 km/s between these two regions, and its radio map shows two hotspots aligned with the major axis of the gas [arXiv:1610.02057].

sources.summary: MP J1758-6738 has a circular bright emission region plus an eastern extension, large velocity separation between regions, broad line widths, and radio hotspots aligned with the gas.

quotes.quote: MP J1758 6738 The emission-line morphology of MP J1758-6738 at z = 2.03 is dominated by a resolved, nearly circular, high-surface brightness region with a diameter of 2.4 \ centered on the nucleus (which we associated with the continuum source), and a fainter extension towards east (Fig. fig:maps3 ). The velocity offset between these two regions is about 800 km s ^ 1 , and velocities are fairly uniform (with offsets 100 km s ^ 1 ) within each region. FWHM line widths are of-order 700-800 km s ^ 1 throughout the central regions of the source, and narrower (500 600 km s ^ 1 ) near the very periphery of the faint emission-line region.

At z = 2.03, H and [ OIII ] 4959 fall outside the H-band, and we only detect [ OIII ] 5007, H , [ NII ] 6583, and [ SII ] 6716,6731 (Fig. fig:maps3 ). With FWHM 600 km s ^ 1 , the H and [ NII ] 6583 lines and the two lines of the [ SII ] doublet are blended, but can nonetheless be individually identified. We only have a low-resolution radio map of this source with a 24 \ beam (Table tab:radioobs ), which shows two hotspots well outside the emission-line gas, and roughly aligned with the major axis of the gas.

gmw_00001886.name: TN J2007 1316
gmw_00001886.overview: TN J2007 1316 is a high-redshift radio galaxy located at z = 3.8 [arXiv:1610.02057, arXiv:1301.1983]. It features a high star formation rate [arXiv:1610.02057] and two extended radio lobes that align with the major axis of its compact emission-line gas [arXiv:1610.02057].

sources.summary: TN J2007 1316 is among the highest-redshift sources in the sample, with compact line emission, broad integrated lines, aligned radio lobes, and high star formation rate discussion.

quotes.quote: TN J2007 1316 TN J2007 1316 at z = 3.8 is amongst our highest-redshift sources. Our integrated spectrum covers [ OII ] 3727, [ OIII ] 4363,4959,5007, and H (Fig. fig:intspec ). Line emission is compact at a spatial resolution of 0.9 0.8 . Collet, 2014, PhD thesis.) The line width in the integrated spectrum, FWHM = 950 km s ^ 1 corresponds to the FWHM found also in the map of Fig. fig:maps .

The radio source shows two extended lobes with a relative distance of 3.3 . The axis of the radio source is well aligned with the major axis of the emission-line gas, and nearly perpendicular to the small velocity gradient seen in the gas. TN J2007 1316 is one of the galaxies discussed in drouart14 and rocca13 with a high star formation rate.

sources.summary: The paper models TN J2007 1316 as a z=3.84 radio galaxy with weak AGN contamination whose SED requires both a young starburst and an older massive early-type component.

quotes.quote: Using the new evolutionary code .3, we made an evolutionary spectral synthesis of the optical-IR-submm spectral energy distribution of two distant ( z=3.8 ) radio galaxies, 4C 41.17 and TN J2007 1316. These two radio galaxies were selected from the HeRG E (Herschel Radio Galaxies Evolution) Project in particular for their faint AGN contribution and because they show evidence of a large stellar contribution to their bolometric luminosity. We chose two radio galaxies in particular, 4C 41.17 and TN J2007 1316, both at z=3.8, because of their small AGN contribution to the overall SED.

TN J2007 1316 (or WN J2007 1316)is also an ultra steep spectrum radio source selected from the 365 MHz TEXAS survey dou96 and the NVSS survey con98 . It was also detected in the 352 MHz WISH survey and is part of the USS sample deb02b . The best fits are that of an S0 galaxy with an age of 700 Myr for 4C 41.17 and an elliptical galaxy scenario with an age of 1200 Myrs for TNJ 2007 1316.

sources.summary: The paper models TN J2007-1316 as a z=3.8 radio galaxy with SED-derived young and old stellar populations whose cumulative supernova-remnant masses are comparable to high-redshift SMBH masses.

quotes.quote: In this paper, we show that the supernova remnant (SNR) masses cumulated from core-collapse supernovae along the star formation history of two powerful z=3.8 radio galaxies 4C41.17 and TN J2007-1316 reach up to 10^9 M_ , comparable with supermassive black hole (SMBH) masses measured from the SDSS sample at similar redshifts. The SNR mass is measured from the already exploded supernova mass after subtraction of ejecta while the mass of still luminous stars fits at best the observed spectral energy distribution (SED), continuously extended to the optical-Spitzer-Herschel-submm domains, with the help of the galaxy evolution model P egase.3. For the recent and old stellar populations, SNR masses vary on 10 ^ 9-10 M_ and the SNR-to-star mass ratio between 1 and 0.1 is comparable to the observed low-z SMBH-to-star mass ratio.

Table AgesMasses provides the age, star mass M_ star , SNR mass M_ SNR , the M_ SNR / M_ star \ ratio and metallicity Z of the young (evolved SSP) and old (Elliptical/S0) stellar components for the two radio galaxies, both best-fitted with the respective values of the reduced ^2 =0.96 for 4C41.17 and ^2 = 1.53 for TN J2007-1316. SNR masses vary from 3.4 to 19 10^ 9 M_ with ratios M_ SNR /M_ star varying from 2.1 10^ 3 \ to 4.2 10^ 2 . The striking result is that the supernova remnant mass M_ SNR \ is of a few 10 ^ 9 \ to 10 ^ 10 M _ , quite comparable to supermassive black hole masses.

gmw_00001887.name: MRC 2025-218
gmw_00001887.overview: MRC 2025-218 is a radio galaxy at z=2.63 that hosts an active galactic nucleus with a bright nuclear point source [arXiv:1610.02057]. The galaxy features extended emission-line gas aligned with its radio hotspots, displaying irregular kinematics and very broad line widths [arXiv:1610.02057].

sources.summary: MRC 2025-218 has a bright nuclear point source, extended emission-line gas, aligned radio hotspots, irregular kinematics, and very broad line widths not attributed to broad-line residuals.

quotes.quote: MRC 2025-218 MRC 2025-218 at z=2.63 has a bright nuclear point source and broad H line emission originating from the AGN. humphrey08 found a deep neutral absorber on top of the Ly line emission, which appears to be kinematically detached from the extended emission-line region, and probably probes a massive reservoir of either infalling our outflowing gas. We used the approach described in ssec:blrremoval to remove the nuclear component before fitting the extended line emission. The remaining emission-line region is extended with a size of 4.75 2.25 , corresponding to a deconvolved size of 37 kpc 16 kpc. The source has two bright radio hotspots just outside the emission-line gas, and an axis between them which aligns well with the major axis of the emission line gas.

The gas kinematics are irregular, with a total velocity offset of 900 km s ^ 1 . The FWHM line widths are between 700 and 1600 km s ^ 1 , and the highest velocities are found in two regions, one centered on the nucleus, the other near the periphery of the northern emission-line gas. These widths are greater than those of the H broad-line region, 8024 km s ^ 1 nesvadba11a , so that we do not think that they are residuals from the nuclear broad-line subtraction. We identify [ OIII ] 4959,5007, H , [ NII ] 6583, and [ OI ] 6300 with widths between 500 km s ^ 1 (for [ OI ] 6300) and 1100 km s ^ 1 (for [ OIII ] 4959,5007 and [ NII ] 6583).

gmw_00001888.name: MRC2104 242
gmw_00001888.overview: MRC2104 242 is a high-redshift radio galaxy located at a redshift of z = 2.49 [arXiv:1610.02057, arXiv:1103.5806]. It features an active galactic nucleus that illuminates an adjacent northern gas cloud, alongside a double-lobed radio continuum structure [arXiv:1610.02057, arXiv:1103.5806].

sources.summary: MRC2104 242 has one of the sample’s most extended radio sources, multiple detected lines, two [OIII] gas blobs, an AGN-illuminated northern cloud, and radio-aligned emission.

quotes.quote: MRC 2104-242 MRC2104 242 at z = 2.49 has one of the most extended radio sources in our sample, with a size of 21.8 , corresponding to 176.6 kpc at z = 2.49. We observed this galaxy during run 079.A-0617, in parts with adaptive optics, taking advantage of a nearby bright star, which we used for the tip-tilt correction. We obtained data cubes in the H and K-band individually, detecting [ OIII ] 4959,5007, H , H , [ NII ] 6548,6583, and the [ SII ] 6716,6731 doublet.

The [ OIII ] 5007 line image in Fig. fig:maps shows two gas blobs, and only the southern blob is associated with continuum emission. The northern blob has however a very high [ OIII ] 5007/H ratio, which is not consistent with gas heated by star formation and suggests that this source is a gas cloud that is illuminated by the AGN of MRC 2104-242. We find a regular velocity gradient in the southern cloud with a total velocity offset of 350 km s ^ 1 , and little variation in velocity in the northern blob. The major axis of the overall emission-line region aligns well with the direction of the radio jets in the southern blob.

sources.summary: The paper studies MRC 2104-242 as a z=2.5 high-redshift radio galaxy, finding a molecular-gas upper limit and resolved 33 GHz lobe-dominated continuum without spectral curvature.

quotes.quote: We set a firm upper limit to the mass of molecular gas in MRC 2104-242 ( z = 2.5 ) of M _ H2 < 2 10^ 10 \ ( _ x /0.8) M _ . For MRC 0943-242 ( z=2.9 ) we derive an upper limit of M _ H2 < 6 10^ 10 \ ( _ x /0.8) M _ . We also find a tentative 3 CO detection in the outer part of the giant \ halo that surrounds MRC 0943-242. The 30-33 GHz radio continuum of MRC 2104-242 and MRC 0943-242 is reliably detected. Both radio sources show a spectral index of 1.5 between 1.4 and 30 GHz, with no evidence for spectral curvature within this range of frequencies.

The continuum structure consists of two components on either side of the optical host galaxy, in agreement with 4.7 and 8.2 GHz VLA continuum observations that identified it as a double lobed radio source pen00_radio . The bright northern lobe has a peak flux density of S_ 33 GHz = 2.7 , while the fainter southern lobe has S_ 33 GHz = 0.39 . Even at 33 GHz the radio continuum structure is dominated by the radio lobes and no core component (at the location of the optical nucleus) is seen in our data.

gmw_00001889.name: 4C 23.56
gmw_00001889.overview: The high-redshift radio galaxy 4C 23.56 is a giant system featuring extended X-ray lobes [arXiv:astro-ph/0608656, arXiv:1008.5042] and a biconal emission-line nebula [arXiv:1610.02057, arXiv:1008.5042]. It resides in a strong proto-cluster candidate field [arXiv:astro-ph/0606407] characterized by a factor-of-two overdensity of submillimeter galaxies [arXiv:1806.10291].

sources.summary: 4C 23.56 is a powerful extended radio source with detected rest-frame optical lines, a biconal nebula, very extended line emission, a large velocity gradient, and broad line widths.

quotes.quote: 4C 23.56 4C 23.56 at z = 2.48 is one of our most extended sources in the radio, with a size of 47 , corresponding to 380 kpc. It is also one of the most powerful radio sources in our sample, with a radio power of P_ 500 = 28.9 W Hz ^ 1 at 500 MHz in the rest-frame. With SINFONI we detect [ OIII ] 4959,5007, H , H , and [ NII ] 6583 in this source. 4C 23.56 was one of the first HzRGs where a biconal emission-line nebula was detected knopp97 .

Line emission in our SINFONI cube is very extended in 4C 23.56, mostly along, but also perpendicular to the axis of the radio jet, and roughly centered on the continuum source, which we use as tracer of the nuclear regions. We find a velocity gradient of 1200 km s ^ 1 with the velocity monotonically increasing from the south-west to the north-east. The line widths are FWHM = 700 1000 km s ^ 1 through most of the source, with lower widths of FWHM=400-500 km s ^ 1 in the periphery, including a faint extension towards north-east.

sources.summary: The paper identifies 4C +23.56 as a central high-redshift radio galaxy in a strong proto-cluster candidate field containing numerous red, color-selected galaxies and overdensities above five.

quotes.quote: Some of the fields contain a bunch of very red galaxies ( J-K 2.3 ), in particular 4C  00.62 and 4C +23.56 show sequences of red galaxies which are likely to be progenitors of the colour-magnitude relation of early-type galaxies commonly seen in lower redshift clusters at z 1 . Therefore these two fields are most plausible high- z cluster candidates.

It is clear that the two fields, 4C  00.62 and 4C +23.56, contain quite high number densities of galaxies that satisfy the two-colours cut (Eq. \ 2) and they are among the most plausible proto-cluster candidates. In particular, the 4C +23.56 field has numerous red galaxies with J-K>2.3 . In fact, also reported a high density of red galaxies with I-K>4 in the central 1.25 arcmin ^ 2 field around 4C +23.56.

In particular, two radio galaxies fields, namely 4C  00.62 and 4C +23.56, show the highest overdensities of the colour-selected galaxies of more than factor of five. Therefore, we are likely to be witnessing the assembly and formation of proto-clusters with many evolved galaxies at z 2.5 associated to the radio galaxies.

sources.summary: The paper presents 4C23.56 as a giant high-redshift radio galaxy whose X-ray/radio structure, gas-rich nucleus, and hard radio spectrum indicate recurrent jet activity.

quotes.quote: The particular object under study in this paper, 4C23.56, is a suitable target to further explore these two phenomena to try and discern what are their fundamental characteristics. 4C23.56 is a giant radio galaxy extending over 60 arcminutes, corresponding to 492 kpc projected on the plane of the Sky, previously studied in X-rays by Johnson2007 .

Although we have no HI observations of 4C23.56 to bring to bear against the two strands of evidence that it has exhibited more than one episode of jet activity ( [ i ] high-energy emission external to the hotspots requiring a pre-existing magnetic field and [ ii ] the harder synchrotron spectrum compared with that of the ICCMB emission) there are two indications of a plentiful supply of gas in the vicinity of this nucleus: (i) the considerable column density of 0.79 0.5 10^ 24 , (ii) the presence of two opposite cones, having 90^ opening angles, revealed in narrow-band Lyman- imaging by Knopp1997 and presumably illuminated by ionising radiation from the nucleus and (iii) a rotating gas cloud with dynamical mass inferred from Keck spectroscopy by VillarMartin2003 to be 2.9 10^ 12 M _ .

In this study, of the giant ( 0.5 Mpc) and high-redshift ( z 2.5 ) radio galaxy of 4C23.56, two strands of evidence have emerged that point to there being a previous episode of jet activity in this object other than the episode delineated by the current radio synchrotron emission. These strands of evidence are: (i) outside of the hotspots, further away from the nucleus, there is extended radio emission that may be plausibly explained by fast particles, freshly accelerated at the hotspots, leaking into pre-existing, weakly magnetised plasma that may be the relic lobes of a previous epoch of jet activity and (ii) the radio spectrum is considerably harder than that of the inverse-Compton emission.

sources.summary: The paper identifies the 4C+23.56 field as one of three radio-galaxy environments with a factor-of-two SMG overdensity above 4 mJy, significant at about 3 sigma.

quotes.quote: We find overdensities of a factor of 2 in the source counts of 3 individual fields (4C+23.56, PKS1138-262 and MRC0355-037) over areas of 200 sq deg.

In the fields towards 4C+23.56, PKS1138-262 and MRC0355-037, however, the numbers of sources with S _ > 4 mJy exceeds the number of expected sources in a blank field by a factor of 2 . The significance of these overdensities is > 3 , which means that the probability of finding overdensities like these ones by chance is < 0.3 .
Only in the surroundings of 4C+23.56, PKS1138-262 and MRC0355-037 did we detect individual overdensity signals of 2 with a significance of 3 .

sources.summary: The paper reports extended X-ray lobes around the high-redshift radio galaxy 4C 23.56 and argues that inverse-Compton emission stores substantial energy affecting its host and proto-cluster environment.

quotes.quote: We present an XMM-Newton observation of the radio galaxy 4C 23.56 at z=2.48 which reveals extended X-ray emission coincident with the radio lobes spanning 0.5 Mpc. These are the largest X-ray-bright lobes known at z>2 . Under the assumption that these features result from inverse-Compton scattering of cosmic microwave background photons by relativistic electrons in the radio source lobes, the measured luminosity of L_ 0.5-8 keV =7.5 10^ 44 erg s ^ 1 implies a minimum energy stored within the lobes of 10^ 59 erg. We discuss the potential of the large-scale radio/X-ray lobes to influence evolution of the host galaxy and proto-cluster environment at high redshift.

gmw_00001890.name: NVSS J2135 3337
gmw_00001890.overview: NVSS J2135 3337 is a moderate-luminosity radio galaxy located at a redshift of z = 2.52 [arXiv:1610.02057]. Its ionized gas emission is extended over approximately 16.3 by 10.4 kiloparsecs, exhibiting a total velocity offset of 600 km/s and broad nuclear line widths [arXiv:1610.02057].

sources.summary: NVSS J2135 3337 has moderate radio luminosity, adaptive-optics line-emission mapping, extended ionized gas, a central peak, large velocity offset, and broad nuclear line widths.

quotes.quote: NVSS J2135 3337 NVSS J2135 3337 at z = 2.52 was taken from the catalog of broderick07a and bryant09 and is one of our galaxies with fairly moderate radio luminosity, P_ 500 =27.9 W Hz ^ 1 miley08 . It has a bright, R = 14.5 mag star at a distance of only 16.9 \ which allows observations with adaptive optics. At a 250 mas 125 mas pixel scale this enabled us to reach a spatial resolution of FWHM =0.4 0.4 . Ionized line emission in NVSS2135 3337 is exended over 2.5 1.4 , corresponding to 16.3 kpc 10.4 kpc after deconvolution with the size of the point spread function.

Fig. fig:maps4 shows the gas morphology and kinematics of NVSS J2135 3337, which was derived from H in this case. The total velocity offset is 600 km s ^ 1 along an axis that is going from the south-east to the north-west of the nucleus. The line widths peak near the nucleus with FWHM=1200 km s ^ 1 , and with much lower widths of FWHM =200-300 km s ^ 1 in the far periphery and in particular the faint northern extension. The radio source is compact and falls inbetween the regions with the most strongly blueshifted and redshifted gas, near the peak in FWHM line width.

gmw_00001891.name: MG 2144 + 1928
gmw_00001891.overview: MG 2144 + 1928 is a high-redshift radio galaxy [arXiv:1610.02057, arXiv:2007.10061] at z = 3.6 [arXiv:2007.10061] featuring bright radio hotspots [arXiv:2105.03467] and detached northern and southern gas plumes [arXiv:1610.02057]. The galaxy is surrounded by a quiescent, multi-phase circumgalactic medium [arXiv:2007.10061] with extended ionized and molecular gas [arXiv:2007.10061].

sources.summary: MG 2144 + 1928 has unusual gas morphology, very extended [OIII] emission, detached northern and southern gas plumes, uniform broad line widths, and radio hotspots at plume ends.

quotes.quote: MG J2144 + 1928 The gas morphology in MG 2144 + 1928 at z = 3.59 is very unusual compared to the rest of the sample. [ OIII ] 5007 line emission extends over 9.1 \ along the major axis, with a very small minor axis of only 2.2 . This corresponds to a size of 66 kpc 16 kpc. Fig. fig:maps4 shows a relatively compact, but elongated central emission-line region, centered roughly on the continuum peak. Towards north and south, but offset from the central nebulosity, we find two gas plumes that are both not directly connected to the central nebulosity, but have very similar velocities.
The line widths are also fairly uniform throughout the source, with FWHM 800 km s ^ 1 . Two unresolved radio hotspots are found right at the far end of each emission-line gas plume (Fig. fig:maps4 ).

sources.summary: The paper studies 4C 19.71 as a z=3.6 radio galaxy with extended ionized and molecular circumgalactic emission, interpreting northern halo gas as quiescent multiphase material.

quotes.quote: ALMA and MUSE observations reveal a quiescent multi-phase circumgalactic medium around the z 3.6 radio galaxy 4C 19.71 Based on observations obtained at the European Organization for Astronomical Research in the Southern Hemisphere under program 097.B-0323(B). We present MUSE@VLT imaging spectroscopy of rest-frame ultraviolet emission lines and ALMA observations of the \ emission line, probing both the ionized and diffuse molecular medium around the radio galaxy \ at z 3.6. This radio galaxy has extended \ emission over a region 100 kpc in size preferentially oriented along the axis of the radio jet. Faint \ emission extends beyond the radio hot spots.

We also find extended \ and \ emission over a region of 150 kpc in size, where the most distant emission lies 40 kpc beyond the north radio lobe and has narrow full width half maximum ( ) line widths of 180 \ and a small relative velocity offset v 130 \ from the systemic redshift of the radio galaxy. The \ is detected in the same region with 100 \ and v 5 , while \ is not detected in the regions south of the radio galaxy. We interpret the coincidence in the northern line emission as evidence of relatively quiescent multi-phase gas residing within the halo at a projected distance of 75 kpc from the host galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper describes 4C + 19.71 as a radio galaxy with bright hot spots, little lobe radio emission, extended X-rays along the hot-spot axis, and the least secure measurements in the sample.

quotes.quote: 4C + 19.71 This \ has bright hot spots and no significant lobe emission. The X-ray emission appears to be extended beyond the hot spots, and so we define the lobes based on the apparent X-ray enhancement. We note that wu17 found no statistically significant evidence for diffuse X-ray emission around the core using circular annuli, but along the axis joining the hot spots there is a significant detection. However, this is only true when summing the north and south lobe regions, and it is not clear whether the cluster of X-ray counts to the south is part of the radio galaxy. Of the radio galaxies our sample, the measurements are least secure for 4C + 19.71.

sources.summary: For 4C+19.71, the paper uses [C I] to constrain redshift where He II is skyline-affected and finds line widths comparable to dusty star-forming galaxies.

quotes.quote: In two sources, the He II line cannot be fit due to its low S/N. Here, the [ C i ] line is used to constrain the systemic redshift for the 4C+03.24 and 4C+19.71.

In 4C+19.71, He II is strongly affected by skyline residuals e.g Falkendal2021 , even though some of the line emission is discernible, we provide as a Gaussian profile that is fixed to the [ C i ] redshift (top panel of Fig. fig:galaxy-spectra-images ) and label this He II fix in the figure.

Two galaxies from our sample, TN J0121+1320 and 4C+19.71 have similar line widths as the DSFGs and SMGs. The line widths in these two HzRGs may be tracing the gas kinematics of molecular clouds spread throughout the galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper reanalyzes 4C 19.71 and finds its radial X-ray profile consistent with background beyond 2 arcsec; possible lobe-aligned enhancements are not statistically significant.

quotes.quote: For 4C 19.71, the detected \ source's radial profile decreases so rapidly that it is consistent with background beyond 2 , similarly to that of the simulated PSF's (top panel of Fig. other_ctsden_fig ). The \ image might suggest the presence of possible, off nuclear \ sources located 4 and 5 away from the central point source in the N and S direction, respectively (see the top right panel of Fig. other_fig ). The detection of co-spatial 8.3 GHz radio lobes at these positions smail+2012 admittedly increases the probability that such extended \ emission is indeed present. However, from a statistical perspective, none of the visual enhancements represents a significant detection above the background.

gmw_00001892.name: MG 2308 + 0336
gmw_00001892.overview: MG 2308 + 0336 is a radio galaxy at a redshift of z = 2.46 [arXiv:1610.02057]. Its emission-line region fills the space between two radio lobes and exhibits a monotonic velocity gradient [arXiv:1610.02057].

sources.summary: MG 2308 + 0336 has line emission between two radio lobes, a regular surface-brightness peak used as the nucleus, multiple detected lines, and a monotonic velocity gradient.

quotes.quote: MG 2308 + 0336 MG 2308 + 0336 at z = 2.46 has a resolved, but relatively small radio source with a size of 3.6 \ (29 kpc at z = 2.46). Line emission shown in Fig. fig:maps5 fills the area between the two radio lobes (right panel of Fig. fig:maps5 ). We did not detect the optical continuum in this source, but the emission-line surface brightness profile is very regular with a peak in the middle between the two radio lobes. We assume that this is approximately the position of the nucleus in MG 2308 + 0336.

The integrated spectrum of MG J2308 + 0336 shows H , [ OIII ] 4959,5007, [ OI ] , H , [ NII ] 6583, and the [ SII ] 6716,6731 doublet (Fig. fig:intspec ). All lines were adequately fit with a single Gaussian component of a common width FWHM = 750 km s ^ 1 . The kinematic maps show a monotonic velocity gradient of 800 km s ^ 1 increasing from the south-south-east to the north-north-west. The FWHMs show that the lowest line widths are near the center, with FWHM 500 600 km s ^ 1 , and a broadening at either end of the emission line region, near the radio lobes.

gmw_00001893.name: J0121+1149
gmw_00001893.overview: J0121+1149 is an active galactic nucleus that hosts a radio jet [arXiv:1101.5942]. Its Faraday rotation map reveals three distinct regions consisting of a core, an inner jet, and an outer jet [arXiv:1101.5942].

sources.summary: The paper maps J0121+1149 as an AGN jet source whose Faraday rotation separates into core, inner-jet, and outer-jet regions with changing polarization structure.

quotes.quote: The Faraday rotation map of this source, shown in Figure  j0121 , displays 3 distinct regions with different characteristic RM values: the core, inner jet and outer jet. After correcting for this Faraday rotation, the magnetic field structure is complex, but appears to become longitudinal in the outer jet. Figure  j0121 also shows a comparison of the polarisation images obtained at epoch C and in January 2004. The three distinct regions of the Faraday rotation are present at both epochs. The polarisation structure in the core and inner jet changed between the two epochs, while that in the outer jet remained constant.

gmw_00001894.name: 3C 345
gmw_00001894.overview: 3C 345 is a highly polarized, flat-spectrum radio quasar located at a redshift of approximately 0.593 [arXiv:1201.4178, arXiv:1902.05470]. The active nucleus features a complex, bent relativistic jet with superluminal components [arXiv:1005.2177, arXiv:1111.2045] and exhibits strong, multiwavelength variability across radio, optical, and X-ray bands [arXiv:1005.2177, arXiv:1402.6385].

sources.summary: The paper describes 3C 345 as an FSRQ with long-term millimeter flux changes, a 2009 outburst, double-peaked flux distributions, simultaneous bands, and stable-to-flattening spectra.

quotes.quote: 3C 345 The 3-mm (1.3-mm) lightcurves of this FSRQ start at flux densities of 4 Jy ( 2 Jy) in 1997 and reach their highest values of 7 Jy ( 4 Jy) in mid-1998. From then the fluxes decrease overall down to 3 Jy ( 2 Jy) in the time range 2004--2008. Around 2009 an outburst occurs, reaching flux levels up to 6.5 Jy ( 4.5 Jy) and slowly decreasing since then. The 2009 outburst raises in 2008--2009 with an exponential timescale of 1 yr.

The 3-mm (1.3-mm) flux distributions follow double-peaked profiles we approximated as superpositions of two Gaussians peaking at 3 Jy and 5.5 Jy ( 2 Jy and 3.5 Jy). Within errors, the 1.3-mm and 3-mm lightcurves are simultaneous. Within errors, the spectral index remains on very similar levels in the range 0.6-0.8 for most of the monitoring time. After 2007 -- the time frame of the most recent outburst -- the spectrum flattens somewhat towards 0.4 .

sources.summary: The paper monitors the flat-spectrum radio quasar 3C 345, measuring optical variability, outbursts, intra-night variability, mixed color behavior, redshift, position, and historical variability.
sources.paper_coordinate_text: Its redshift is 0.5928 marz96 and its position is 16:42:58.8, 39:48:37 (J2000.0).

quotes.quote: Base on a 3.5-year three-colour monitoring programme, we studied the optical variability and colour behaviour of one FSRQ, 3C 345. There is at least one outburst in this period. The overall variation amplitude is 2.640 mags in the i band. Intra-night variability was observed on two nights. The bluer-when-brighter and redder-when-brighter chromatisms were simultaneously observed in this object when using different pairs of passbands to compute the colours.

3C 345 is the first established variable quasar burbidge65 and is classified as FSRQs later. Its redshift is 0.5928 marz96 and its position is 16:42:58.8, 39:48:37 (J2000.0). The high declination enables short monitoring gap ( 80 days) each year.

sources.summary: The paper connects 3C 345's Fermi gamma-ray and optical variability to VLBA parsec-scale radio jet structure, favoring synchrotron self-Compton emission from the quasar jet.

quotes.quote: 3C 345 was recently identified as a ray emitter, based on the first 20 months of Fermi LAT data and optical monitoring. In this paper, a connection between the ray and optical variability of 3C 345 and properties of its parsec-scale radio emission is investigated. The Fermi LAT data of 3C 345, covering an energy range of 0.1-300 GeV, were combined with 32 Very Long Baseline Array observations of the object made at 43.2 GHz in the period of January 2008 - March 2010. The VLBA data reveal morphology and kinematics of the flow on scales of up to 5 milliarcseconds (deprojected linear distances of 380 parsecs).

Variations of the ray, optical and parsec-scale radio emission show a similar long-term trend persistent during the entire VLBA monitoring period. The ray and optical variations on shorter time scales are related to structural changes in the jet on scales of 0.3 mas ( 23 parsecs, deprojected), with the ray and optical flares possibly related to the evolution of four distinct superluminal components identified in the flow. The observations indicate that both the quiescent and flaring components of the ray emission are produced in a region of the jet of 23 pc in extent. This region may mark the Compton-loss dominated zone of the flow and its large extent may favor the synchrotron self-Compton mechanism for ray production in the relativistic jet of the quasar 3C 345.

sources.summary: The paper adds quasar 3C 345 as a 2015 target, resolving its bent western jet, knotty large component, steep spectral-index structure, and a new 43 GHz component.

quotes.quote: 3C 345 3C 345 is a quasar with a redshift of 0.593 Marziani1996 . We included this source in our target list in 2015 as a replacement for 3C 84. Although 3C 345 has relatively high redshift compared to our other targets, it clearly shows its detailed jet structure in our KaVA maps. 3C 345 has been a ``popular'' object of study since the 1960s e.g., Goldsmith1965 ; here, we focus on features like superluminal motion of the jet and evolution of the spectral index.

3C 345 shows a complicated jet structure that extends out to 9 mas toward the west. The jet appears to bend at a distance of 1.5 mas from the core. The separate large jet component 5 mas west of the core shows a knotty structure. We were able to resolve four model components (including the core) at 22 GHz and found one more component at 43 GHz which seems to be a recently launched component that has not been resolved by the 22 GHz observations.

sources.summary: The paper reports that 3C 345 has a northwest-bending jet, rising edge polarization, a statistically significant transverse RM gradient with a sign change, and a mainly longitudinal magnetic field.

quotes.quote: 3C 345 : The jet extends towards the north-west at a PA of 55 ^ . At the smaller scales seen on the MOJAVE maps, the jet of 3C 345 initially extends towards the west and then it bends towards the north-west in roughly the same PA seen in our observations. The EVPAs corrected for the integrated RM are predominantly transverse to the local jet direction (Fig. Fig2_3C345_BLLac_3C454.3 a) following the overall behaviour seen in the 2 cm observations. There is an interesting polarization structure about 35 mas from the core composed of orthogonal polarization near the jet edge and longitudinal polarization near its central axis that could be a `spine+sheath' composition. The fractional polarization of 3C 345 is roughly 2-5 per cent in the core (Fig. Fig2_3C345_BLLac_3C454.3 b), and reaches values of 15-30 per cent in the jet (Fig. Fig2_3C345_BLLac_3C454.3 ).

Our RM map (Fig. 3C345_RM ) shows a monotonic, transverse RM gradient in the jet at a distance of approximately 10 mas from the observed core, visible in both the intrinsic-beam (statistical significance 2.5 ) and circular-beam (statistical significance 2.2 ) RM maps. The RM values display a sign change across the jet. Although the statistical significance of this gradient is smaller than 3 , the gradient spans more than two beamwidths, making it statistically significant, as is discussed in Section disc1 .

sources.summary: The paper studies 3C 345 as a flat-spectrum radio quasar with multiwavelength monitoring, a type 1 AGN spectrum, intense flaring activity, superluminal motion, and a complex relativistic jet.

quotes.quote: We report simultaneous observations at different energy bands in radio, optical, UV, X-rays and rays of the flat-spectrum radio-quasar 3C 345. We built the light curve of the source at different frequencies from 2008, the beginning of the Fermi all-sky survey, to 2016, using new data and public archives. In particular we obtained several optical spectra, to study the behavior of emission lines and the continuum in different activity states and to derive the black hole mass.

The radio quasar 3C 345 is a well studied object showing superluminal motion on very large baseline interferometry (VLBI) scales ( see the MOJAVE project, see also Lister09 Roberts12, Roberts13 ). Its optical spectrum, as expected from a FSRQ, is that of a type 1 AGN although X-ray observations suggest the presence of a Compton thick absorber in its nucleus similar to those found in type 2 AGN Eguchi17 . The first optical magnitude measurements were already carried out in the late nineteenth century, and in the following years the source showed an intense flaring activity Zhang98 . Some authors suggested that such flaring episodes are induced by perturbations on the accretion flow and disk generated by the presence of a binary system of supermassive black holes Lobanov05, Klare05 . A possible consequence of these is the complex structure of the relativistic jet, which exhibits an helical parsec-scale shape Schinzel10 , and a counter-jet symmetric to the approaching jet Matveyenko13 .

sources.summary: The paper identifies 3C 345 as the main Fermi gamma-ray counterpart, linking its radio, optical, X-ray, and gamma-ray activity to a bright blazar jet.

quotes.quote: For more than 15 years, since the days of the Energetic Gamma-Ray Experiment Telescope (EGRET) on board the Compton Gamma-Ray Observatory (CGRO; 1991-2000), it has remained an open question why the prominent blazar 3C 345 was not reliably detected at ray energies 20 MeV. Recently a bright ray source (0FGL J1641.4+3939/1FGL J1642.5+3947), potentially associated with 3C 345, was detected by the Large Area Telescope (LAT) on Fermi . Multiwavelength observations from radio bands to X-rays (mainly GASP-WEBT and Swift ) of possible counterparts (3C 345, NRAO 512, B3 1640 + 396) were combined with 20 months of Fermi LAT monitoring data (August 2008 - April 2010) to associate and identify the dominating ray emitting counterpart of 1FGL J1642.5+3947. The source 3C 345 is identified as the main contributor for this ray emitting region.

The source 3C 345 is by far the brightest of the three quasars in the ray region studied during the presented time period. In all bands it was consistently brighter than NRAO 512 and B3 1640 + 396. The improved Fermi position combined with multi-wavelength activity and a moderate significance of correlated optical/ ray variability, using a plausible range of in the ray and optical PSDs, strongly suggest an identification of 3C 345 as ray source and the main contributor to the observed ray flux in 1FGL J1642.5+3947.

sources.summary: The paper contrasts 3C 345 with other blazars, finding no orphan flare and no detected sheath signature, instead setting an upper limit on sheath luminosity.

quotes.quote: We display a stacked radio map of the blazar 3C 345 in Figure fig20 . In contrast to the blazars discussed above, there is no polarimetric signature of a jet sheath present in our stacked map. Instead, the polarization is concentrated along the center of the jet, with the EVPAs aligned predominantly parallel to the jet axis. This alignment, in contrast to jet shear, is that expected from diffusive shock acceleration within the jet that compresses the magnetic field and aligns the field lines perpendicular to the jet flow, resulting in EVPAs oriented parallel to the spine of the jet ( wardle94 ). The jet undergoes two bends downstream of the radio core, and the EVPAs maintain their orientation with respect to the jet axis around each bend. We searched through archival ray and optical light curves to look for instances of orphan ray flares within 3C 345. We were unable to find any (see Figure fig21 ).

This suggests that an enhancement in the jet sheath is not present within 3C 345. The lack of a polarimetric signature of a jet sheath within 3C 345 might indicate that, while jet sheaths are almost certainly present within all blazars, the sheath itself only becomes ``enhanced'' at certain times within the span of a blazar's lifetime (perhaps as a portion of the sheath encounters a standing shock within the jet). This would explain why the majority of blazar ray flares are not orphan in nature. It is also possible that the jet sheath is present, but we are unable to detect it due to the extreme gradient in intrinsic brightness between the spine and the sheath. In order to compute a rough estimate of the upper limit of the bolometric luminosity of a jet sheath within 3C 345, we trace (in gray) a region on the periphery of the jet within which a sheath might exist (see Figure fig20 ).

sources.summary: The paper treats 3C 345 as a blazar whose MgII broad line changed from mildly asymmetric to strongly redward asymmetric, with HST data used to compare CIV and MgII.

quotes.quote: The Broad Emission Lines of 3C 345 The blazar 3C 345 appears in both the pea81 complete sample and the 3CR complete sample. It was included in this study because it has changed from having a very mildly asymmetric MgII BEL for two decades to a very asymmetric MgII BEL in the summer of 2016 ber18 . The emergence of the RA profile is illustrated in Figure 5. Note that the law96 fit does not appear in Table 2 since the quality (spectral resolution and signal to noise) is rather poor (compared to the other spectra) and not conducive to accurate fitting. We have also obtained numerous low signal to noise spectra that cannot be fit convincingly and were not included in Table 2.

The HST spectra were of lower quality and required smoothing, but these are unique opportunities to simultaneously observe CIV and MgII. One of the topics of interest is the comparison of the redward asymmetry of high ionization lines with the low ionization lines.

sources.summary: The paper uses RadioAstron polarimetry to resolve 3C 345's innermost jet, identifying a self-absorbed core, downstream bright feature, polarized components, and magnetic-field orientation consistent with shocks.

quotes.quote: We study the innermost jet morphology and magnetic field strength in the AGN 3C 345 with an unprecedented resolution using images obtained within the framework of the key science programme on AGN polarisation of the Space VLBI mission RadioAstron . We observed the flat spectrum radio quasar 3C 345 at 1.6 GHz on 2016 March 30 with RadioAstron and 18 ground-based radio telescopes in full polarisation mode. Our images, in both total intensity and linear polarisation, reveal a complex jet structure at 300 as angular resolution, corresponding to a projected linear scale of about 2 pc or a few thousand gravitational radii. We identify the synchrotron self-absorbed core at the jet base and find the brightest feature in the jet 1.5 mas downstream of the core. Several polarised components appear in the Space VLBI images that cannot be seen from ground array-only images.

Except for the core, the electric vector position angles follow the local jet direction, suggesting a magnetic field perpendicular to the jet. This indicates the presence of plane perpendicular shocks in these regions.

sources.summary: The paper divides 3C 345 into two radio activity phases, measuring strong 14.5 GHz variability, spectral-index changes, interband lags, and random-walk-like red-noise periodograms.

quotes.quote: We present the results of a time series analysis of the long-term radio lightcurves of four blazars: 3C 279, 3C 345, 3C 446, and BL Lacertae. We exploit the data base of the University of Michigan Radio Astronomy Observatory (UMRAO) monitoring program which provides densely sampled lightcurves spanning 32 years in time in three frequency bands located at 4.8, 8, and 14.5 GHz.

3C 345 We divided the lightcurves into two activity phases. Phase A, ranging from 1980 to 1994, is characterized by strong variability, with fluxes (at 14.5 GHz) moving between 5 and 17 Jy. This variability is also expressed in the spectral index which fluctuates between 0.4 and 0.2. The DCF analysis finds a time lag of =1.65^ +1.27 _ 1.01 years ( 3 confidence intervals) between the 4.8 GHz and 14.5 GHz lightcurves, with smaller, marginally significant, lags between the other frequency pairs.

sources.summary: The paper tests visibility-based brightness-temperature estimates on 3C 345, showing that correcting for core elongation improves the longest-baseline temperature limit.

quotes.quote: The methodology of this approach is described in Sect. sc:method and is tested with the visibility data from two VLBA Very Long Baseline Array of National Radio Astronomy Observatory, Socorro NM, USA; http://www.nrao.edu observations of the prominent compact radio sources 3C 345 and NGC 1052.

The effect of correcting for the core elongation is illustrated in Fig. fg:tbcorr , where the correcting factor _ is applied to brightness temperature estimates made from the visibility data on 3C 345 presented in Figs. fg:visexample fg:tbexample . The corrections are derived for an axial ratio = 0.5 (weighted average of the model-fitting results in kovalev+2005 kovalev+2005 ) and a jet position angle _ jet = -104^ inferred from the source structure shown in Fig. fg:visexample . The increased scatter at shorter baselines shows the effect of contributions from larger structures that are incorrectly described by the adopted values of _ jet and, in particular, . However, at the longest baselines, correcting for the core elongation clearly improves the T_ b,lim estimate and brings it well within the errors of the modelfit-based estimate.
Applying \ the visibility-based brightness temperature estimates to VLBI data on 3C 345 and NGC 1052 has demonstrated that the method is reliable in two particular cases.

sources.summary: The paper interprets parsec-scale acceleration in the quasar 3C 345 jet as magnetic driving, matching the observed C7 component acceleration from 5 to 10.

quotes.quote: Extended acceleration has also been indicated in superluminal blazar jets. For example, VLBI images of the quasar 3C 345 have shown that the jet component speeds increase with separation from the core e.g., Z95,LZ99 . In particular, in the case of the C7 component, U97 combined a VLBI proper-motion measurement with an inference of the Doppler factor from a synchrotron self-Compton calculation to deduce an acceleration from 5 to 10 over a (deprojected) distance range (measured from the core) of 3-20 \ pc .

We propose that the parsec-scale acceleration inferred by U97 in component C7 of the 3C 345 jet is most plausibly interpreted in terms of magnetic driving, and we present in Figure fig2 an self-similar MHD solution describing a proton-electron jet that supports this claim. The parameters/boundary conditions that determine this solution are, in the notation of VK: =5/3 , F=0.99 , z_c=0 , x_ A ^2=0.987 , _ M =8.66 , _ A =1.0007 , =75 , _ A =25 , and B_0 _0^ 2-F =2.1 10^ 17 \ cgs . In particular, it is seen from Figure fig2 d that the Lorentz factor increases from 5 at / _ A 5.55 to 10 at / _ A 13.31 .

sources.summary: The paper models 3C 345 with a binary black hole whose orbital motion and accretion-disk precession reproduce its optical variability and parsec-scale jet behavior.

quotes.quote: This scenario, applied to the quasar 3C 345, explains the observed variations of radio and optical emission from the quasar, and reproduces the structural variations observed in the parsec-scale jet of this object. The binary system in 3C 345 is described by two equal-mass black holes with masses of 7.1 10^8 M _ separated by 0.33 pc and orbiting with a period 480 yr. The orbital motion induces a precession of the accretion disk around the primary black hole, with a period of 2570 yr. The jet plasma is described by a magnetized, relativistic electron-positron beam propagating inside a wider and slower electron-proton jet.

First, the precession characteristics of the accretion disk are determined by fitting the kinematic and flux density evolution of C7. This yields a precession period T_ p = 2570 yr, which is in a good agreement with the periodicities inferred from the changes of kinematic properties of individual jet components and variations of the ejecting position angle in 3C 345 .

sources.summary: The paper presents 1641+399 as a quasar with a close companion, cluster membership, previous BBH modeling, a bent jet, multiwavelength quasi-periodicity, and infrared variability.

quotes.quote: * 1641+399 (3C 345) (quasar, z=0.595 ): This source appears to have a close companion (at a projected separation of 47 kpc) and is also member of a cluster, implying the possibility of multiple mergers. This source has already been modeled as BBH ( Lobanov2005 ). The bent jet agrees well with the presence of a BBH system. Almost periodical in radio, optical, and X-rays. Variable also in the infrared.

sources.summary: The paper describes 3C 345 as a z=0.593 powerful highly polarized quasar with a short 25 kpc radio jet, bright hotspot, asymmetric halo, and possible V-shaped counter-hotspot structure.

quotes.quote: 3C 345 or B3 1641+399 is a powerful HPQ located at redshift z = 0.593 (1 at the distance of the source corresponds to 6.6 kpc). The primary radio structure consists of a short jet at position angle 327 that contains a bright hotspot, 2 7 away from the unresolved core (Figure figxrayimage ). The radio jet extends to a projected distance of 25 kpc. The 1.5 GHz image shows an asymmetric halo centered on the core that has a radius of approximately 10 . Although there is no clear indication of a radio counterjet, there is a suggestion of a weak hot spot along the counterjet direction so that the hotspot-core-counter-hotspot forms a V-shape see Murphy93 .

sources.summary: The paper presents 3C 345 as an FSRQ blazar whose monitored SED shows declining jet power, broad-line variability, and changing thermal-component prominence.

quotes.quote: An example of such application is illustrated in Fig. 2, for the case of the FSRQ blazar 3C 345 Berton17 . During the period monitored by the Fermi/LAT ray telescope between August 2008 and October 2016, this source showed a significant decrease of high-energy activity, which, in spite of the occurrence of strong outbursts, apparently involved its overall emission. Like many other blazars, its SED is energetically dominated by the non-thermal processes arising in the jet and the observed decrease in luminosity might be interpreted as a loss of jet power.

The ejection of new relativistic plasma blobs, reported by VLBI radio observations in correspondence with some flares represents episodic breaks in this general trend, which, in some cases, appears to affect other spectral components, like the prominent broad Mg  II 2798 emission line. A subsequent analysis of its spectrum and SED shows that, while moving from the maximum activity to the minimum observed during the monitoring, the source featured a decrease of jet emission power, but a relevant enhancement in the relative importance of the thermal component, for which we also observe a slight increase of temperature.

sources.summary: The paper reports time-variable rotation measures in 3C 345, including a larger 43/86 GHz RM one month later and a flare whose timing may explain the change in frequency dependence.

quotes.quote: 3C 345 This source shows almost the same RMs at 22/43 and 43/86 GHz, |RM| 10^4 \ rad/m^2 in the 2016 December data, with a being consistent with zero within errors. However, the RM at 43/86 GHz is much larger than that at 22/43 GHz about one month later, resulting in a=1.86 0.3 . These results indicate that there is substantial time variability in this source. Similarly to the case of CTA 102, this source shows a flare during our KVN observations and the flux density in early 2017 is almost three times higher than that in mid 2016 at 1 mm.
This might be related to a substantial change in a within 1 month for this source, though the sign of RM does not change during the period.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes 3C 345 as a highly polarized, flat-spectrum radio quasar with long 22 and 37 GHz monitoring, nonlinear variability, and multifractal optical/radio behaviour.

quotes.quote: The HPQ quasar 3C 345 2011A&A...532A.146L at a distance of z = 0.595 1996MNRAS.279..429N, 1984ApJ...279..465M, 1965ApJ...142.1674B is one of the most studied radio-loud flat-spectrum quasars whose radio spectrum is dominated by a flat-spectrum core 1989AJ.....97.1550K . 3C 345 has been monitored by Mets a hovi Radio Observatory at 22 and 37 GHz for 24 and 38 years, respectively. The radio flux observations of 3C 345 have shown that variations are common in the radio frequency range 1985ApJS...59..513A . 1991ApJ...380..351V indicated that the light-curve of 3C 345 is nonlinear and stochastic.

As an example, using multifractality analysis, it has been shown that the X-ray light curve of the BL Lac object PKS 2155-304 is monofractal, and the optical light curve of the quasar 3C 345 has a multifractal nature - nonlinear behavior 1992ApJ...391..518V .

sources.summary: The paper discusses 3C 345 as an AGN with helical VLBI jet structure, using S95 fits to C4/C5 trajectories and a distant component with a changed position angle as comparison.

quotes.quote: VLBI observations have revealed a helical structures in some AGNs, e.g., 3C 345 (S95), 3C 120 (Hardee et al. ), and Mkn 501 (Villata Raiteri ).
S95 applied the third model to fit the trajectories of C4 and C5 in 3C 345 simply because this model reflects the basic character of the trajectories and motion observed in 3C 345.

S95 fitted this helical model to the trajectories of C4 and C5 in 3C 345. In 3C 345 there is a component, 18 mas from the core, whose P.A. is different from the P.A.s of its inner components by 20 (Unwin Wehrle ), which is contrary to the general prediction of this helical model that the jet component will move close to the jet axis little by little; i.e., there is a position-angle regression. Because of this, S95 only restricted their analysis to the inner 3 mas from the core.

sources.summary: The paper identifies 1641+395 as 3C345, a well-studied quasar with strong polarization, larger infrared than optical variation, and two infrared outbursts.

quotes.quote: 1641+395, 3C345 , z=0.595 3C345 is one of the well studied quasars (Bregman et al. 1986a; Smith 1996, and references therein), it gives us a well-documented example of a clear connection between brightness and polarization as well as polarization angle (Smith 1996). It is polarized at P_ IR =0 20.27 (Mead et al. 1990) and P_ opt =5 35 (Bregman et al. 1986a). The optical variation of m=2.5 (Bregman et al, 1996a) is smaller than that in the infrared variation of J=3.16 mag. The infrared observations in the paper of Bregman et al (1986a) are also included in our discussion although the paper is not listed in table 1 for they have not indicated their telescopes used. The infrared light curves show clearly two outbursts separating by about 11.3 years which is consistent with the 11.4-year optical outburst period ( Webb et al. 1988, also see Fan et al. 1998a for summary).

sources.summary: The paper describes pc-scale jet knots moving westward, a northward curve close to the nucleus, and a kpc-scale X-ray/radio knot offset from the core.

quotes.quote: 1641 + 399 (3C 345) This source was in the sample and is included in our analysis for completeness. VLBA data show pc scale jet knots moving at about 12 along an average PA of -90 \ vlba,2009AJ....138.1874L . The jet curves north to a PA of about -45 \ about 4 mas from the nucleus 2009AJ....138.1874L . The kpc scale images (Fig.  fig:images ) show an X-ray and radio knot about 3 \ from the core at a PA of -35 .

sources.summary: The paper reports new B-band photometry of quasar 3C 345 from Asiago archival plates, confirming its complex long-term luminosity variations with continuous activity and roughly two-magnitude outbursts.

quotes.quote: Well followed in particular is 3C 345, one of the most luminous and violently variable ones, for which many recent studies have highlighted the complex behavior (Caproni et al., 2004, Zhang et al., 2000). Many plates of 3C 345 were taken at the Asiago Observatory in a larger project aimed to study the optical variability of a selected sample of quasars (see for instance Barbieri et al., 1988 and references therein).

3C 345 The data for 3C 345 are coherent with the behavior reported by Zhang et al. (1998, 2000).This new Asiago light curve (Fig. 5) confirms the very complicated behaviour of the luminosity variations characterized by a continuous activity at the level of 0.3 mag with superimposed sudden outbursts of approximately 2 mag.

sources.summary: The paper gives a 15 GHz core limit for 3C 345, compares a previous 15 GHz detection and 8 GHz nondetection, and notes intermittent historical detections.

quotes.quote: 1641 + 399 (3C 345): We find a limit of |m_c| < 0.19 in the core at 15 GHz in November of 2002. HW03 detected circular polarization in the core at 15 GHz, m_c = -0.38 012 , in January of 1998. The same location was optically thick at 8 GHz where they did not detect any circular polarization in simultaneous measurements. 3C 345 was detected intermittently (and with both signs) at 8 GHz and below in the historical integrated measurement compiled by WdP83.

sources.summary: The paper describes 1641+399/3C 345 as a heavily studied quasar with strong optical/radio variability and bent jet components, whose campaign radio light curves declined while the optical band rose strongly.

quotes.quote: 1641+399 (3C 345) The quasar 3C 345 has been studied extensively at all accessible wavelength. The observed strong variability in the optical and radio bands, the existence of possible periodicities, and the detection of bent trajectories of the radio jet components, made this source particularly interesting to test jet precession and binary black hole models (e.g. \ 1986ApJ...308...93B,Caproni2004,Lobanov2005,1999ApJ...521..509L,Schramm1993,Stevens1996,Webb1994 . Our radio light curves are slowly declining (Fig. fig:lc ), but in the optical band a strong increase can be seen from the beginning of the last observing season (Fig. fig:olc ). This becomes even more evident by looking at the evolution of the SEDs (Fig. fig:SEDs ), specially when considering that the last four SEDs are separated by only a few weeks.

sources.summary: The paper uses 3C 345 to test a flare time-lag method, finding agreement with VLBI core shifts and deriving jet-core magnetic fields near 0.1 G.

quotes.quote: Application of the method to the core shifts of the quasar 3C 345 shows a very good agreement between the core shifts directly measured from VLBI observations and derived from flares in the the total flux-density using the proposed method.
Our estimates for 3C 345 indicate core magnetic fields 0.1 G and magnetic field at 1 pc 0.4 G.

Applying the method to the 3C 345 VLBI jet and integrated light curves from 4.8 to 37 GHz, we found that the directly measured frequency-dependent core shifts and the core shifts calculated with our new method agree very well, supporting the validity of the method and our assumptions.

sources.summary: The paper uses 3C 345 as a blazar/quasar jet example, saying its superluminal knots follow curved/repeating trajectories and that knot C7 accelerates from Lorentz factor 3 to 10 over 3-20 pc.

quotes.quote: The superluminal knots in blazar jets such as 3C 345 e.g., U97 and 3C 279 e.g., W01 are inferred to move along distinct curved paths, although in the former case it has been argued W96 that at least two of the knots follow each other along the same trajectory. This behavior is consistent with the ``patchy shell'' model discussed above in connection with GRBs. There are also indications that the outflows continue to accelerate on large scales: for example, the Lorentz factor of knot C7 in the quasar 3C 345 was inferred to increase from 3 to 10 as it moved from a distance of 3 pc from the galactic nucleus to 20 pc U97 .

sources.summary: The quasar cannot be fit acceptably by a single power law; a two-power-law or broken-power-law model reproduces the hard-spectrum flattening near 1.7 keV.

quotes.quote: In only one case, 3C 345, is a single power law not an acceptable fit for a quasar. A second power law is necessary to account for the flattening above 1.7 keV in the core spectrum. This is in agreement with the results of gambill03 , who used a broken power law to fit the spectrum.

sources.summary: The paper cites 3C 345 as an observed quasar jet with inferred extended acceleration, motivating magnetic-driving interpretations and model fits to its jet data.

quotes.quote: There have also been explicit inferences of component acceleration based on radio proper motion and X-ray emission measurements for the jets in the quasars 3C 345 U97 and 3C 279 P03 . Extended acceleration in the 3C 345 jet has been independently indicated by the higher apparent speeds of jet components located further away from the nucleus LR05 and by the observed luminosity variations of the moving components LZ99 .

VK04 argued that these observations are most naturally interpreted in terms of magnetic driving and employed self-similar relativistic jet solutions to generate model fits to the 3C 345 data in support of this conclusion.

sources.summary: The superluminal quasar 3C 345 is known to vary in radio and X-rays; the representative PSPC observation is fit by a point-source model.

quotes.quote: 3C 345 This superluminal quasar is known to be variable in both the radio and X-ray (e.g. \ Unwin \ 1997). We use a single PSPC observation, taken between 1993 Mar 06 and 1993 Mar 11, as representative (since the available HRI observations are all short). The data are acceptably fit with a point-source model.

gmw_00001895.name: SPRC-1
gmw_00001895.overview: SPRC-1 is an active galactic nucleus host galaxy classified as a red early-type galaxy [arXiv:2208.12943]. It features a nearly orthogonal polar dust ring [arXiv:2208.12943], a two-sided radio jet [arXiv:2209.02717], and a companion galaxy located at a projected separation of 40 kpc [arXiv:2209.02717].

sources.summary: The paper describes J0009-0036 as a double-peaked radio-loud AGN with a companion galaxy, a two-sided radio jet, no VLBA detection, and tentative 21-cm absorption.

quotes.quote: Out of the ten double-peaked AGN, five (J0738+3156, J0842+0547, J1203+1319, J1243-0058 and J1517+3353) appear as single, undisturbed systems in the available optical images [ Dark Energy Camera Legacy Survey DECaLS; dey2019 , PanSTARRS-1 chambers2016 and SDSS ] . The remaining AGN are either part of galaxy pairs and interacting systems or show disturbed morphology. J0009-0036 has a companion galaxy at a projected separation of 40 kpc and a velocity separation of 460 . J1526+4140 is undergoing merger with another galaxy at 5 kpc and 150 . The optical continuum image of J0912+5320 shows two peaks and a tidal tail, while that of J1352+6541 shows signatures of a possible tidal disturbance. J1356+1026 is a post-merger system that has been observed as part of our \ 21-cm absorption survey in low- z galaxy mergers dutta2018 . Long-slit spectroscopy and NIR imaging of this system show double nuclei separated by 2.9 kpc greene2011,shen2011 . Moreover, there have been observations of extended \ emission in this system, suggesting large-scale outflows powered by feedback from the obscured, radio-quiet quasar greene2012 . Three of the AGN that appear as single systems (J0738+3156, J0842+0547 and J1203+1319) have been observed with the Keck Laser Guide Star Adaptive Optics system and the NIR camera NIRC2 mcgurk2015 . Based on these imaging observations, J1203+1319 has a double spatial structure on scales of <3 arcsec, indicating it is a candidate dual AGN. J0738+3156 and J0842+0547, on the other hand, show single spatial structure with no companions within 3 arcsec. Furthermore, long-slit optical spectra have been obtained for four AGN (J0738+3156, J0842+0547, J1243-0058 and J1517+3353) by comerford2018 . Based on these spectra, J0842+0547 and J1243-0058 are classified as outflows, whereas J1517+3353 is classified as an outflow composite, i.e. an outflow that includes many gas clouds at different velocities. On the other hand, J0738+3156 has a disturbed rotation-dominated profile.

Based on high-resolution ( 0.2 arcsec) multi-band (8-12 GHz) VLA observations muller2015 , J0009-0036 shows a two-sided radio jet suggesting that a radio jet-driven outflow could produce the double-peaked emission. In addition, six of the AGN have been imaged with VLBA at milliarcsecond (mas; 10 pc) resolution liu2018a . Three of them (J0009-0036, J0738+3156 and J1356+1026) remain undetected in the VLBA 8.4 GHz images. J0912+5320 shows three components in the VLBA image, an unresolved central core and northern and southern components extending over 25 mas, indicative of a jet structure. J1243-0058 shows a compact, unresolved component in the VLBA image, and there are no other radio sources stronger than 1 mJy within 1 arcsec. J1352+6541 shows a faint component at 2.4 mas from the primary component.

The observations of two of the sources, J0738+3156 and J1352+6541, were affected by strong radio frequency interference (RFI), preventing us from reliably studying the associated \ 21-cm absorption in these cases. The \ 21-cm spectra towards the remaining eight sources, along with the double-peaked \ emission lines in the SDSS spectra, are shown in Fig. fig:sdss_gmrt_spec . Among the eight sources with reliable \ 21-cm absorption spectra, we detect absorption from three sources (J1356+1026, J1517+3353, J1526+4140). In two cases, J0009-0036 and J0912+5320, we find tentative absorption features at the expected position of the \ 21-cm line. We reobserved the source J0009-0036 using the same observational set-up for an additional 7 hours in October 2019. We combined the spectra of J0009-0036 from the two different observing runs using inverse noise weighting. The peak optical depth of the absorption feature is detected at 3 significance in the combined spectrum. In the case of the absorption towards J0912+5320, the peak optical depth is detected at 5 significance. However, the absorption lines towards both J0009-0036 and J0912+5320 are narrow (velocity width encompassing 90 of the total optical depth, 90 \ 30-40 ), whereas most associated \ 21-cm absorption lines tend to broader on average ( 100 ). Further, the absorption profiles in the stokes LL and RR spectra do not resemble each other perfectly, although they are consistent within the uncertainties. Since there could be low-level sporadic RFI affecting the absorption features, we consider these as tentative detections for our analysis. The two tentative \ 21-cm absorption lines are shown in Fig. fig:ten_hi_spec . For the remaining three sources, J0842+0547, J1203+1319, J1243-0058, where we do not detect absorption, we place 3 upper limit on the integrated optical depth for a velocity width of 100 .

sources.summary: The paper describes SDSS J000911.58-003654.7 as a double-peaked AGN target with nearby spiral emission suggesting a merger-rich environment relevant to dual AGNs.

quotes.quote: SDSS J000911.58-003654.7 For observations of this galaxy, we purposefully aligned one of the slit position angles ( =67^ .0 ) so that it would include the nearby spiral galaxy to the southwest of the target (Figure fig:unusual_images , middle). This spiral galaxy has photometric redshifts from SDSS of z_ photo =0.083 0.017 (measured with the template fitting method) and z_ photo =0.066 0.022 (measured with a Neural Network method), which are similar to the target galaxy's spectroscopic redshift z=0.073 . If the spiral galaxy is indeed at a similar redshift as the target galaxy, then the spatially extended emission we observe (Figure fig:unusual_spectra , middle) could be .

We measured the wavelength of the centroid of the emission (as in Section measure ), and if the emission is indeed then this wavelength corresponds to a spectroscopic redshift z=0.071 or a velocity offset 600 km s ^ 1 blueward of the target galaxy. This is suggestive of the type of rich environment that we expect for dual AGNs, which are created from galaxy mergers.

sources.summary: The paper describes SPRC-1 as a red early-type AGN galaxy with an orthogonal polar dust ring, luminous polar halo, and compact-group tidal environment suggesting a wet merger.

quotes.quote: SPRC-1 is a red early-type galaxy with a polar dust ring which is aligned almost orthogonally to the major axis of the host galaxy ( =84.5 ). Its optical spectrum is typical for active galactic nucleus galaxies with broad emission lines. Its polar dust structure looks like a nearly edge-on dust ring. The visible dust lane noticeably distorts the observed galaxy profile in the central region, therefore we mask it in our modelling. The optical diameter of the ring reaches 18 kpc 2011AstL...37..171R .

sources.summary: The paper leaves J0009 0036 ambiguous: it has a single NIR nucleus, a nearby minor structure not contributing emission, and needs higher-resolution follow-up.

quotes.quote: J0009 0036 . In this object the two \ components are spatially offset by <0.2 \ in the 2d spectrum, and the NIR image shows a single nucleus. Its NIR image shows a minor structure 1 \ south-east from the center of the peak NIR emission, which is not contributing to the \ emission. A better slit position and higher resolution imaging and spectroscopy are needed to rule out the existence of a companion at <0.2 \ separation.

gmw_00001896.name: SPRC-4
gmw_00001896.overview: SPRC-4 is a kinematically confirmed polar-ring galaxy dominated by a central elliptical-like body [arXiv:1202.5033, arXiv:2208.12943]. It features a blue polar ring with inner star formation [arXiv:1202.5033, arXiv:2208.12943], surrounded by chain-like loops that may represent spiralling material [arXiv:2208.12943].

sources.summary: PRG 3 is a small galaxy with blue nuclear-side emission, probable inner star formation, a dominant elliptical-like body, and a barely visible faint polar structure.

quotes.quote: PRG 3 (SDSS J024258.42-005709.3): Examining the publicly available SDSS colour image of this small galaxy reveals the presence of blue emission on both sides of the nucleus. Light from these blue regions probably entered the SDSS fiber aperture and is likely related to the star formation activity detected by analyzing the prominent emission lines in the inner spectra. The light from the central elliptical-like body dominates the inner isophotes whereas the faint polar structure is barely visible against the host background starlight in single SDSS exposures. We count at least 10 galaxies in the vicinity of PRG 3, with the nearest one at a projected distance of about 0.4 Mpc. The images and central spectra of these neighbours indicate that star-forming galaxies are frequent in this environment.

sources.summary: The paper identifies SPRC-4 as a kinematically confirmed polar-ring galaxy with a blue ring, surrounding chain-like loops, and likely gas accretion or dwarf disruption.

quotes.quote: SPRC-4 It is a kinematically-confirmed PRG 2014ASPC..486...71M . The deep image of this galaxy reveals the presence of a blue polar-ring which was previously noted by fink2012 . The small S ersic index of the PS model n=0.2 confirms that this structure is a ring. We notice two chain-like structures around SPRC-4 which seem to be spiralling towards the host galaxy. In fact, these structures can be parts of fainter extended loops of gaseous and stellar material.

gmw_00001897.name: SPRC-69
gmw_00001897.overview: SPRC-69 is a nearby polar-ring galaxy featuring a central body surrounded by a highly inclined, luminous polar ring [arXiv:1202.5033, arXiv:1107.1966]. This polar structure contains ionized gas, stars, and internal dust, with faint spiral-like structures extending from its ends [arXiv:1202.5033, arXiv:2208.12943].

sources.summary: The paper compares SPRC-69's ionized-gas and CO rotation velocities, finds it mildly offset from the normal Tully-Fisher relation, and notes its polar structure is brighter than its host.

quotes.quote: For SPRC-69 the ionized gas velocity field yields Vmax=178 3 km/s, (172 after projecting with the inclination of 76 ^ of the polar ring) a value also in agreement with the CO(1-0) value 193 40 km/s. These comparisons show that we are not underestimating too much the maximum velocities of the polar rings, even though the CO beam might not encompass all the optical extent of it.

In Fig. fig:TF , one of the PRGs falls in the expected range for a spherical potential (SPRC-14), two are mildly offset (SPRC-69 and SPRC-42), and two are very far from the usual relation, with very broad velocities (SPRC-48 and SPRC-47). This large scatter might indicate different formation mechanisms for the systems, and at least four out of the five should have their dark matter halo aligned with the polar plane. The position of the five PRGs in the TF diagram does not appear related to the mass ratio between the polar disk/ring and the host. The galaxies SPRC-42 and SPRC-69 have polar structures brighter than their hosts, while SPRC-14 and SPRC-48 are comparable, and SPRC-47 is weaker than its host.

sources.summary: PRG 15 has the lowest recession velocity in the sample, a conspicuous luminous ring-like polar structure, a faint ring tail, internal dust, and line-emitting clumps.

quotes.quote: PRG 15 (SDSS J204805.66+000407.8): The recession velocity of this object is the lowest in our sample. The peculiar appearance of this object was noted already by Zwicky Zwicky (1971) and the extended, luminous polar structure can be seen clearly even on single SDSS frames. The dim light within an outer bright rim gives the polar structure a ring-like appearance. The ring light does not drop abruptly to zero on both sides of the ring and a faint tail extends from the north-east side of the ring. Strong internal dust extinction is detected along the north-west side of the ring.

sources.summary: The paper describes SPRC-69 as an older, relaxed polar-ring system whose wide ring may be a disc formed by merger or tidal accretion.

quotes.quote: SPRC-69 (II Zw 92) Its deep image displays that the outer, almost polar wide ring ( =77.5 , n=0.3 ) surrounds the central body ( n=2.4 ). The results of spectral observations confirm the presence of a PS, in both ionised gas and stars moiseev2011 . Along the major axis of the central body, moiseev2011 also point out a linear increase in the rotation velocity of stars. Our deep and colour images clearly show faint bound structures (spirals?) which start from the ends of the polar-ring. This may indicate that the polar ring is in fact a disc.

sources.summary: The paper identifies SPRC-69 as the nearest observed galaxy in its subset and says SDSS imaging and spectroscopy confirm an inclined polar ring with ionized gas and stars.

quotes.quote: SPRC-69=II Zw 92 This is the most nearby galaxy we observed. The notes to the catalogue by Zwicky1971 indicate that this galaxy ``appears like Saturn''. The SDSS colour image clearly demonstrates how the outer highly inclined ring surrounds the central galaxy (Fig. fig_example ). The results of spectral observations confirm the presence of the polar ring, which reveals both ionized gas and stars (see the slit position with PA(1)=34 ).

gmw_00001898.name: SPRC-73
gmw_00001898.overview: SPRC-73 is a star-forming polar-structure galaxy [arXiv:1202.5033, arXiv:2208.12943] that features a diffuse disc inclined relative to its host body [arXiv:1202.5033, arXiv:2208.12943]. The system displays a long, curved tidal tail [arXiv:1202.5033, arXiv:2208.12943] and may be undergoing multiple minor mergers [arXiv:2208.12943].

sources.summary: PRG 1 has a diffuse disc, a long tidal tail apparently connected to its polar ring, no nearby neighbours, and central emission characteristic of star formation.

quotes.quote: PRG 1 (SDSS J003209.80+010836.5): A puffy, diffuse disc is inclined by 20 ^ to the polar axis of the main body. The deep Stripe82 image reveals a long tidal tail of faint luminous material which appears to connect to the polar ring at its northern side where a diffuse stellar source, the suspect donor, is detected. The tidal feature twists around the host and extends about one arcmin to the south. We find no other galaxies in the neighbourhood of PRG1. The SDSS spectrum shows significant central emission characteristic of a star-forming galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper interprets SPRC-73 as a star-forming polar-structure system with a giant curved tail and high polar-structure luminosity indicating an ongoing major merger.

quotes.quote: SPRC-73 The spectrum of SPRC-73 is typical for star forming galaxies. The major axis of the diffuse PS is inclined by 63 with respect to the major axis of the host galaxy. There is also a bright blue point source (a bulge or a nucleus of the victim galaxy?) to the north of the centre which lies exactly in the plane of the diffuse polar component and has the same redshift z=0.059 as the host galaxy. In addition, we can see a faint arc with another red dwarf galaxy SDSS J003208.55+010834.5 to the west. Many small extended objects around SPRC-73 may also be involved in the multiple (wet and dry) minor merging with the host galaxy (for example, there is a faint bridge with SDSS J003207.63+010847.4 at the north-western outskirts of SPRC-73).

gmw_00001899.name: SPRC-74
gmw_00001899.overview: SPRC-74 is a distorted polar-ring candidate galaxy featuring a bent, asymmetric blue structure that crosses its main body [arXiv:1202.5033, arXiv:2208.12943]. The host galaxy exhibits distorted outer isophotes and appears to be undergoing a gravitational interaction with a diffuse, irregular companion to its northeast [arXiv:1202.5033, arXiv:2208.12943].

sources.summary: PRG 2 is described as a peculiar disturbed system with a bent polar structure, distorted main-body isophotes, diffuse outer light, and signs of recent or ongoing interaction.

quotes.quote: PRG 2 (SDSS J004812.18-001255.5): This peculiar galaxy lies close to the celestial equator and was imaged a number of times by the SDSS. An asymmetric elongated structure crosses the main body at an angle of about 15 ^ from the polar axis. The arc-like structure is strongly bent and displaced relative to the nucleus, and shows a diffuse irregular clump at its east side. The main body shows distorted outer isophotes and its surface brightness drops abruptly at the west side compared to the opposite side.
