A weak enhancement is observed at the north side of the host and faint diffuse light is detected outside the galaxy to its north-east.

sources.summary: The paper describes SPRC-74 as a distorted polar-ring candidate whose bent blue structure, diffuse companion, stream, and bridge imply tidal accretion from a gas-rich neighbor.

quotes.quote: SPRC-74 A blue asymmetric elongated structure crosses the main body of SPRC-74 at an angle of about 75 to the major axis. The PS is strongly bent and displaced relative to the centre of the host galaxy, which shows a S ersic index close to exponential ( n=1.3 ). We can distinguish a diffuse, LSB galaxy of irregular shape on the north-eastern side of SPRC-74 and a faint short stream to the east. The main body of the host galaxy demonstrates distorted outer isophotes and bends towards the diffuse LSB galaxy in the north-east. The disturbed shape of both the main and diffuse galaxies hints at the ongoing gravitational interaction.

gmw_00001900.name: SPRC-76
gmw_00001900.overview: SPRC-76 is a strongly distorted system undergoing a major merger that is generating its polar structure [arXiv:2208.12943]. The red host galaxy is surrounded by blue merger debris, with tidal features including a curved tail, a plume, and multiple bridges connecting to nearby galaxies [arXiv:2208.12943].

sources.summary: The paper describes SPRC-76 as a strongly distorted system where blue merger debris, bridges, and plumes indicate an ongoing major merger generating its polar structure.

quotes.quote: SPRC-76 The red host galaxy in SPRC-76 is surrounded by the blue material of the merging galaxy, the debris (its profile has n=1.1 ) of which we observe as the blue object SDSS J012129.26+003732.6 (it has a concordant z=0.04386 ) with the blue curved tail emerging from it. Also, we can see a clear bridge (depicted by a maroon colour in Fig. 1) which connects the main body and the red satellite SDSS J012129.86+003719.7. Furthermore, we detect a plume on the eastern side of SPRC-76 to the north, up to another dwarf galaxy SDSS J012130.98+003745.6. Possibly, there is also a link with the spiral galaxy SDSS J012130.98+003745.6 to the north-east. We also detect a faint bridge with the LSB galaxy SDSS J012132.24+003758.6 which is located beyond the previously mentioned spiral in the same direction.

gmw_00001901.name: SPRC-77
gmw_00001901.overview: SPRC-77 is a galaxy featuring a distinctive oval central host embedded within a blue, wide polar ring [arXiv:2208.12943]. Deep imaging also reveals an oval envelope that appears misaligned with the major axis of the polar ring [arXiv:2208.12943].

sources.summary: The paper describes SPRC-77 as having an oval host embedded in a blue wide polar ring plus an envelope and nearby interaction signatures, likely from a past major merger.

quotes.quote: SPRC-77 fink2012 note on SPRC-77 and 185 that they ``resemble `normal' disc galaxies with a symmetric bright disc around their central bulge rather than typical PRGs''. However, careful examination of the deep image and its photometric decomposition reveals that SPRC-77 has a distinctive oval central component (the host), which is embedded in the blue wide polar ring ( n=0.1 ). This galaxy demonstrates quite regular inner and outer isophotes. Our deep image also reveals an oval envelope ( n=1.1 ), the major axis of which seems to be misaligned with the major axis of the ring We will consider this galaxy, along with SPRC-185, in a separate paper. Here we only present the results of our multicomponent (host+ring+halo) decomposition.

gmw_00001902.name: SPRC-185
gmw_00001902.overview: SPRC-185 is an edge-on disk galaxy featuring a dominant bulge, a flared disk, and a nucleus [arXiv:2208.12943]. Rather than a typical polar ring, it possesses an orthogonal, boxy polar halo and an eastern plume likely associated with merger-driven halo formation [arXiv:2208.12943].

sources.summary: The paper describes SPRC-185 as an edge-on galaxy with an orthogonal smooth boxy polar halo and an eastern plume probably linked to merger-driven halo formation.

quotes.quote: SPRC-185 SPRC-185 is another pure edge-on galaxy in our sample. The smooth outer structure, which is almost orthogonal to the central galaxy, becomes prominent only in our deep image. It has a boxy shape and does not resemble ``normal'' polar ring galaxies from our sample. Our decomposition model for this galaxy consists of a nucleus, a dominant bulge, a flared disc, and a polar component with the S ersic index n 1.5 . Thus, the PS in this galaxy is not a ring, but a halo.

gmw_00001903.name: SPRC-186
gmw_00001903.overview: SPRC-186 is a disk galaxy that features a reddish inner disk, which is classified as a pseudobulge [arXiv:2208.12943]. The galaxy also hosts a blue inner ring and two spiral arms, with no low-surface-brightness details detected around it [arXiv:2208.12943].

sources.summary: The paper rejects SPRC-186 as a polar-ring galaxy, instead interpreting it as a reddish inner disc with a blue inner ring and spiral arms and no low-surface-brightness details.

quotes.quote: SPRC-186 Our decomposition shows that this galaxy has a reddish inner disc (a pseudobulge with n 1 ), a blue inner ring and two spiral arms (as a single component with n=0.6 ), instead of an outer ring which might be interpreted as tilted with respect to the inner component. Therefore, this suggests that SPRC-186 is not a PRG. Unlike most polar-ring galaxies in our sample, we see no LSB details around it.

gmw_00001904.name: SPRC-188
gmw_00001904.overview: SPRC-188 is a highly inclined galaxy that features a dust lane and an off-center, edge-on polar structure [arXiv:2208.12943]. Its highly asymmetric and disturbed morphology is likely influenced by interactions with several large neighboring galaxies and multiple dwarf galaxies that show connecting bridges [arXiv:2208.12943].

sources.summary: The paper describes SPRC-188 as a highly inclined galaxy with an off-center polar edge-on structure, bridges to dwarfs, disturbed morphology, and a likely older encounter origin.

quotes.quote: SPRC-188 This highly inclined galaxy with a prominent dust lane shows a polar edge-on structure ( 83 ), which is slightly off the centre and is more pronounced in the south direction than to the north. We also see several dwarf galaxies which show bridges with SPRC-188. The galaxy SDSS J033405.00+010541.0 to the west in the plane of the main body of SPRC-188 demonstrates a tidal feature which goes outwards. The overall shape is highly asymmetric and disturbed, possibly due to the recent formation of the PS (the ring also looks disturbed but does not have any fine structures connected to it) and interaction with several large neighbours (the irregular galaxy SDSS J033406.02+010615.0 to the north and the triplet of interacting galaxies to the east -- all of them have concordant redshifts z 0.048 ).

gmw_00001905.name: SPRC-234
gmw_00001905.overview: SPRC-234 is a galaxy with a central body likely consisting of a highly inclined lenticular galaxy [arXiv:2208.12943]. Deep imaging reveals a faint, low surface brightness two-armed spiral structure rather than a classical polar ring [arXiv:1202.5033, arXiv:2208.12943], which exhibits spectral emission characteristic of star formation activity [arXiv:1202.5033].

sources.summary: PRG 16 has faint extended structure revealed by stacked Stripe82 images, two spiral arm-like features, weak resemblance to classical PRGs, and star-forming spectral emission.

quotes.quote: PRG 16 (SDSS J212339.15-002235.2): This galaxy is an example of an object with faint extended structure that could not have been detected in single SDSS exposures. Stacking the multiple SDSS images, taken as part as the Stripe82 project, reveals two arm-like features spiralling out from both sides of the main body. PRG 16 does not resemble classical PRGs and is the least promising PRG candidate in our sample. In fact, it is the only object in our sample classified in the SPRC as a `related object', rather than a `good' or `best' candidate. The SDSS spectrum of PRG 16 shows significant emission characteristic of star formation activity.

sources.summary: The paper presents SPRC-234 as uncertain: it may be a PRG host or projection, with a faint two-armed spiral-like structure rather than a clear ring.

quotes.quote: SPRC-234 The central object in SPRC-234 is likely a highly inclined S0 galaxy ( n=1.8 ). The ordinary SDSS image of this galaxy does show a tilted structure with respect to the central body. However, the deep image displays it as a very faint two-armed spiral rather than a ring. Nevertheless, there is also a possibility that these spiral arms are tightly wound into a ring, similar to NGC 660. The average surface brightness along these arms is indeed very low ( _ 26  mag arcsec ^ 2 and the estimated arms-to-total ratio is unusually low: f_ 0.07 ).

gmw_00001906.name: SPRC-238
gmw_00001906.overview: SPRC-238 is an interacting galaxy that exhibits multiple signatures of interaction with its neighbors [arXiv:2208.12943]. These features include an arc above the galaxy plane and a pronounced, straight tidal tail to the east, likely caused by the tidal disruption of a gas-rich dwarf galaxy [arXiv:2208.12943].

sources.summary: The paper describes SPRC-238 as an interacting galaxy with an arc, straight tidal tail, and polar structure most plausibly produced by disruption of a gas-rich dwarf.

quotes.quote: SPRC-238 This galaxy shows multiple signatures of interaction with its neighbours which distorted its shape, such as an arc above the galaxy plane in the north-west direction. Perhaps, the other side of this PS is covered by the galaxy disc, so that we cannot observe it. The deep image demonstrates that there is a pronounced straight tidal tail to the east of the galaxy with a north-east direction. The colour of this tail is similar to that of the host galaxy. The most plausible scenario for generating the observed PS in SPRC-238 is tidal disruption of a gas-rich dwarf ( L_ ps /L_ h 0.1 ), the debris of which is either covered by the host galaxy body, or completely stretched by the tidal forces, so that we do not see any nucleus left (although we note a small clumpy structure right above the plane).

gmw_00001907.name: SPRC-275
gmw_00001907.overview: SPRC-275 is visually connected to its close twin galaxy, SDSS J205354.20-005814.3, though their gravitational connection remains unconfirmed [arXiv:2208.12943]. The galaxy features a bluish ring and a poorly resolved central component that may contain a tiny bulge embedded in a bar [arXiv:2208.12943].

sources.summary: The paper argues SPRC-275 likely has a blue ring in the plane of a central bulge/bar structure rather than a true polar ring, despite visual connection to a twin galaxy.

quotes.quote: SPRC-275 This galaxy is visually connected to its close twin, SDSS J205354.20-005814.3, although we cannot determine its redshift and confirm their real gravitational bound. Also, we do not see any faint structures in their vicinity. Although these two galaxies look similar, the main difference between them is the bluish colour of the ring ( n=0.2 ) in SPRC-275, which points to a different origin of the observed structure. The central component in this galaxy is poorly resolved but its S ersic index n=1.5 is not typical of a bar. However, we can see a nucleus which may be, in fact, a tiny bulge embedded in a bar.

gmw_00001908.name: GRB 020127
gmw_00001908.overview: The host galaxy of GRB 020127 is classified as an extremely red object [arXiv:1606.08285]. It has a high stellar mass, a high metallicity, and a modest unobscured star formation rate of 6 solar masses per year [arXiv:1606.08285].

sources.summary: The paper describes the GRB 020127 host as a very red, extremely red object with high stellar mass, modest unobscured star formation, and likely high metallicity.

quotes.quote: GRB 020127 was a HETE II-detected burst with T90 5 sec Ricker1229 . Chandra follow-up observations revealed an X-ray counterpart Fox1249, Fox1306 , and also a faint radio counterpart was identified Fox1250 , but no optical afterglow. The spectral energy distribution of the host galaxy is very red, leading to an ERO (extremely red object) classification. Using a dust-obscured star-forming galaxy template, derive the following parameters: a photometric redshift of 1.9 ^ +0.2 _ 0.4 , an absolute rest-frame magnitude M_ AB (B) = 23.5 0.1 mag, a stellar mass of the host in the range 10 ^ 11 10^ 12 \ (consistent with a more recent estimate of 10 ^ 11.51 \ by ), and an unobscured star formation rate of 6 /yr. Furthermore, a comparison to the mass-metallicity relation of UV-selected galaxies at similar redshift indicates that the host of GRB 020127 has a high metallicity, in the range of about 0.5-1 \ Berger07 .

gmw_00001909.name: GRB 020819B
aliases_external: GRB 020819B z=1.9621 probable host / radio-position blob
gmw_00001909.overview: The host of GRB 020819B is a massive, star-forming barred spiral galaxy located at a redshift of approximately 0.41 [arXiv:1606.08285, arXiv:1602.00188]. It has a large angular size [arXiv:1402.4006], high metallicity [arXiv:1606.08285, arXiv:1602.00188], and one of the highest dust masses relative to its star-formation rate [arXiv:1402.4006].

sources.summary: The paper describes the GRB 020819B host as a barred spiral at z=0.41, with high stellar mass and metallicity, while its radio emission is likely afterglow contaminated.

quotes.quote: GRB 020819B was a HETE II-detected burst with T90 20 sec Vanderspek02 (and is indeed GRB 020819B, though most papers in the literature designate this burst as GRB 020819). While even deep near-infrared imaging did not reveal an afterglow Klose1520 , VLA observations at 8.46 GHz revealed a radio afterglow, declining from about 380 Jy to < 35 Jy over the course of 150 days Frail1842 . The underlying host galaxy, a 8 \ diameter barred spiral, was identified by at z=0.41, with the GRB position about 3 \ off the galaxy core. The best-fit galaxy model implies a stellar mass of 10 ^ 10.4 , extinguished by A_ V = 2.2 0.4 mag kgk10 . The burst occurred in a high (about ) metallicity environment (host and burst site have similar metallicity) Levesque10, Graham15 .

The only clearly detected source in our sample is GRB 020819B at z=0.41. The star-formation rate implied by our radio detection is SFR _ Radio = 20.2 5.2 /yr. This is consistent with the 2 upper limit from of < 22.6 /yr, re-computed as described above. Accounting for the error in our measurement, our SFR _ Radio is only 50 larger than the H based SFR _ H = 10.2 /yr from (no error given). However, it is surprising that no radio emission is detected from the nucleus of the host galaxy itself (Fig. 020819B_zoom ).

aliases_in_paper: higher-redshift galaxy at the GRB position
sources.summary: The paper identifies a compact dusty star-forming z=1.96 galaxy at the radio afterglow position as the likely GRB 020819B host and estimates a LIRG-like star-formation rate.

quotes.quote: The probable radio afterglow is coincident with a compact, highly star-forming, dusty galaxy at z=1.9621 . The revised redshift naturally explains the apparent nondetection of CO(3-2) line emission at the afterglow site from ALMA.

No strong constraints can currently be placed on either the metallicity or the molecular gas mass in the higher-redshift galaxy at the GRB position with our data, although the metallicity is probably subsolar. The galaxy at z=1.96 is a remarkable object in its own right, and may be relevant to understanding GRB progenitors if indeed this source is genuinely the host of GRB 020819B.

A brief summary of the available multiwavelength constraints of the new, probable host galaxy is provided in Table tab:hostproperties . The H luminosity and the millimetre flux (fit against a variety of starburst galaxy templates; Silva+1998,Michalowski+2010 ) both imply an SFR of between 30--120 M_ yr ^ 1 , consistent with a luminous infrared galaxy (LIRG).

sources.summary: The host of GRB 020819B is a relatively local, large angular-size GRB host with SPIRE detections and one of the highest dust masses relative to star formation in the detected sample.

quotes.quote: After background subtraction, the flux densities of the entire host galaxy at all \ wavelengths were obtained in apertures of radius 6 , except for the host of GRB 020819B for which we used an aperture of radius 13 . This larger aperture was used because of the large angular size of the GRB 020819B host levesque10b .
We detect 7 GRBHs with \ at redshifts ranging from z=0.35-2.1 ; all of these detections are PACS, but GRBH 020819B ( z=0.4 ) also has SPIRE detections.

In Figs. fig:duststars and fig:dustsfr , the two (IR-detected) galaxies with the highest dust mass relative to their SFR or sSFR are GRB 020819B and 090417B, both relatively local galaxies with z 0.4 and z 0.3 , respectively. These hosts have properties very similar to the z 0 galaxies we have included in the comparison.

sources.summary: The paper describes the GRB 020819 host galaxy as a massive star-forming spiral at z 0.41, with the burst site in a spiral arm and high metallicities in both nucleus and explosion site.

quotes.quote: By contrast, GRB 020819 had a ``cosmological" luminosity more typical of the general LGRB population and was localized in a much more distant and massive star-forming spiral galaxy, at z 0.41 . It was also classified as a ``dark" GRB, which are characterized by drop-outs in the optical regimes of their afterglows (e.g. Perley et al. \ 2013); however, radio afterglow observations were able to localize the explosion site of this burst in a small star-forming region located in one of the galaxy's spiral arms. Observations showed that both the host nucleus and the explosion site had comparable metallicities; however, both were also quite high, marking this as the first confirmed observation of an LGRB produced in a high-metallicity environment (Levesque et al. \ 2010b).

gmw_00001910.name: GRB 050219
gmw_00001910.overview: GRB 050219 is an early-type host galaxy located at a redshift of z=0.211 [arXiv:1606.08285]. It has a stellar mass of 10^9.98 solar masses and exhibits a very low star-formation rate of 0.06 solar masses per year [arXiv:1606.08285].

sources.summary: The paper identifies the GRB 050219 host as an early-type galaxy at z=0.211, with very low star formation and a measured stellar mass.

quotes.quote: GRB 050219 was a Swift-detected burst with T90 = 23 sec Hullinger05 . No optical/radio afterglow was detected. identify a 6 \ diameter early-type galaxy at the border of the 1 9 UVOT-enhanced X-ray error circle. A VLT/X-shooter spectrum reveals a redshift of z=0.211, and a surprisingly low star-formation rate of 0.06 ^ +0.01 _ 0.02 /yr was derived based on the non-resolved [ O II ] doublet Rossi14 . Based on the spectral energy distribution, further derive a stellar mass of 10 ^ 9.98 .

gmw_00001911.name: GRB 100621A
gmw_00001911.overview: The host galaxy of GRB 100621A is a bright, blue galaxy [arXiv:1108.0674] located at a redshift of z = 0.542 [arXiv:1108.0674, arXiv:1606.08285]. It features a young stellar population with low stellar mass [arXiv:1108.0674] and exhibits active star formation [arXiv:1108.0674, arXiv:1407.8180].

sources.summary: The host of GRB 100621A is a bright z=0.542 galaxy, blue despite the red afterglow, with a young stellar population, low stellar mass, and active star formation.

quotes.quote: The host of GRB 100621A The host of GRB 100621A was reported very early after the trigger as a DSS2 source, providing a constant contribution to the afterglow in the UV/blue light curve 2010GCN.10874....1U, 2010GCN.10878....1O . In fact, the redshift of z=0.542 of GRB 100621A is based on emission lines from a bright host 2010GCN.10876....1M . The SED of the ^ 21.5 mag host is well-sampled from the UV to the NIR and shown in Fig. hostfigs .

In strong contrast to the extremely red afterglow (R-K)_ AB 5.8 mag, the host is blue with an inferred color of (R-K)_ AB 0.3 mag and (uvw2-K)_ AB 0.9 mag . The SPS host fit returns an intrinsic extinction of A'_V=0.6_ 0.2 ^ +0.1 mag for a very young stellar population of age =0.05^ +0.07 _ 0.03 Gyr. This host has the lowest stellar mass in the presented sample with (M_ ) = 8.98^ +0.14 _ 0.10 , and an absolute magnitude of M_B=-20.68 0.08 mag , which is 0.6 L^ at z 0.5 . The SFR and SSFR are 13_ 5 ^ +6 /yr and 14 Gyr ^ 1 , respectively.

sources.summary: The paper argues that GRB 100621A's late radio flux is likely host-galaxy emission, deriving a 5.5 GHz flux and high star-formation rate for the host.

quotes.quote: However, given the lack of evidence for fading or other significant time variation in this source at either 5.5 or 9.0 GHz, we suggest that the constant flux is at least as likely to arise from underlying star formation in the host galaxy, rather than the burst afterglow. We make this assumption in the analysis that follows, while noting that it is inevitably subject to debate. Combining all available ATCA data in a single image (and thereby improving uv plane coverage and sensitivity), we determine a best 5.5 GHz flux for the host of GRB 100621A of 142 19 Jy, corresponding to a star formation rate of 119 16 M _ yr ^ 1 (well within the range of star formation rates seen for the GRB population, see next section) - consistent with each of the reported individual measurements to within their errors.

A relevant example is that of GRB 100621A, which has a measured obscuration from the burst itself of A _v=3.8 , while a fit to the SED of the host yields just A _V=0.6 2011A&A...534A.108K . The star formation associated with the burst clearly occured in, or behind, a heavily obscured region, and the optical data are not representative of this extinguished population. Since radio wavelengths are less affected by dust extinction than the ultraviolet and optical, we might expect this flux to be a more complete measurement of the galaxy star formation rate, and to exceed the optically-derived values if substantial quantities of dust are present in the host galaxy.

Comparison of our late time measurements with published data suggests that the radio flux reported at early times by 2013A&A...560A..70G in GRB 100621A likely arises from the host galaxy rather than the burst afterglow as previously suggested.

sources.summary: The paper describes the GRB 100621A host as faint and dust-reddened, with several emission lines and a metallicity solution constrained by combining R23 with another diagnostic.

quotes.quote: GRB 100621A ( z=0.543 ) is a low- z GRB with a faint and dust-reddened afterglow 2013A&A...560A..70G . The host spectrum has, in addition to well-detected , , , and emission lines, a significant detection of (but not ). The degeneracy of the double-peaked R_ 23 probability distribution in metallicity is clearly evident in Fig. fig:zexamples . It is, however, broken through . Even though the individual based metallicity is not well constrained, in combination with R_ 23 we can derive accurately to =8.52 0.10 with only a very small remaining probability for the lower branch solution of the R_ 23 diagnostic.

sources.summary: The paper describes the GRB 100621A host as a visible z=0.542 galaxy with measured luminosity, stellar mass, star formation rate, metallicity, and radio flux constraints.

quotes.quote: GRB 100621A was a Swift-detected burst with a duration of about 100 sec with a bright X-ray afterglow Ukwatta10870 , but a highly dust-extinguished optical/NIR afterglow Updike10874, gkn13 , suffering an extinction of A_ V = 3.8 mag kgs11 . At a redshift of z=0.542 Milvang-Jensen10876 , the host is bright enough to be seen in the DSS2 Updike10874 , with an absolute magnitude of M_ B = -20.68 0.08 mag, stellar mass of log(M / ) = 8.98 ^ +0.14 _ 0.10 , and a host extinction-corrected (SED-derived) SFR _ UV = 13 ^ +6 _ 5 /yr kgs11 . Based on the H line in a X-shooter spectrum of the host, derive SFR _ H = 8.7 0.8 /yr. estimate a metallicity of 0.4 , while derives 0.68 . ATCA radio observations during 15--19 April 2011 at 5.5 and 9 GHz yield fluxes of F(5.5 GHz) = 120 32 and F(9.0 GHz) = 106 42 Stanway14 .

gmw_00001912.name: Andromeda II
gmw_00001912.overview: Andromeda II is a dwarf spheroidal satellite of M31 [arXiv:1505.05412, arXiv:1606.09259] that is devoid of gas [arXiv:1606.09259] and ceased star formation approximately 5 to 6 Gyr ago [arXiv:1606.01207, arXiv:1411.3324]. It exhibits a strong metallicity gradient [arXiv:1505.05412, arXiv:2206.08988] and a peculiar prolate rotation [arXiv:1505.05412, arXiv:1708.03311], which likely originated from a past gas-rich dwarf-dwarf merger [arXiv:1505.05412, arXiv:1809.04653].

sources.summary: The paper compares And II with And XXI as a dwarf-merger remnant, citing a stellar stream, two old populations, split RR Lyrae periods, and a bifurcated RGB.

quotes.quote: According to amo2014 , among the M31 satellites, And II is another example of dwarf merging remnant. This would explain the stellar stream they detected kinematically in the galaxy. The presence of two different old populations in And II is supported also by the analysis of the CMD and by the properties of the variable stars. The RR Lyrae stars of And II were studied by pri04 . Using the periods in Table 2 of pri04 , we performed a multi-gaussian fit and found two separate peaks, a first one at P=0.52 d with = 0.05 d and a second one at P=0.68 d with =0.01 d.

Hence, similarly to And XXI, also And II seems to host two distinct old stellar populations with different metallicity/age and corresponding different RR Lyrae populations, likely belonged to the two galaxies that merged in the past. The left panel of Figure  fig:comp shows the CMD of And II we obtained using HST archive data (Prop ID 6514, PI: Gary Da Costa). The galaxy RGB clearly bifurcates in two arms and blue and red HBs also seem to split.

sources.summary: The paper explains Andromeda II as a bright M31 dwarf spheroidal whose prolate rotation, stellar populations, and quiescent gas-free state can result from a dwarf-dwarf merger.

quotes.quote: Recently, a new merger candidate appeared in the form of the Andromeda II (And II) dSph galaxy, one of the brightest satellites of M31. The object has a number of peculiar features. ho12 detected strong rotation signal along the 2D minor axis which means that the object probably has a streaming motion around the longest axis in 3D, i.e. prolate rotation. Note that And II is the only dSph with a clear prolate rotation reported so far.

mcc07 identified a few distinct stellar populations in And II differing in age, metallicity and density profiles. However, ho12 found no significant difference in the kinematics of the metal rich and metal poor stars. The presence of the metallicity gradient was recently confirmed by ho15 and var14 . var14 also found an intermediate age population which makes And II an exception among their sample of six fainter dSphs that possess only populations of stars older than 10 Gyr. Moreover, am14 reported the detection of regions with lower velocity dispersion in the kinematic data of ho12 which can be interpreted as a presence of a stellar stream in And II.
And II most likely formed as a result of a merger between two disky gas-rich dwarf galaxies of similar mass and disk properties.

sources.summary: The paper presents HST photometry for And II, using resolved stellar populations to compare its CMD coverage, horizontal-branch features, and early star-formation history with other M31 dwarf spheroidals.

quotes.quote: The positions of the observed fields are shown in Figure f4 . As can be seen in Figure f4 , for And I and And II the ACS field covers only part of the galaxy within the half light radius and the parallel WFC3 field contains many member stars. Some member stars were detected in the WFC3 field of And III, but not nearly enough to produce a SFH with reasonable uncertainties. For And XV, And XVI, and And XXVIII, the ACS field of view covers most of the galaxy out to the half light radius and the WFC3 field is distant enough that a minimal number of member stars are expected.

Figure f5 shows that the ACS photometry reaches below the oldest main-sequence turn-offs allowing for very strong constraints on the oldest epochs of star formation. These observations of And I, And II, and And III are 2.5 mag fainter than the deepest CMDs previously obtained for these galaxies dacosta1996, dacosta2000, dacosta2002 . There are several notable features that are common to all six ACS CMDs. All six galaxies show the presence of blue horizontal branch stars (BHBs). This is generally taken as evidence of the presence of stars with ages comparable to the Milky Way GC population see discussion in gallart2005 . To date, all galaxies that have been observed with sufficient photometric depth have shown at least some star formation at the earliest times.

Figure f7 shows a variety of SFHs. However, all six SFHs have the common properties of starting their star formation early (there is significant star formation in the earliest time bin for all six galaxies), having at least 50 of their stars formed by a lookback time of 9 Gyr, and then ceasing all star formation by a lookback time of 6 Gyr. And XV and And XXVIII show the oldest mean stellar populations of the sample, but both are clearly not consistent with a single age population and are clearly not consistent with forming all of their stars before the epoch of reionization (as indicated in Figure f7 by the shaded region).

sources.summary: The paper models Andromeda II as a dwarf spheroidal formed by an early gas-rich major merger and later M31 interaction, reproducing its SFH, stellar populations, morphology, kinematics, and gas loss.

quotes.quote: Andromeda II (And II) has been known for a few decades but only recently observations have unveiled new properties of this dwarf spheroidal galaxy. The presence of two stellar populations, the bimodal star formation history (SFH) and an unusual rotation velocity of And II put strong constrains on its formation and evolution. Following Lokas2014 , we propose a detailed model to explain the main properties of And II involving (1) a gas-rich major merger between two dwarf galaxies at high redshift in the field and (2) a close interaction with M31 about 5 Gyr ago.

Other simulations are used to study the effects of tidal forces and the ram pressure stripping during the interaction between And II and M31. The model successfully reproduces the SFH of And II including the properties of stellar populations, its morphology, kinematics and the lack of gas.

In this study, we investigate specifically the origin of the Andromeda II (And II) dSph galaxy. This dSph discovered in 1972 VdB1972 lies in the vicinity of M31 at a distance of 184 kpc McConnachie2012 . It seems to be a typical, relatively luminous dSph devoid of gas, without star formation for the last several Gyr Hidalgo et al. in preparation Weisz2014, Skillman2016 , with a low central surface brightness of 24.8 mag arcsec ^ 2 and a quasi-spheroidal shape with ellipticity close to 0.2 del Pino et al. in preparation McConnachie2007 .

sources.summary: The paper models Andromeda II as a two-component dwarf spheroidal with distinct stellar populations, predicting a nearly flat velocity-dispersion profile and testable kinematic signatures.

quotes.quote: And II is a dSph satellite of M31 located at a distance of 650 kpc from the Milky Way ( mcconnachie2004a,mcconnachie2005a ). present its surface brightness profile based on Isaac Newton Telescope wide field imaging, and suggest that a discontinuity in the profile at 2^ may be evidence that it possesses a secondary ``core'' component. Deeper, follow-up imaging of And II was obtained by using the Subaru Suprime-Cam wide field camera. The black squares in Figure 1 reproduce the overall radial profile of And II derived using these data.

The deeper data allow the derivation of the radial profiles of distinct stellar populations; the bottom two profiles shown in the same panel trace the spatial distribution of horizontal branch stars (HB) and the reddest red-giant branch (RRGB) stars. Clearly, the HB stars are more spatially extended than the RRGB stars, which trace an exponential profile. As discussed in detail in , it is very likely that these two profiles represent the density distribution of two distinct stellar components in And II. Not only does an appropriately weighted sum of these two profiles fits the overall number density profile remarkably well, as indicated in Figure 1, but it also explains significant differences seen in the stellar populations at small and large radii.

sources.summary: The paper argues that Andromeda II is a dwarf spheroidal whose prolate rotation, stellar populations, and lack of gas are best explained by a past gas-rich dwarf merger.

quotes.quote: Andromeda II (And II) dwarf spheroidal galaxy (dSph) is a relatively luminous satellite of M31. It has a stellar mass of 10^7 M _ , a half-mass radius 1 kpc, and ellipticity 0.1--0.2 (e.g., and ). Unlike most dSphs, And II contains not only old stars ( >10 Gyr) but also significant intermediate-age population. The two stellar populations differ in age, metallicity, and density profiles ( ).

In a follow-up study, we performed a two-stage hydrodynamical simulation including cooling, star formation, and feedback. An equal-mass radial merger of disky dwarfs with initial gas fraction of 0.7 was followed by a ram-pressure stripping of the merger remnant in the hot gaseous halo of M31. Such a simulation successfully reproduces the star-formation history of And II, see Fig. fig2 , as well as other observed properties: the magnitude of the prolate rotation, the mass and shape, the lack of gas, and the spatial distribution of the two stellar populations.

sources.summary: Andromeda II is discussed as a dwarf whose prolate rotation can be explained by a merger, and whose two stellar populations have distinct kinematics.

quotes.quote: The merger scenario has been proposed as a way to explain the origin of prolate rotation in Andromeda II ( ). In this scenario, the progenitors are two disky dwarfs of equal mass and similar structural parameters approaching each other on a radial orbit. Their disks are initially inclined at the right angle but both have significant components of their angular momenta aligned with the merger axis. These parts of angular momenta are preserved after the merger in the form of prolate rotation. The remnant dwarf rotates around its long axis while the rotation around the short axis is close to zero.

Andromeda II has been recently shown to host at least two stellar populations with different kinematics. While the older population shows prolate rotation, the younger one rotates around the minor axis ( ).

sources.summary: The paper discusses And II as an M31 satellite with a kinematic twist interpreted as a possible dwarf-dwarf merger remnant, using simulations to argue such merger histories are plausible.

quotes.quote: This may be a valid assumption for many of the dwarf galaxies of the Local Group, though observations of kinematic and chemical peculiarities in, for example, Fornax battaglia06,deboer12,hendricks14 , Sculptor e.g., tolstoy04 , Carina e.g., venn12 and And II ho12 have led to the suggestion that external factors, such as dwarf-dwarf encounters, should be considered more seriously.

For satellites of higher mass ( M_ star > 10 ^ 6 M_ , corresponding to Fornax, Leo I, or Sculptor in the MW; And I, And II, or And VII in M31), 10 experienced a major merger since z = 1 . At lower masses ( M_ star = 10 ^ 5 - 6 M_ , corresponding to Sextans or Draco) these fractions drop to 5 , down to 3 at M_ star = 10 ^ 3 - 5 M_ , the mass regime of the ultra-faints. Thus, particularly for the most massive satellites of MW and M31, recent major mergers ( z 1 ) are not particularly rare.

Furthermore, the apparent kinematic twist of And II ho12 , a satellite of M31 with mass similar to Fornax, led amorisco14 to suggest that it is the remnant of a dwarf-dwarf major merger see also lokas14 . In the context of our results, these suggestions are plausible; specifically, 1 - 3 satellite dwarf galaxies in the halos of the MW and M31 probably experienced a major merger since z = 1 . Although dwarf-dwarf mergers are not particularly common, neither are they extremely rare. Thus, our results highlight the importance of considering mergers in interpreting the star formation histories, metallicity distribution functions, and chemical abundance patterns of dwarf galaxies, particularly for systems such as Fornax or And II which display kinematic irregularities.

sources.summary: The paper uses published kinematic and photometric membership criteria for And II, observes more carbon stars than predicted, and combines SPLASH and literature FBPS samples for its carbon-star population.

quotes.quote: In And II, the authors use a kinematical restriction, requiring member stars to be within 3 of the systemic velocity of the dSph ( 228 km s ^ 1 < v < -157 km s ^ 1 ). They then apply a photometric and spectroscopic cut, V-I < 2.5 and EW _ Na I < 4 , to further eliminate foreground MW contamination.
However, in And II, NGC 147, NGC 185 and NGC 205 we observe far more carbon stars than are predicted, despite the fact that our calculated C/M ratios match those in the literature.

In addition to the new fields discussed above, we also compile carbon star populations in And II, NGC 147 and NGC 185 from the literature. We combine our carbon star samples observed by SPLASH with these samples from the literature. The observations of And II Kerschbaum2004 , NGC 147 and NGC 185 Nowotny2003 also use FBPS, but with V and i_0 as the broad-band colors rather than R and I .

sources.summary: The paper maps Andromeda II's two stellar populations, finding different ages, metallicities, spatial distributions and kinematics, distinct kinematic position angles, and evidence they may be incompletely relaxed merger remnants.

quotes.quote: We analyze in detail the spatial distribution and kinematic properties of two different stellar populations in Andromeda II (And II) dwarf spheroidal galaxy. We obtained their detailed surface density maps, together with their radial density profiles. The two populations differ not only in age and metallicity, but also in their spatial distribution and kinematics.

The centres of rotation and kinetic position angles (PA _ kin ) of both populations differ. For intermediate-age stars we obtained PA _ kin = 18^ 2^ and for the old ones PA _ kin = 63^ 3^ in good agreement with photometric PA measured from isopleths fitted to the photometry. We conclude that the two stellar populations may not be fully relaxed and thus be the result of a merger occurred at redshift z 1.75 .

sources.summary: The HST census treats And II as an M31 dSph, detects 251 RRLs, and discusses its high-amplitude short-period variables within the massive-satellite subsample.

quotes.quote: Under the ISLAndS Initial Star formation and Lifetimes of Andromeda Satellites project (based on very deep, multi-epoch HST ACS and WFC3 data), six M31 dSph satellite companion galaxies were observed: And I, And II, And III, And XV, And XVI and And XXVIII.
We detect 870 RR Lyrae (RRL) stars in the fields of And I (296), II (251), III (111), XV (117), XVI (8), XXVIII (87).

We confirm this trend with the six ISLAndS galaxies, as HASPs have only been detected in the two most massive satellite galaxies: And I (3 The other two most likely belong to the M31 GSS, see sec:rrl_gss for further details. ) and And II (2). A detailed analysis of the chemical properties of RRL stars will be discussed in a forthcoming paper. It is worth noting that a few stars with HASP properties were already identified in the catalogs by Pritzl2004 and Pritzl2005 for And II and And I, respectively. In the case of And I, we confirm the HASP nature of 3 out of the 7 stars, while the period was likely underestimated for the other 4, possibly due to aliasing (see Appendix sec:comp_pritzl ). However, we do not confirm any of the 8 HASP stars in And II (see the Appendix sec:comp_pritzl for a detailed comparison with literature values). Nevertheless, we discovered 2 new HASPs in And I and 2 in And II, which are all located outside the WFPC2 field studied by Pritzl2004,Pritzl2005 .

sources.summary: The paper identifies Andromeda II among Local Group dwarfs with the strongest metallicity gradients, linking its chemo-kinematic evidence and likely past dwarf-dwarf merger to centrally renewed star formation.

quotes.quote: In our sample, several systems may have experienced a dwarf-dwarf merger in their past, namely: Andromeda II Amorisco2014,Lokas2014,delPino2015 , Phoenix Kacharov2017 , Sextans Cicuendez+Battaglia2018 , Fornax Battaglia2006,Amorisco+Evans2012,deBoer2012,delPino2015,Leung2020,Pace2021 , and NGC 6822 deBlok2000,deBlok2006,Demers2006,Battinelli2006,Hwang2014,Zhang2021 . Evidence supporting a past ( z 1 ) major merger in these dwarf galaxies includes the existence of stellar substructures (including the presence of shell-/stream-like features), and peculiar structural or internal kinematic properties (e.g., prolate rotation). We find in our analysis that the merger candidates are all characterised by a strong metallicity gradient, with _ Fe/H (R/R_e) values narrowly distributing around 0.35 dex R_e^ 1 (Figs. fig:mgrad_Dist fig:Lv_T90_mgrad ).

The strongest gradients in our sample, _ Fe/H (R/R_e) 0.25 dex R_e^ 1 , are observed in And II, Phoenix, Sextans, Fornax, and NGC 6822, all systems that in the literature have been proposed as likely to have experienced a past merger event, given the chemo-kinematic properties of their stellar component. Simulations have shown that a dwarf-dwarf merger event followed by a re-ignition of star formation activity in the galaxy's centre would indeed produce strong metallicity gradients Benitez-Llambay2016,Cardona-Barrero2021 . Excluding the merger candidates from the full distribution of metallicity gradients, the median _ Fe/H (R/R_e) reduce to 0.08 dex R_e^ 1 with an associated intrinsic scatter of only 0.05 dex R_e^ 1 .

sources.summary: The paper maps and observes And II because its velocity overlaps extended H I features, finding similar surrounding emission and only an upper limit on gas tied to the dwarf.

quotes.quote: And II And II, at a distance of 0.65 Mpc and V _ HEL = -188 km s ^ 1 (V _ LSR = -187 km s ^ 1 ) McConnachie2005,Cote1999 , is much closer to Galactic velocities than And XV. With an angular size 4 , the And II stars, like those of And XV, are well covered by a GBT beam. Spectra taken directly toward this dwarf show weak \ emission ( 30 mK) at the velocity of the galaxy. However, similar emission is seen at surrounding positions up to several degrees away.

Any \ coincident in position and velocity with the stellar component of this dwarf must have a 5 limit on N _ HI 7.7 10^ 17 cm ^ 2 for a 25 km s ^ 1 line, with an associated 5 \ mass limit M _ HI 1.4 10^4 M _ .

sources.summary: The paper identifies Andromeda II as a well-studied two-population dwarf, compares simulation luminosities to it, and argues its metal-rich population most likely formed during a gas-retaining pericentric passage.

quotes.quote: Sculptor, Fornax and Andromeda II, particularly well studied examples of two-population dwarfs, are identified on the plot. It can be seen that our sample contains galaxies of comparable luminosities to these three dwarfs.

The grey, green and purple bands show the mass ranges for Sculptor, Andromeda II and Fornax, respectively (assuming stellar mass-to-light ratio equal to 1) mcconnachieCensus . As satellites, Andromeda II and Fornax are most likely to have formed their metal-rich populations during their pericentric passages; however this scenario is dependent on these dwarfs passing though pericentre while retaining a sufficient supply of star-forming gas.
This scenario has some backing from observations of substructure and rotation in dwarfs like Fornax and Andromeda II.

sources.summary: The paper cites And II as an off-plane M31 dSph whose HST star-formation history resembles And XVI, with extended star formation 12-5 Gyr ago and quenching about 5 Gyr ago.

quotes.quote: Photometry of these systems suggest that they are `old', and possess many stars with ages >2 Gyr martin06,martin09,mcconnachie12 , and often possess RR-lyrae stars, which are at least 10 Gyrs old (e.g., And II, pritzl04 ). Recently weisz14a measured and compared the SFHs of And II (an off-plane satellite) and And XVI (on-plane) using HST imaging. They found that both had similar, extended star formation histories, with 50-70 of their stars forming 12 to 5 Gyrs ago. They were also both quenched 5 Gyrs ago, right around the time of the merger purported to have created the plane by hammer13 .

sources.summary: Andromeda II is cited as an M31 dwarf spheroidal with strong minor-axis prolate rotation, two stellar kinematic populations, and observed kinematics reproduced by dwarf-merger simulations.

quotes.quote: The satellite of M31, Andromeda II is a classical dwarf spheroidal galaxy for which reported a strong rotation signal along the minor axis, comparable in magnitude to the velocity dispersion. In a more thorough analysis of the same data, identified two kinematically different populations in this dwarf, one dominated by old stars with clear prolate rotation and another, composed mostly of intermediate-age stars, that appears to rotate around the optical minor axis.

The observed prolate rotation of Andromeda II and Phoenix could be an exceptional indicator of a past major merger of dwarf galaxies. In our previous work, and , we showed that it is rather impossible to obtain prolate rotation in pure tidal stirring scenario in which the satellite is transformed from disky to spheroidal by the tidal forces of the host galaxy see also lok15ts . However, we reproduced the observed kinematics of Andromeda II in controlled, self-consistent simulations of mergers between equal-mass disky dwarf galaxies on a radial, or close-to-radial orbit.

sources.summary: And II is selected as an M31 dSph with enough kinematic stars; the paper finds a prolate model favored and cites prior evidence for prolate rotation.

quotes.quote: For the M31 dSphs, we select five dSphs (And I, And II, And III, And V, and And VII) such that more than 50 stars which can be used as kinematic data, because for a dSph with less than 50 sample stars it is difficult to evaluate its two-dimensional velocity dispersion maps correctly as described below.

In this section, we describe the best-fit dark halo model for each of seven MW dSphs (Carina, Fornax, Sculptor, Sextans, Draco, Leo I, and Leo II) and five M31 dSphs (And I, And II, And III, And V, and And VII) and present confidence maps between each parameter.
It is found that for most of the dSphs, the oblate case yields a much better fit than the prolate one, whilst the ratios for some dSphs indicate the prolate one is better, especially for And II dSph.

sources.summary: The paper reports strong-significance stellar rotation in And II near the minor axis, with a kinematic axis offset from previous measurements and possible minor-merger origin.

quotes.quote: And II: Ho2012 detect =8.6 1.6 along the minor axis and a maximum =10.9 2.4 located at =113 9 the photometric position angle is = 46 6 , Ho2012 . Our kinematic axis is offset from this value: =1.43_ 0.17 ^ +0.18 ; =11.43_ 1.33 ^ +1.31 ; =-26 4 . We detect stellar rotation at strong significance near the minor axis, which could have been caused by a minor merger Amorisco2014 .

sources.summary: The paper treats Andromeda II as a dwarf spheroidal with peculiar kinematics, prolate rotation about the stellar minor axis, and dark-halo parameters that may contain systematics.

quotes.quote: Here, we note that the observational results from Sextans, Andromeda II and VII dSphs are significantly affected by the sizes of data samples and their peculiar stellar kinematics.

For Andromeda II, Hoetal2012 first inspected the kinematical properties of this galaxy and suggested that this is a prolate rotating system, that is, their stars rotate with respect to its stellar minor axis. This is a peculiar system having a rotation around the minor axis, to which our models cannot be properly applied.

sources.summary: The review describes And II as having prolate rotation, rotational properties that change with metallicity and age, and a debated stellar stream consistent with a disrupted smaller dwarf galaxy.

quotes.quote: \ rotation around the major axis, has been detected in And II Ho2012 and in Phoenix Kacharov2017 , while hints are found in Ursa Minor Pace2020 and in NGC6822 e.g. Belland2020 .

In And II, found the rotational properties to change with metallicity/age, in that the metal-poor/old stars do exhibit prolate rotation, but the metal-rich/intermediate-age ones do not. In And II, the detection of a stellar stream with stellar population properties compatible with being a disrupted smaller DG seemed to confirm this hypothesis Amorisco2014 , although such detection is controversial delPino2017AndII .

gmw_00001913.name: Sunburst Arc
gmw_00001913.overview: The Sunburst Arc is a strongly lensed galaxy at a redshift of z = 2.37 that acts as a Lyman continuum emitter [arXiv:2305.13405]. This galaxy contains a compact, vigorously star-forming region harboring a super star cluster that drives the escape of ionizing photons [arXiv:2305.13405].

sources.summary: The paper studies the Sunburst Arc as a strongly lensed z=2.37 LyC emitter whose compact starburst region enables anisotropic ionizing-photon escape.

quotes.quote: We investigate the Sunburst Arc: a strongly lensed, LyC emitter at z =2.37 . This galaxy reveals the exceptionally small scale (tens of parsecs) physics of LyC escape thanks to high magnification from strong lensing. Analyzing HST broadband and narrowband imaging, we find that the small ( < 100 pc) LyC leaking region shows distinctly extreme properties: a very blue UV slope ( = -2.9 0.1 ), high ionization state ( 5007 / 3727 \ = 11 3 and 5007 / \ =6.8 0.4 ), strong oxygen emission (EW( ) = 1095 40 \ ), and high Lyman- escape fraction ( 0.3 0.03 ), none of which are found in any non-leaking regions of the galaxy.

The Sunburst Arc is a clear example of a LyC escape process that is driven by a specific compact star-forming region within a galaxy. The compact and vigorously star-forming region is harbouring a super star cluster considering its small, compact size with extreme properties (Figures fig1 and fig2 and Table tab2 ). The super star cluster 1) produces an enormous amount of ionizing photons and 2) likely plays a key role in enabling the photons to escape into the IGM through specific lines of sight where there is little nebular continuum emission and dust screening.

gmw_00001914.name: ELISA
aliases_external: ELISA / G2, southern AGN near AO 0235+164
gmw_00001914.overview: ELISA is a faint active galactic nucleus [arXiv:0801.1236] classified as a Seyfert galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0701325]. It is located two arcseconds south of the blazar AO 0235+164 [arXiv:0801.1236, arXiv:astro-ph/0701325] and belongs to a galaxy group at a redshift of z=0.524 [arXiv:astro-ph/0701325].

sources.summary: The paper treats ELISA as a faint southern AGN contaminating AO 0235+164 photometry, adopting optical and UV fluxes so its contribution can be subtracted.

quotes.quote: A major point in the analysis of the optical and UV radiation coming from AO 0235+164 is to eliminate the contamination from the southern AGN, named ELISA by rai05 . Since it lies only 2 arcsec south of the source and it is very faint nil96 , this object is not resolved from AO 0235+164 in most optical frames as well as in the UVOT ones. Consequently, it gives a contribution to the AO 0235+164 photometry, which affects more the blue than the red part of the optical--UV spectrum and is stronger when the blazar is fainter.

To correct for this effect, rai05 estimated the UBVRI magnitudes of ELISA from images taken at medium-size telescopes participating in the 2003--2004 WEBT campaign. More recently, rai07a analysed an ELISA optical spectrum taken at the 8 m Very Large Telescope (VLT).

sources.summary: The paper identifies ELISA as a southern Seyfert/AGN in the AO 0235+164 field, extracts its spectrum, and treats its light as an important contaminant in faint-state photometry.

quotes.quote: The environment of AO 0235+164 is rather complex (see Fig. \ field ): the z=0.524 absorber is probably an elliptical galaxy lying 1 3 to the east Following the notation by nil96 , it was indicated as G1 in the figure, but it is not discernible from the source. , which is very likely interacting with a Seyfert galaxy 2 \ south of the source (G2). This southern AGN, named ELISA by rai05 , can noticeably affect the source photometry during faint states, especially in the blue part of the optical spectrum. The four galaxies G1--G4 belong to the same z=0.524 group sti93 , which might include other close objects with still unknown redshifts.
A small aperture was used in order to avoid the inclusion of ELISA. From the VLT frame we could also extract the ELISA spectrum.

gmw_00001915.name: And III
gmw_00001915.overview: And III is a dwarf spheroidal satellite galaxy of Andromeda [arXiv:1912.02186] that may form a physical pair with the nearby satellite Andromeda I [arXiv:1211.3161]. The galaxy ceased all star formation by 6 billion years ago [arXiv:1606.01207] and hosts a low-metallicity stellar population [arXiv:1710.09038] containing blue horizontal branch stars [arXiv:1606.01207].

sources.summary: The paper presents HST photometry for And III, using resolved stellar populations to compare its CMD coverage, horizontal-branch features, and early star-formation history with other M31 dwarf spheroidals.

quotes.quote: The positions of the observed fields are shown in Figure f4 . As can be seen in Figure f4 , for And I and And II the ACS field covers only part of the galaxy within the half light radius and the parallel WFC3 field contains many member stars. Some member stars were detected in the WFC3 field of And III, but not nearly enough to produce a SFH with reasonable uncertainties. For And XV, And XVI, and And XXVIII, the ACS field of view covers most of the galaxy out to the half light radius and the WFC3 field is distant enough that a minimal number of member stars are expected.

Figure f5 shows that the ACS photometry reaches below the oldest main-sequence turn-offs allowing for very strong constraints on the oldest epochs of star formation. These observations of And I, And II, and And III are 2.5 mag fainter than the deepest CMDs previously obtained for these galaxies dacosta1996, dacosta2000, dacosta2002 . There are several notable features that are common to all six ACS CMDs. All six galaxies show the presence of blue horizontal branch stars (BHBs). This is generally taken as evidence of the presence of stars with ages comparable to the Milky Way GC population see discussion in gallart2005 . To date, all galaxies that have been observed with sufficient photometric depth have shown at least some star formation at the earliest times.

Figure f7 shows a variety of SFHs. However, all six SFHs have the common properties of starting their star formation early (there is significant star formation in the earliest time bin for all six galaxies), having at least 50 of their stars formed by a lookback time of 9 Gyr, and then ceasing all star formation by a lookback time of 6 Gyr. And XV and And XXVIII show the oldest mean stellar populations of the sample, but both are clearly not consistent with a single age population and are clearly not consistent with forming all of their stars before the epoch of reionization (as indicated in Figure f7 by the shaded region).

sources.summary: The paper includes And III among five M31 dwarf spheroidals observed with Keck/DEIMOS, measuring member-star kinematics, dynamical masses, rotation limits, metallicities, and abundance trends.

quotes.quote: We obtained spectroscopy with Keck/DEIMOS fab03 of individual red giants in five dSphs: Andromeda VII, I, III, V, and X . They are numbered in the order in which they were discovered: And I and III van72 , And V arm98 , And VII kar99 , and And X zuc07 . However, in this paper, we list them in decreasing order of stellar mass whenever they appear in a list (e.g., in tables and in figures).

We present deep spectroscopy from Keck/DEIMOS of Andromeda I, III, V, VII, and X, all of which are dwarf spheroidal satellites of M31. The sample includes \ spectroscopic members across all five dSphs. We confirm previous measurements of the velocity dispersions and dynamical masses, and we provide upper limits on bulk rotation. Our measurements confirm that M31 satellites obey the same relation between stellar mass and stellar metallicity as Milky Way (MW) satellites and other dwarf galaxies in the Local Group. The metallicity distributions show similar trends with stellar mass as MW satellites, including evidence in massive satellites for external influence, like pre-enrichment or gas accretion.

sources.summary: The paper identifies And I and And III as a possible M31 satellite pair, unusually close by chance and separated by 33 kpc, 32 km/s, and 1.7 magnitudes.

quotes.quote: Besides the previously recognised pairings of the Magellanic Clouds and of NGC 147/NGC 185, other candidate pairs include the Ursa Minor and Draco dwarf spheroidals, as well as the And I/And III satellites of M31. These pairs are much closer than expected by chance if the radial and angular distributions of satellites were uncorrelated; in addition, they have very similar line-of-sight velocities and luminosities that differ by less than three magnitudes.
In this case, And IX is unlikely to be a member of a pair ( P=0.90 ), whereas And III is unusually close to And I ( P=0.10 ), hinting at a possible physical association.

The procedure also joins together two pairs of satellites around M31: NGC 147 and NGC185, as well as And I and And III. The latter are separated by 33 kpc; differ in velocity by just 32 km/s; and in magnitude by 1.7 .

sources.summary: The HST census treats And III as an M31 dSph, detects 111 RRLs, and places its old population among the lower-metallicity ISLAndS galaxies.

quotes.quote: Under the ISLAndS Initial Star formation and Lifetimes of Andromeda Satellites project (based on very deep, multi-epoch HST ACS and WFC3 data), six M31 dSph satellite companion galaxies were observed: And I, And II, And III, And XV, And XVI and And XXVIII.
We detect 870 RR Lyrae (RRL) stars in the fields of And I (296), II (251), III (111), XV (117), XVI (8), XXVIII (87).

Relatively low values were found for And III, And XV, And XVI, and And XXVIII, on average close to [ Fe/H ] -1.8 or lower. On the other hand, in the case of And I and And II, different authors Kalirai2010,Ho2012 agree on a much higher mean metallicity ( [ Fe/H ] -1.4), and a relatively large metallicity spread ( _ AndI =0.37 dex, _ AndII =0.72 dex). Nevertheless, the small number of HASP stars (see sec:rrl ) suggests that, even if the tail of the RRL metallicity distribution reaches such relatively high values, the bulk of the RRL stars must have a lower metallicity ( [ Fe/H ] < -1.5, Fiorentino2015a ). Therefore, as representative values of the metallicity for the RRL population, we adopted --in agreement with their SFHs Skillman2017 - [ Fe/H ] =--1.8 for And I and And II while, for the rest of the galaxies, we assume that the metallicity of the old population must to be quite similar to that obtained by the spectroscopic studies (see column 8).

sources.summary: The paper describes Andromeda III as an M31 dwarf spheroidal with metallicity and horizontal-branch evidence for age effects.
sources.paper_coordinate_text: Andromeda III dwarf spheroidal galaxy ( 0 h  35 m  33.8 s , +36^  29'  52'' )

quotes.quote: Andromeda III The Andromeda III dwarf spheroidal galaxy ( 0 h  35 m  33.8 s , +36^  29'  52'' ) was discovered by vandenbergh1972a (see also vandenbergh1972b ) along with Andromeda I and II, and the background dwarf irregular Andromeda IV. have used WFPC2 to obtain deep photometry as part of their HST survey of the stellar populations of M31's dwarf spheroidal satellites ( dacosta1996,dacosta2000 ). By comparing the RGB with those of standard globular clusters, And III is found to have a mean metallicity of Fe/H 1.88 and a dispersion of 0.12 (see also armandroff1993 ). It has a very obvious HB, which is in fact redder than that in both And I II.

sources.summary: And III is selected as an M31 dSph for dark-halo modeling; the paper maps its parameters and infers a steep inner density slope.

quotes.quote: For the M31 dSphs, we select five dSphs (And I, And II, And III, And V, and And VII) such that more than 50 stars which can be used as kinematic data, because for a dSph with less than 50 sample stars it is difficult to evaluate its two-dimensional velocity dispersion maps correctly as described below.

In this section, we describe the best-fit dark halo model for each of seven MW dSphs (Carina, Fornax, Sculptor, Sextans, Draco, Leo I, and Leo II) and five M31 dSphs (And I, And II, And III, And V, and And VII) and present confidence maps between each parameter.

In Figure  fig:fig2 and  fig:fig3 , we present 68  ( 1 ), 95  , and 99  confidence levels of contours in the two-dimensional plane of Q- _z , _0- _z , _z , b_ halo _z , and i- _z for all sample dSphs: Carina, Fornax, Sculptor, Sextans, Draco, and Leo I are shown in Figure  fig:fig2 , and Leo II, And I, And II, And III, And V, and And VII are shown in Figure  fig:fig3 .

gmw_00001916.name: And V
gmw_00001916.overview: Andromeda V is a bright dwarf spheroidal galaxy that is a satellite of M31 [arXiv:1912.02186, arXiv:1103.2121]. It resembles a typical dwarf spheroidal with little to no evidence of young or intermediate stellar populations [arXiv:astro-ph/0410489], and it is modeled as having a prolate dark matter halo [arXiv:1507.07620].

sources.summary: The paper includes And V among five M31 dwarf spheroidals observed with Keck/DEIMOS, measuring member-star kinematics, dynamical masses, rotation limits, metallicities, and abundance trends.

quotes.quote: We obtained spectroscopy with Keck/DEIMOS fab03 of individual red giants in five dSphs: Andromeda VII, I, III, V, and X . They are numbered in the order in which they were discovered: And I and III van72 , And V arm98 , And VII kar99 , and And X zuc07 . However, in this paper, we list them in decreasing order of stellar mass whenever they appear in a list (e.g., in tables and in figures).

We present deep spectroscopy from Keck/DEIMOS of Andromeda I, III, V, VII, and X, all of which are dwarf spheroidal satellites of M31. The sample includes \ spectroscopic members across all five dSphs. We confirm previous measurements of the velocity dispersions and dynamical masses, and we provide upper limits on bulk rotation. Our measurements confirm that M31 satellites obey the same relation between stellar mass and stellar metallicity as Milky Way (MW) satellites and other dwarf galaxies in the Local Group. The metallicity distributions show similar trends with stellar mass as MW satellites, including evidence in massive satellites for external influence, like pre-enrichment or gas accretion.

sources.summary: The paper reanalyzes Andromeda V LRIS data, identifying 17 probable member stars and deriving systemic velocity and velocity dispersion consistent with earlier work and T12.

quotes.quote: Andromeda V (And V) was observed using the LRIS instrument on Keck I rather than the DEIMOS instrument on Keck II. LRIS has a lower resolution than DEIMOS, and a smaller field of view, which lowers the accuracy of velocity measurement and limits us to only 50 targets within a mask compared with 100-200 for a DEIMOS mask. The raw data were also not reduced using our standard pipeline, owing to problems with the arc-lamp calibrations, and were instead analyzed using the NOAO.ONEDSPEC and NOAO.TWODSPEC packages in IRAF. The results from this reduction, plus an analysis of the data using hard cuts in velocity, distance and color to determine membership were first published in collins11b . Our full probabilistic analysis identifies 17 stars with a non-negligible probability of belonging to And V.

sources.summary: The paper presents And V as a bright M31 dwarf spheroidal, revises its Subaru structural parameters from star counts, and measures Keck kinematics showing a typical dispersion for its size.

quotes.quote: And V Structural properties with Subaru Suprime-Cam Andromeda V is a bright M31 satellite with M_V=-9.8 mcconnachie06b , and was first discovered with the Second Palomar Sky Survey armandroff98 .

Briefly, we construct an isopleth map of a 60' 60' field from the Subaru data centred on And V, then determine the centre of gravity, _0, _0 , position angle =32 2 (measured from East to North), and ellipticity, =0.17 0.02 , for each isophote by using the intensity-weighted moments and mcconnachie06a equations 2 and 3. We then construct a background corrected radial profile for And V, where a background level is estimated by measuring the average number of stars per arcmin within circular annuli located beyond the tidal radius of And V (as derived in mcconnachie06b ).

In terms of their kinematics, we measured systemic velocities of v_r=-393.1 4.2 and 344.8 2.5 , and dispersions of _v=11.5^ +5.3 _ 4.4 and _v=9.4^ +3.2 _ 2.4 for And V and And VI respectively, meaning that these two dSph appear very typical for objects of their size when compared with MW dSphs.

sources.summary: The paper discusses Andromeda V among recent M31 subgroup dwarf spheroidals, compares metallicity estimates, and measures its TRGB distance.

quotes.quote: Andromeda V, Andromeda VI (Pegasus dSph) and Andromeda VII (Cassiopeia dSph) Andromeda V ( 1 h  10 m  17.1 s , +47^  37'  41'' ), VI ( 23 h  51 m  46.3 s , +24^  34'  57'' ), VII ( 23 h  26 m  31 s , +50^  41'  31'' ) were, prior to the discovery of And IX, the three newest additions to the M31 subgroup. And V and VI were discovered by after they had performed an analysis on the disgitised version of the second Palomar Sky Survey (POSS-II; reid1991,reid1993,lasker1993 ). At the same time, independently discovered And V (which they named the Pegasus dSph) as well as yet another companion, the Cassiopeia dSph (also named And  VII) from their analysis of the POSS-II plates. All of these satellites appear to resemble typical dwarf spheroidals, with little or no evidence for young or intermediate stellar populations ( armandroff1998,armandroff1999,grebel1999a,hopp1999,davidge2002 ).

sources.summary: And V is one of the selected M31 dSphs; the paper treats it as having a prolate dark halo and reports its converted axial ratio for simulation comparison.

quotes.quote: For the M31 dSphs, we select five dSphs (And I, And II, And III, And V, and And VII) such that more than 50 stars which can be used as kinematic data, because for a dSph with less than 50 sample stars it is difficult to evaluate its two-dimensional velocity dispersion maps correctly as described below.

In this section, we describe the best-fit dark halo model for each of seven MW dSphs (Carina, Fornax, Sculptor, Sextans, Draco, Leo I, and Leo II) and five M31 dSphs (And I, And II, And III, And V, and And VII) and present confidence maps between each parameter.

As shown in Figure  fig:fig1 , for dSphs with a prolate dark halo like And I and And V, the line-of-sight velocity dispersion along the minor axis of these galaxies is relatively small compared with those along the other axes.

gmw_00001917.name: LPs-J0209
gmw_00001917.overview: LPs-J0209 is a strongly lensed, hyperluminous infrared starburst galaxy at a redshift of approximately 2.55 [arXiv:1807.03313, arXiv:2108.13016]. It hosts a compact, radio-loud active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1807.03313, arXiv:2010.16231] and exhibits intense star formation within a dense molecular gas reservoir [arXiv:1807.03313, arXiv:2209.09268].

sources.summary: 9io9 is a lensed z=2.553 hyperluminous infrared, radio-luminous galaxy with a radio-loud AGN and intense star formation; ALMA finds broad dense-gas emission possibly tracing an AGN-linked molecular outflow.

quotes.quote: Cross-matching with archival and existing data from Herschel and the Very Large Array (VLA), and through a series of follow-up observations, 9io9 was revealed to be a submm- and radio-bright galaxy at z = 2.553 , with the dust emission approaching an astonishing 1 Jy at the peak of the spectral energy distribution.

Even taking into account the 10 lensing magnification (Geach et al. \ 2015), 9io9 is a hyperluminous infrared ( L_ IR 10^ 13 L_ ) and radio luminous ( L_ 1.4GHz 10^ 25 W Hz ^ 1 ) galaxy. The galaxy's radio-to-infrared luminosity ratio, q_ IR = 1.8 , betrays a radio-loud AGN (Ivison et al. \ 2010), but with copious amounts of ongoing star formation -- of the order 10^3 M_ yr ^ 1 - contributing to L_ IR .

Regardless of this, it is clear that one, or both, of the lines must be very broad, and this might provide clues as to the nature of the very dense gas in 9io9 compared to the molecular ring. The model line width is _ dense =(289 48) km s ^ 1 , corresponding to a fwhm 680 km s ^ 1 , nearly a factor of two larger than the deprojected maximum rotation speed of the molecular ring. Note that fixing the velocity width of the and components to the _ disk =73 km s ^ 1 dispersion of the molecular ring does not result in a sensible fit to the data. The compact nature of the emission compared to and , coupled with its large velocity width compared to the deprojected rotation speed of the ring, suggests that the gas traced by does not trace the bulk of the gas reservoir and could be dynamically decoupled from the ring. One interpretation of these observations is that the gas traced by is outflowing, potentially indicating interaction between the AGN and the inner part of the molecular ring or smaller scale circumnuclear disk.

sources.summary: The paper presents 9io9 as a strongly lensed z=2.6 hyperluminous starburst whose dense, kiloparsec-scale ionized and molecular ISM makes it an intense early-Universe star-formation laboratory.

quotes.quote: 9io9 is a galaxy discovered as part of the citizen science project Spacewarps see marshall_space_2016, more_space_2016 and independently as a bright millimetre source by Planck harrington_early_2016 , Herschel viero_herschel_2014 , and the Atacama Cosmology Telescope su_redshift_2017 . 9io9 lies at z=2.6 , close to the peak epoch of galaxy growth geach_magnified_2018 . Even after taking into account the 15 magnification factor, 9io9 appears to have a total infrared luminosity, L_ IR , exceeding 10^ 13 L _ , putting in the hyperluminous class, with an inferred SFR of around 2000 M _ yr ^ 1 , modulo the sometimes high and often hidden AGN contribution to L_ IR in such systems e.g. ivison_2019 , and also modulo evidence suggesting that the stellar initial mass function in high-density star-forming regions is markedly top heavy e.g. cf. \ romano_2019 romano_evolution_2017, zhang_stellar_2018, motte_2018, schneider_2018, brown_extreme_2019 . It is therefore an excellent laboratory for studying the conditions of intense star formation in the early Universe.

The measured electron density of n_ e = 290^ +90 _ 70 cm ^ 3 is significantly higher than that expected for the WIM, which is comprised of material at densities 3--4 orders of magnitude lower gaensler_vertical_2008,weisberg_arecibo_2008 . The electron density in 9io9 is high, but not extreme when we consider individual local star-forming environments. For example, the Galactic H ii region, G333.6 0.2, has an electron density, 300 cm ^ 3 , also determined via the \ ratio colgan_detection_1993 . This is consistent with the conditions in 9io9, with the key difference that we are measuring the characteristic electron density on scales of several kiloparsecs, rather than for an individual star-forming complex. Thus, a possible scenario is that star formation in 9io9 is proceeding in environments that resemble `normal' star-forming regions like G333.6 0.2 but with the key distinction that, while G333.6 0.2 represents a tiny fraction of the total Milky Way ISM by mass and volume, in 9io9 the majority of the ISM may be in this state.

sources.summary: The paper describes HERS1 as a z=2.553 gravitationally lensed dusty star-forming galaxy with a partial Einstein ring, high stellar mass, strong star formation, molecular gas, and imminent quenching.

quotes.quote: We present a detailed study of an extremely luminous SMG at z =2.553 , gravitationally lensed by two foreground galaxies at z=0.202 . The lensed galaxy, dubbed HERS1, features a partial Einstein ring with a radius of 3 ^ observed in high-resolution maps of HST/WFC3, SMA, and ALMA. Based on the reconstructed lens model, we find magnification factors _ star = 13.6 0.4 and _ dust = 12.8 0.3 for stellar and dust emissions of HERS1, respectively. We perform SED fitting on multiband photometry of HERS1, corrected for magnification, to measure its physical properties including stellar mass M_*=4.3 10^ 11 \ M_ , star formation rate SFR= 1023 \ M_ yr ^ 1 , and dust temperature T_ d =35 K. We analyze the physical conditions of the molecular gas through CO SLED modeling and find that its low-excitation component has a gas density n_ H_2 =10^ 3.8 cm ^ 3 , and kinetic temperature T_ k =18 K, while the high-excitation component has n_ H_2 =10^ 3.1 cm ^ 3 , and T_ k =479 K.

We also find that HERS1 shows higher excitation compared to an average SMG. We further derive the total molecular gas of HERS1 using three distinct tracers, including CO(1-0), CI lines, and SMA 870 \ m. We measure a gas fraction f_ gas =0.19 with a depletion time t_ dep 100 Myr.

sources.summary: The paper identifies J0209 as a Planck-selected, strongly lensed DSFG first found by SpaceWarps; JVLA resolves its K-band morphology resembling the FIR continuum.

quotes.quote: The three sources selected from the Planck imaging are J0209, J1202 and J1609. J0209 was first identified in the Subaru VISTA-CFHT Stripe 82 survey by the SpaceWarps citizen-science project 9io9 Geach2015 . The remaining two sources are drawn from the Planck 's Dusty GEMs - Gravitationally Enhanced sub-Millimetre Sources ( Canameras2015 , where J1202 is listed as PLCK G138.6+62.0 and J1609 as PLCK G092.5+42.9). All Planck DSFGs have very high apparent (lensed) FIR luminosity ( L_ FIR 10^ 14 L_ ). Although the observations towards these sources are relatively shallow (1-2 hours per source), they still achieve high sensitivity in HCN/FIR and HCN/CO ratios.
J0209 and J1609 are well resolved by the 3 arcsec beam; their K-band morphology resembles the FIR continuum (see Canameras2015 ).

sources.summary: The paper describes LPs-J0209 as a lensed starburst with a compact molecular gas reservoir, high inferred conversion factor, and known compact radio AGN activity.

quotes.quote: We can further test the reliability of our Turbulence model if we consider two of the \ with estimates of the intrinsic, lens model reconstruction of the source size, based on high-angular resolution data: LPs-J105353 canameras2017b and LPs-J0209 geach2018, rivera2019 . As summarized in harrington2019 for LPs-J0209 Also referred to as the Red Radio Ring. , the lens model reconstruction from the low-J CO line image presented in both geach2018, rivera2019 suggests a molecular gas reservoir with an emitting radius of 1 - 2 kpc. The flux-weighted mean magnification factor derived for this source is 15 , corresponding to an expected intrinsic emitting radius for LPs-J0209 of R_ eff / _ L = 16.1/ 14.7 4 kpc.

We note that the system with one of the highest values is the known radio-AGN/starburst, LPs-J0209, and the highest redshift source in our sample, LPs-J105322 ( z 3.5 ) both have well sampled dust photometry and CO lines out to J _ up = 11 and both [ CI ] lines, yet we still derive the highest values of _ CO(1-0) 10 , albeit with larger uncertainty.

Since the LPs-J0209 source is known to have a compact radio AGN geach2015 , future studies may explore the reasons whether or not star-forming galaxies with coeval AGN activity harrington2019 may tend to show higher values of _ CO .

sources.summary: PJ020941.3 is presented as a lensed dusty starburst at z=2.5536, detected strongly by AzTEC and CO, with independent identification as the Einstein-ring source 9io9.

quotes.quote:
PJ020941.3 is a 
egal source found in the Stripe 82 Survey field and is associated with the z=0.202 galaxy SDSS J020941.27+001558.5. Our photometric redshift analysis predicts a lensed, dusty starburst galaxy at z 2.5 with the dust peak near 350  with S_ 350 = 897 89 mJy. We detect 1100  continuum emission easily using AzTEC, measuring S_ 1100 = 147 20 mJy, centered on the 
egal source position (see Fig.  fig:aztec ). Using the RSR we detected CO (3-2) line at 97.31 GHz, yielding a redshift of z = 2.5536 0.0002 (a single line redshift resolved using the photometric redshift support as shown in Fig.  fig:photoz ).

sources.summary: The paper reports ALMA observations of 9io9 at z=2.5543, detecting CO and NH3 emission; the NH3 is interpreted as tracing dense molecular gas at star-forming sites.

quotes.quote: 9io9 (J2000, 02^ h 09^ m 41 3 , 00 15 58 5 , z=2.5543 ) was observed with the ALMA 12 m array during project 2019.1.01365.S.

CO J = 5 4 at _ obs =162.133 GHz is detected at high significance as expected, and exhibits the characteristic double horned profile as other molecular and atomic lines geach_red_2015,geach_magnified_2018,harrington_red_2019,doherty_n_2020 , and well-modelled in the source-plane reconstruction by a rotating disc geach_magnified_2018 . We also detect a fainter, but significant, emission feature at _ obs =161.072 GHz that is consistent with the redshifted ground state ortho-NH _3 J_K=1_0 0_0 rotational line at _ rest =572.498 GHz hereafter we refer to the line as NH _3 cazzoli_hyperfine_2009 .

While radiative pumping would further serve to lower the effective density of the gas responsible for NH _3 emission, we can be reasonably confident that the observed NH _3 is tracing some of the densest molecular gas in 9io9, and therefore the actual sites of star formation.

gmw_00001918.name: LPs-J105353
gmw_00001918.overview: LPs-J105353 is a compact, bright starburst galaxy at high redshift [arXiv:1506.01962, arXiv:2010.16231]. Its emission is strongly boosted by gravitational lensing [arXiv:1506.01962], and its reconstructed structure reveals two independent emitting regions separated by 500 parsecs [arXiv:2010.16231].

sources.summary: The paper identifies PLCK G244.8 + 54.9 as a compact, very bright lensed high-redshift galaxy, with minor possible AGN contribution and an isolated radio counterpart.

quotes.quote: These figures clearly show that our sources are either single, compact objects at 2 \ resolution (as is the case for, e.g., PLCK G244.8 + 54.9 and PLCK G138.6 + 62.0), or giant arcs (e.g., PLCK G145.2 + 50.9 and PLCK G080.2 + 49.8). PLCK G244.8 + 54.9 and PLCK G138.6 + 62.0 have flux densities of 1054 10 and 664 8 mJy, respectively, making it implausible that these are individual high-redshift galaxies if not benefitting from a strong boost from gravitational lensing through a massive foreground source.
